glossary.def: The conventional standard for full-time employment in the United States, consisting of 40 hours of work per week. In reality, only 4 in 10 U.S. workers actually work this schedule. The average work week for many Americans is approximately 47 hours, and 39% work 50 or more hours per week.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A blood test used to diagnose and monitor diabetes by measuring the average blood glucose levels over the past 3 months. A normal A1C is under 5.7; 5.7–6.4 indicates prediabetes; 6.5 or higher indicates diabetes. An A1C of 5.0 corresponds to approximately 97 mg/dL, and a 6.0 corresponds to approximately 126 mg/dL.

The habitual pattern of absence from work or other duty. Middle-aged workers with higher job satisfaction tend to exhibit lower rates of absenteeism compared to younger workers, contributing to their greater productivity and reliability in the workplace.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The traditional societal basis on which men are evaluated, including their accomplishments, skill, and power. Because men are valued for these attributes rather than reproductive capacity, they are not considered socially 'old' until they are physically incapable of working, creating a stark contrast with how aging women are perceived.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A common term for heartburn or pyrosis, referring to the discomfort caused when stomach acid backs up into the esophagus. It is often used interchangeably with heartburn.

A lifestyle that includes physical activity equivalent to walking more than 3 miles per day at 3 to 4 miles per hour, in addition to the activities of independent living. Individuals with active lifestyles have the highest caloric needs and gain the most extensive health benefits from their activity levels.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Short-term pain that is typically associated with tissue damage or illness. Individuals who have suffered acute pain in the past week experience approximately 14 minutes of sleep debt per night.

Sugars and syrups added to foods or beverages during processing or preparation, as distinguished from naturally occurring sugars. Dietary guidelines recommend that adults consume less than 10% of daily calories from added sugars. Currently, about 15% of calories in the American adult diet come from added sugars, equivalent to approximately 22 teaspoons per day. Excess added sugar contributes to weight gain, diabetes, and other health problems.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: ADHD, Specific Learning Disorders, and ASD
glossary.def: ADHD (Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder): great difficulty concentrating for more than a few moments; also inattentive, impulsive, and overactive. Rates in the U.S. are higher than most other nations; concerns about misdiagnosis and overmedication. SPECIFIC LEARNING DISORDER: marked deficit in a particular area not caused by intellectual disability or unusual stress. DYSLEXIA: unusual difficulty with reading — a processing problem, not an intelligence problem. DYSCALCULIA: unusual difficulty with math. AUTISM SPECTRUM DISORDER (ASD): difficulty with social communication and interaction; restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior. Social interaction is ALWAYS impaired. NEURODIVERSITY perspective: human brains make people diverse in abilities and needs.

glossary.term: Adolescent Brain: Lopsided Growth and Body Rhythms
glossary.def: The limbic system (fear, emotional impulses) matures BEFORE the prefrontal cortex (planning, emotional regulation). This lopsided growth makes teenagers vulnerable to social pressures. NUCLEUS ACCUMBENS: region of the ventral striatum connected to the limbic system; heightened arousal in this reward center during adolescence. POSITIVE aspects of adolescent brain development: increased myelination decreases reaction time; enhanced dopamine activity promotes pleasurable experiences; synaptic growth enhances moral development. CIRCADIAN SHIFT: puberty causes a shift toward 'eveningness' — later sleep-wake cycles. Three of every four high school seniors are sleep deprived.

glossary.term: Adolescent Egocentrism: Imaginary Audience and Personal Fable
glossary.def: ADOLESCENT EGOCENTRISM (ages 10–13): characteristic of adolescent thinking that leads young people to focus on themselves to the exclusion of others. IMAGINARY AUDIENCE: thinking intensely about themselves and what other people think of them; interpreting everyone else's behavior as a personal judgment; feeling more unique, special, admired, or disliked than they actually are. PERSONAL FABLE: belief that one's thoughts, feelings, or experiences are unique, more wonderful or awful than anyone else's. INVINCIBILITY FABLE: egocentric conviction that one cannot be overcome or harmed by anything that might defeat a normal mortal (leads to unprotected sex, drug use, reckless driving).

A parenting orientation identified by Greene et al. (2003) in which a divorced parent expects that children can adapt and should accommodate parental wishes regarding repartnering. Anderson and Greene (2011) found that adult-focused custodial mothers tended to be older, more educated, employed, had been married longer, reported less rapport with their children, and spent less time in joint activities with their children.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Exercise that uses large muscle groups in a rhythmic, continuous fashion and relies on oxygen for energy production. Includes walking, jogging, cycling, and swimming. Per guidelines, sessions should last at least 10 minutes and be spread throughout the week. Aerobic activity benefits cardiovascular health, weight management, and stress reduction.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The age demographic that spends less time on leisure activities than any other age group aged 15 or older in the United States (U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, 2016). This is attributed to the likelihood that individuals in this group are actively parenting and advancing in their careers simultaneously.

The practice of treating an applicant or employee less favorably because of their age. In the context of midlife employment, employers may prefer younger workers who start at lower pay scales and have more years of work ahead of them, making it harder for middle-aged unemployed adults to find new jobs.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Prejudice or discrimination against individuals on the basis of their age. In the context of women in midlife, ageism is closely linked to the double standard of aging, where women face harsher social judgments about growing older compared to men.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The relationship between advancing age and specialized knowledge, in which accumulated experience can confer advantages in familiar domains; however, expertise cannot fully compensate for age-related declines in general cognitive functioning, and the benefits of additional practice diminish with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the Big Five personality traits characterized by cooperativeness, trustworthiness, empathy, and prosocial behavior. Generative men and women were found to score high on agreeableness, consistent with the caring orientation that defines the generativity stage.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A subcategory of single adults who have never been married, as distinguished from those who are single due to divorce or widowhood. Research by DePaulo (2014) suggests that always-single individuals show little difference in emotional well-being or health compared to married individuals, particularly when compared to those married four or more years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by memory loss, cognitive decline, and behavioral changes. Research by Chang et al. (2016) found that higher BMI in midlife is associated with earlier onset of Alzheimer's disease, with each one-point increase in BMI at age 50 associated with developing the disease six to seven months earlier. Scientists speculate that fat cells may produce inflammatory chemicals that damage brain vasculature.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder and the most common form of dementia, characterized by memory loss, cognitive decline, and behavioral changes. Chang et al. (2016) found that midlife adiposity predicts earlier onset, neuropathology, and presymptomatic cerebral amyloid accumulation.

A professional medical organization that establishes clinical guidelines and standards for sleep medicine. The AASM recommends that adults receive at least 7 hours of sleep per night to avoid the health risks associated with chronic sleep deprivation.

An almond-shaped structure in the brain's limbic system responsible for processing emotions, particularly fear and negative stimuli. In middle and older adulthood, the amygdala demonstrates reduced reactivity to negative stimuli compared to younger adults.

Hormones responsible for building and maintaining muscle mass and other tissues, including testosterone and growth hormone. Their decline with aging is a key contributing factor to sarcopenia and the associated loss of muscle mass and strength.

Researchers who studied post-divorce dating behavior and parental gatekeeping. They found that most divorced adults had dated by one year after filing for divorce, over half were in a serious relationship by one year post-filing, and identified three gatekeeping approaches used by post-divorce parents when introducing new romantic partners to their children.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Also known as late-onset hypogonadism, this condition refers to a significant decline in testosterone levels in aging men, particularly after age 50. Symptoms include low sex drive, erectile dysfunction, fatigue, loss of muscle mass, loss of body hair, and breast enlargement. It is analogous to menopause but less universal and more gradual.

Chest pain or discomfort caused by reduced blood flow to the heart muscle, often a symptom of cardiovascular disease. It typically occurs when the heart is working harder than usual (e.g., during exercise or stress) and is an indication that the coronary arteries may be narrowed or partially blocked.

A commercial sector that markets products, procedures, and cosmetic surgeries aimed at reducing or reversing visible signs of aging. The media and anti-aging industry significantly influence how women perceive their own aging by promoting standards of youthful beauty and selling products to help women 'look younger,' as noted by Gilleard & Higgs (2000).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Quick assessment of newborn health, completed at 1 minute AND 5 minutes after birth. Rates five categories: heart rate, breathing, muscle tone, color, and reflexes. Each scored 0, 1, or 2; maximum is 10. Score of 7 or above indicates a healthy transition to extrauterine life; below 7 means the newborn may need assistance. Developed by Virginia Apgar in 1952.

An early researcher who found that boomerang children and adult children who failed to exit the home placed greater financial hardship on parents, and that parents reported more negative perceptions of this living arrangement. This early research contrasts with more recent findings showing greater parental tolerance for adult children returning home.

glossary.term: Arenas of Identity: Religious, Political, Ethnic, Gender
glossary.def: RELIGIOUS identity: most adolescents accept broad outlines of parental and cultural religious identity; specific beliefs may be questioned. POLITICAL identity: more 21st-century adolescents identify as independent; they tend to be more liberal than parents. ETHNIC identity: establishing a solid ethnic identity is especially difficult for multiracial adolescents or immigrants; pride in ethnic identity correlates with academic achievement and well-being. GENDER IDENTITY: DSM-5 replaced 'gender identity disorder' with GENDER DYSPHORIA — people distressed at their biological gender. This shift from 'disorder' to 'dysphoria' means the problem is in the distress, which can be mitigated by social conditions.

An abnormal heart rhythm in which the heartbeat may be too fast, too slow, or irregular. Arrhythmias are a common cause of sudden cardiac arrest. In a healthy heart, a fatal arrhythmia is unlikely to develop without an outside trigger.

ASSIMILATION: new experiences are interpreted to fit into existing schemas (old ideas). ACCOMMODATION: old ideas are restructured to include new experiences that do not fit. Process: Equilibrium → New experience → Disequilibrium → Adaptation (assimilation or accommodation) → New Equilibrium. Disequilibrium leads to cognitive growth.

ASTHMA: chronic disease of the respiratory system in which inflammation narrows the airways, causing wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and coughing. HYGIENE HYPOTHESIS: the idea that early exposure to microbes and allergens helps develop natural resistance. Two pieces of evidence: (1) first-born children are more likely to develop asthma than later-born children; (2) farm children have much lower rates of asthma and allergies. Suggests that reduced outdoor exposure and cleaner environments may be increasing asthma incidence.

The buildup of fatty plaque in the arteries, considered the most common cause of cardiovascular disease. The plaque thickens the artery walls and restricts blood flow to organs and tissues. Atherosclerosis is also a major contributing factor in heart attacks, as the plaque can form the basis for blood clots that block coronary arteries.

A cardiovascular condition involving the buildup of plaque—composed of fat, cholesterol, calcium, and other substances—in the arterial walls. Over time the plaque hardens and narrows the arteries, restricting blood flow and oxygenation to organs including the penis, making it a leading medical cause of erectile dysfunction.

ATTACHMENT: a lasting emotional bond that one person has with another, beginning in early infancy and influencing close relationships throughout life. Demonstrated through proximity-seeking (approaching and following) and contact-maintaining (touching, snuggling). Measured using the Strange Situation. Four types: SECURE (Type B) — explores freely, misses caregiver, joyfully welcomes return; INSECURE-AVOIDANT (Type A) — indifferent to caregiver; INSECURE-RESISTANT (Type C) — clingy and hard to comfort; DISORGANIZED (Type D) — confused, contradictory responses.

A feature of expert performance in which complex thoughts and actions become more routine and instinctive over time. Expertise allows individuals to process information faster and more effectively, making responses appear instinctive (Crawford & Channon, 2002).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A dimension of psychological well-being referring to an individual's sense of self-determination and independence. Formal religious participation was associated with lower levels of autonomy, while spirituality was associated with higher levels of autonomy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A generational cohort in the United States consisting of individuals born between 1946 and 1964, following the end of World War II. This cohort is characterized by a significant spike in birth rates during that period. The aging of the Baby Boom cohort into middle and late adulthood has led to increased research interest and funding directed toward understanding the developmental stage of middle adulthood.

The generation of approximately 78 million Americans born between approximately 1946 and 1964. Promoting exercise and healthy habits among baby boomers is considered one of the most effective strategies for reducing health care costs and improving quality of life, as cited by Shure and Cahan (1998).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The large generational cohort born roughly between 1946 and 1964. In 2002, baby boomers were aged 38–56, representing the prime employment group. By 2012, the youngest were 48 and the oldest had just reached retirement age (66). Their aging is a major factor in the declining overall workforce participation rate.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A long-term scientific study used in the Chang et al. (2016) research that tracked 1,394 men and women with an average age of approximately 60 for 14 years. The study provided data linking higher BMI in midlife to earlier onset of Alzheimer's disease and greater brain changes typical of the disease.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: AUTHORITARIAN: high behavioral standards, strict punishment, little communication. Children tend to become conscientious and obedient but not especially happy; may feel guilty or depressed. PERMISSIVE: high nurturance and communication but little discipline or control. Children are unhappy, lack self-control, are immature, tend to remain dependent. AUTHORITATIVE: parents set limits and enforce rules but are flexible and listen to their children. Children are successful, articulate, happy, generous, well-liked. NEGLECTFUL/UNINVOLVED: parents are indifferent and unaware of what is going on in children's lives — distinct from permissive. Cultural and cohort context always matters.

The degree to which an individual's bedtime fluctuates from night to night. High bedtime variability exposes the body to varying levels of light, disrupting the circadian clock and impairing glucose metabolism and energy homeostasis.

A researcher who challenged the assumption that single individuals fare worse emotionally and in health than married individuals. She argued that most studies create a bias by comparing 'married' versus 'not married' without separating always-single individuals from the divorced or widowed, and found little meaningful difference between always-single and married individuals once the honeymoon phase ends.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A perspective proposed by Vern Bengtson (2001) suggesting that grandparents do not maintain a single fixed grandparenting style across all relationships. Instead, they may adopt different styles with different grandchildren and change their approach over time as family circumstances evolve, such as during crises or when stepping in as sole care providers.

A non-cancerous growth of tissue that does not invade nearby tissues and, when removed, usually does not grow back. Benign tumors can sometimes be quite large and are generally not life threatening—with the exception of benign brain tumors, which can be life threatening due to limited space within the skull.

A widely used model of personality consisting of five dimensions: Openness to Experience, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism (OCEAN). Research by de St. Aubin & McAdams (1995) and Peterson, Smirles & Wentworth (1997) found that highly generative individuals scored high on conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, and openness to experience, and low on neuroticism.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The use of both hemispheres of the brain to perform cognitive tasks. Older adults who perform best on cognitive tasks are more likely to demonstrate bilateralization compared to those who perform worst, suggesting it is an adaptive compensatory strategy.

A digestive fluid produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder, used to digest fats. Bile pigments are one of the components that can crystallize to form gallstones; increased cholesterol in bile (e.g., due to obesity) raises the risk of gallstone formation.

A tubular structure that carries bile from the gallbladder to the small intestine. Gallstones that become lodged in the bile duct can block the flow of bile or pancreatic juices, causing pain, infection, and potentially life-threatening complications requiring surgical or medical intervention.

Colored compounds derived from the breakdown of red blood cells (primarily bilirubin) that are secreted into bile. Bile pigments are one of the three main components—along with fatty materials and calcium deposits—that can crystallize and contribute to gallstone formation.

A stress-reduction technique in which an individual is provided with real-time information about normally unconscious bodily processes (e.g., heart rate) and then taught strategies to consciously alter these signals. Has shown promise in reducing heart disease, hypertension, and other serious conditions (Moravec, 2008; Patel, Marmot, & Terry, 1981).

The progressive, time-dependent decline in the physiological functioning of the body at the molecular, cellular, tissue, and organ levels. Biological aging underlies primary aging and includes processes such as telomere shortening, DNA damage accumulation, reduced cellular repair mechanisms, and oxidative stress.

In 2012, Black women had the highest labor force participation rate among women at 58%, compared to approximately 57% for Hispanic and Asian women, and 55% for White women. Despite leading female participation rates, rates for all groups were projected to decline by 2022.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The force exerted by circulating blood on the walls of blood vessels. High blood pressure (hypertension) is associated with high sodium intake, obesity, and excessive fat consumption. Reducing sodium intake, especially to 1,500 mg per day for those already hypertensive, is associated with corresponding decreases in blood pressure.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: BMI (Body Mass Index): ratio of weight to height. CHILDHOOD OVERWEIGHT: BMI above the 85th percentile for a child's age. CHILDHOOD OBESITY: BMI above the 95th percentile for a child's age. In 2016, 18% of 6-to-11-year-olds in the United States were obese. Obesity now causes more deaths worldwide than malnutrition. Childhood overweight correlates with asthma, high blood pressure, elevated cholesterol, and Type II diabetes. Obesity can have a genetic component but environmental factors (family diet, lifestyle) are major influences.

A numerical measure derived from an individual's height and weight, used to categorize underweight, normal weight, overweight, and obesity. BMI was measured alongside insulin resistance in the SWAN Sleep Study to assess the metabolic impact of irregular sleep schedules.

A numerical measure derived from a person's weight and height, used to categorize individuals as underweight, normal weight, overweight, or obese. In the Chang et al. (2016) study, each one-point increase in BMI at age 50 was associated with developing Alzheimer's disease approximately six to seven months earlier.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The amount of mineral matter per unit volume of bone, used as an indicator of bone strength and fracture risk. Exercise, particularly weight-bearing and resistance activities, is known to increase bone density. Maintaining adequate bone density is especially important in midlife to prevent osteoporosis and fractures in later adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Young adults who return to live in their parents' home after having previously lived independently. Motivations for returning include financial hardship, emotional distress, and mental health issues. Research indicates that those who return are more likely to have had good relationships with their parents previously, and today's parents are more tolerant of this arrangement than in past decades due to its increasing normalization.

A researcher who conducted a comprehensive review of the research literature on the empty nest and post-parental period, finding that few parents reported significant loneliness or loss after children left home, and that marital satisfaction commonly increased during the launching phase and persisted over time, supporting the role strain relief hypothesis over the empty nest syndrome concept.

The cognitive and neurological activity of the brain during the middle adult years, approximately ages 40-65. Research indicates that brain functioning in middle adulthood reflects both gains and losses — while processing speed and certain memory functions may begin to decline, expertise, vocabulary, and emotional regulation often remain stable or improve. Neuroplasticity continues throughout this period.

The brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life. In middle age, the brain continues to rewire itself based on experiences, demonstrating that cognitive development does not cease after young adulthood.

BRAIN STEM: controls automatic responses like breathing — present at birth. MIDBRAIN: affects emotions and memory. CORTEX: outer layers where most thinking, feeling, and sensing occurs — continues developing through adolescence into young adulthood. PREFRONTAL CORTEX: at the very front; specializes in anticipation, planning, and impulse control — LAST area to mature. LIMBIC SYSTEM: produces emotions — amygdala, hypothalamus, hippocampus.

Researchers who documented that women experience more fear, anxiety, and concern about their identity as they age compared to men, and may feel pressure to prove themselves as productive and valuable members of society, particularly during and after midlife.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who coined the term 'graying of divorce,' noting that while the overall U.S. divorce rate has declined since the 1990s, the divorce rate among adults 50 and older has doubled. They also found the rate of remarriage dropped more for younger adults than for middle-aged and older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The theory proposed by Cohen and Wills (1985) that social support moderates (buffers) the negative effects of stress on psychological and physical health, particularly during high-stress conditions.

BULLYING: repeated, systematic efforts to inflict harm through physical, verbal, or social attack on a weaker person. BULLY-VICTIM: someone who both attacks others and is attacked (also called a provocative victim). FOUR TYPES: Physical (hitting, pinching, kicking), Verbal (teasing, taunting, name-calling), Relational (destroying peer acceptance and friendship — often more devastating than physical bullying), Cyber (using electronic means to harm another). CAUSES: genetic predisposition, parenting/caregiving environment, peers. CONSEQUENCES: depression, impaired social understanding, lower school achievement. PREVENTION: the WHOLE SCHOOL must be involved; intervention is more effective in earlier grades.

The researcher credited with establishing the distinction between primary aging and secondary aging. Ewald Busse's classification framework helped differentiate between inevitable biological aging processes and those caused by modifiable lifestyle and environmental factors, providing a foundation for health promotion in middle and late adulthood.

A stimulant found in coffee, tea, and certain beverages that is a known trigger for heartburn. Caffeine can relax the lower esophageal sphincter, making it easier for stomach acid to reflux into the esophagus.

Accumulations of calcium salts that can combine with bile pigments and fatty materials in the gallbladder to form gallstones. Calcium-rich gallstones (pigment stones) are one type of gallstone composition.

The number of calories required per day to maintain body weight, which varies by age, sex, and physical activity level. Aging brings about a reduction in the number of calories required. For example, a sedentary 36-40 year old male requires approximately 2,400 calories per day, while a sedentary 36-40 year old female requires approximately 1,800 calories per day, with needs decreasing further with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A collection of more than 100 related diseases characterized by uncontrolled cell division and the spread of abnormal cells into surrounding tissues. Cancer cells do not mature into specific cell types, ignore signals to stop dividing, evade the immune system, prompt nearby cells to form blood vessels that supply tumors, and can metastasize. Cancer was the second leading cause of death in the United States in 2013, accounting for 22.5% of all deaths.

A waste gas normally expelled by the lungs during exhalation. With age-related declines in lung capacity caused by rib cage changes and diaphragm weakening, carbon dioxide levels in the blood increase while oxygen levels decrease, contributing to shortness of breath and fatigue.

A subset of heart disease involving narrowed, blocked, or stiffened blood vessels that prevent adequate blood flow to the body and heart. The most common cause is atherosclerosis. Cardiovascular disease can lead to heart attack, angina (chest pain), or stroke. Risk factors include high blood pressure, high cholesterol, diabetes, obesity, smoking, poor diet, advanced age, male sex, family history, stress, and poor hygiene.

The virtue associated with the successful resolution of Erikson's seventh stage of generativity vs. stagnation. Erikson defined it as the obligation to 'take care of the persons, the products, and the ideas one has learned to care for' (Erikson, 1982, p. 67). It represents the psychosocial strength gained from mastering this stage.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The point at which individuals often reach the highest level of professional achievement, which frequently occurs during middle adulthood due to the accumulation of expertise, experience, and domain-specific knowledge over many years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The physical, emotional, financial, and social strain experienced by individuals providing care for a disabled or ill family member. Beach et al. (2000) found both negative and positive health effects of caring for a disabled spouse.

Elevated levels of emotional and physical stress experienced by a caregiver that result in negative health outcomes. Research by Beach et al. (2000) found that caregivers who experienced strain had the poorest perceived health, increased health-risk behaviors, and elevated anxiety and depression. In contrast, those who provided spousal care without strain had mental health similar to non-caregivers.

The physical and psychological burden experienced by individuals who provide unpaid care to a disabled child, spouse, parent, or other family member. Caregiving is associated with poorer global health, increased physical symptoms, and higher rates of chronic conditions. The typical caregiver in the U.S. is a 49-year-old female caring for a 69-year-old female with a long-term physical condition.

A researcher who articulated the contrast between how men and women are socially valued as they age: men for achievements, competence, and power (considered old only when unable to work), versus women for reproductive capabilities (considered old once postmenopausal), contributing to the double standard of aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A surgical procedure to remove the eye's clouded natural lens and typically replace it with an artificial intraocular lens. It is one of the most common surgical procedures performed on older adults. Individuals who have undergone cataract surgery are at increased risk for developing floaters.

A research center that conducted the National Survey of Sexual Health and Behavior (NSSHB) in 2010, providing data on sexual behaviors across various age groups in the United States. Their findings highlighted the continued sexual activity of middle-aged adults and the low rates of condom use among adults over 40.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
glossary.def: A U.S. federal public health agency responsible for tracking disease incidence and promoting public health. The CDC reported in 2014 that adults aged 50 and older accounted for 16.7% of new HIV diagnoses, highlighting growing STI risks in older populations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Two patterns of prenatal and postnatal growth. CEPHALOCAUDAL: development proceeds from head downward (e.g., infants gain head and hand control before walking). PROXIMODISTAL: development proceeds from the center of the body outward (e.g., trunk before extremities). Both patterns continue after birth.

The buildup of amyloid plaques in the brain, a hallmark pathological feature of Alzheimer's disease. Research by Chang et al. (2016) linked midlife obesity to earlier presymptomatic accumulation of these plaques.

Researchers who reviewed literature on aging and expertise, concluding that the superior performance of older experts compared to younger novices is task specific, and that the rate of return for practice effort diminishes as individuals age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who described and categorized three principal styles of grandparenting — Remote, Companionate, and Involved — based on the degree of contact and level of authority grandparents exercised in their grandchildren's lives. Their typology remains a foundational framework in the study of grandparent-grandchild relationships.

glossary.term: Child Maltreatment: Abuse, Neglect, and Prevention
glossary.def: CHILD MALTREATMENT: intentional harm to or avoidable endangerment of anyone under 18. CHILD ABUSE: deliberate action harmful to a child's physical, emotional, or sexual well-being. CHILD NEGLECT: failure to meet a child's basic physical, educational, or emotional needs. REPORTED maltreatment: someone has notified authorities. SUBSTANTIATED maltreatment: reported, investigated, and verified. THREE PREVENTION LEVELS: Primary (macrosystem/exosystem — stable neighborhoods, family cohesion), Secondary (identifying insecure attachment), Tertiary (limiting harm after maltreatment — permanency planning, foster care, kinship care, adoption). PTSD may develop as a delayed reaction to shocking or frightening events.

A parenting orientation identified by Greene et al. (2003) in which a divorced parent allows the child's views, reactions, and needs to significantly influence the repartnering process and selection of a new partner.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Older adults in China, particularly in rural areas, who experience greater loneliness and depression after their children leave home compared to American samples. This more negative response is attributed to the cultural tradition of family support for the elderly, loss of income when children move to cities for education and work, and the practical hardships associated with children's departure. Urban Chinese empty-nesters did not report the same degree of distress.

One of the most common bacterial sexually transmitted infections, caused by Chlamydia trachomatis. It is often asymptomatic but can lead to serious reproductive complications if untreated. Older adults who do not use condoms remain at risk for contacting chlamydia.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A phenomenon associated with matchmaking websites in which having so many possible romantic options makes a thoughtful, satisfying choice difficult or overwhelming.

A waxy, fatty substance carried by lipoprotein molecules in the blood. It is produced by the body to create hormones and to aid in the digestion of fatty foods, and is also found in many foods. While the body requires cholesterol for normal function, excessively high levels can lead to plaque formation in the arteries, increasing the risk of heart disease and stroke. Total cholesterol is calculated as HDL + LDL + 20% of triglycerides.

A long-term inflammation of the bronchial tubes characterized by persistent cough and mucus production. Individuals with chronic bronchitis experience more pronounced effects of normal age-related lung changes, resulting in greater reductions in lung capacity and oxygen exchange than non-affected individuals.

Long-term health conditions that persist over extended periods and typically cannot be cured but can be managed. Examples relevant to middle adulthood include diabetes and arthritis. Chronic diseases contribute to secondary aging by reducing overall health, physical functioning, and quality of life, and may accelerate other age-related declines.

Persistent or long-lasting pain that significantly impacts daily functioning. Individuals with chronic pain experience an average of 42 minutes of sleep debt per night, contributing substantially to overall sleep deficiency.

A pattern in young adult romantic relationships in which partners break up and get back together again repeatedly. Churning correlates with the stormiest relationships; in the Toledo, Ohio study, 44 percent of participants were churners and half of churners fought both physically and verbally.

Tiny muscle fibers that surround the lens of the eye and control its shape by contracting and relaxing. When viewing near objects, these muscles contract to increase the curvature (bending) of the lens. With aging, these muscles weaken, contributing to presbyopia and the reduced ability to focus on close objects.

The body's internal biological timing system that regulates sleep-wake cycles and many other physiological processes. Light is the most important timing cue for the circadian clock, and disruptions caused by variable bedtimes can impair glucose metabolism and energy homeostasis.

The process by which the body's circadian clock aligns physiological functions, including sleep, hormone release, and metabolism, with the 24-hour light-dark cycle. Irregular bedtime schedules disrupt circadian timing by exposing the body to varying levels of light at inconsistent times.

The population of individuals aged 16 and older who are employed and not confined to institutions such as prisons or care facilities. This group forms the basis for U.S. labor force participation statistics. The participation rate peaked at 67% in the late 1990s and was projected to decline to 62% by 2022.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A process in which a person or animal learns to associate a neutral stimulus with a meaningful stimulus, gradually reacting to the neutral stimulus with the same response as to the meaningful one. Focuses on a specific response to a specific stimulus. Also called respondent conditioning.

The period of hormonal and reproductive change that occurs in both females and males during middle adulthood. In females, it refers to the transition leading to menopause, including perimenopause and menopause itself, characterized by declining estrogen levels. In males, it refers to a gradual decline in testosterone levels. The climacteric involves physical, hormonal, and sometimes psychological changes.

The midlife transition period during which fertility declines in both men and women. It is biologically based but also influenced by environmental factors. In women, it culminates in menopause; in men, it involves a gradual reduction in reproductive capacity and hormonal changes.

The broad transitional phase in both men and women during middle adulthood involving hormonal and physiological changes related to reproductive aging. In women, it encompasses perimenopause, menopause, and postmenopause; in men, it involves a gradual decline in testosterone. It is associated with changes in sexual functioning, mood, and physical health.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The study of how cognitive abilities change across the adult lifespan. Research shows a complex pattern in which some abilities such as vocabulary and inductive reasoning improve into the 70s, while others such as perceptual speed and processing speed decline, influenced by both biological aging and accumulated experience.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The broad set of mental abilities including memory, processing speed, attention, reasoning, and problem solving. Expertise cannot fully compensate for all losses in general cognitive functioning that occur with aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The documented phenomenon in which some middle-aged individuals show actual gains in cognitive functioning rather than decline. Those with cognitive improvements tend to be more physically, cognitively, and socially active.

glossary.term: Cognitive Theory and Piaget's Four Stages
glossary.def: Thoughts and expectations profoundly affect actions, attitudes, beliefs, and assumptions. Piaget's four stages: SENSORIMOTOR (0-2, senses and motor skills; object permanence), PREOPERATIONAL (2-6, symbolic thinking; egocentric), CONCRETE OPERATIONAL (6-11, logic applied to real experiences; conservation), FORMAL OPERATIONAL (12+, abstract and hypothetical thinking; ethics). Intellectual advancement occurs lifelong because humans seek cognitive equilibrium.

The degree of individual differences in cognitive performance within an age group. Greater cognitive variability occurs among middle-aged adults compared to both younger and older adults, reflecting diverse trajectories of cognitive aging.

Living with an unrelated person—typically a romantic partner—to whom one is not married. Most young adults in the United States, England, and northern Europe cohabit rather than marry before age 25; half of all cohabitating couples in the United States plan on marrying eventually. Education increases the chance of marriage and marital childbearing.

An arrangement in which two people in a romantic relationship live together without being formally married. Teachman (2008) found that more than two-thirds of women under age 45 had cohabited with a partner between their first and second marriages. Cohabitation has contributed to declining marriage and remarriage rates.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: People born within the same historical period who move through life together, experiencing the same events, technologies, and cultural shifts at the same ages. The effect of any experience varies depending on a person's cohort.

A group of people who share a common characteristic, typically birth year or birth period, and who move through historical time together. In developmental research, cohort effects refer to the influence of shared historical and cultural experiences on development. The Baby Boom cohort (born 1946-1964) is a well-known cohort that has significantly influenced research on middle adulthood.

The influence that being born and raised in a particular historical period has on individuals' development, attitudes, and experiences. A major criticism of Levinson's research is that because all participants were of similar ages and likely shared historical experiences, the findings may reflect cohort-specific trends rather than universal developmental patterns.

The influence of generational or historical experiences on attitudes and behaviors. In the context of religion and spirituality, a cohort effect explains why older individuals tend to be more religious and spiritual than younger individuals — older cohorts were socialized more strongly to be religious during their formative years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A research methodology used in longitudinal studies, such as the Seattle Longitudinal Study, that combines cross-sectional and longitudinal approaches by testing multiple age cohorts at multiple time points to distinguish age effects from cohort and period effects.

A form of generativity described by Erikson in which individuals contribute to and engage with the broader society beyond the family and workplace. It represents a way of extending care and contribution to the wider community during middle adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of three grandparenting styles identified by Cherlin and Furstenberg (1986). Approximately 55% of grandparents fall into this category — making it the most common style. These grandparents spend time with and enjoy activities alongside their grandchildren but exercise little authority or control over them. They prefer to avoid interfering with parenting decisions and tend to act more like friends to their grandchildren.

A category of divorced individuals identified by Hetherington and Kelly (2002) who used their divorce experience to grow emotionally but chose to remain single. Like enhancers, the majority of competent loners were women.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Piaget's third stage — ability to reason logically about direct experiences and perceptions. Key abilities: CLASSIFICATION: organizing things into groups according to shared characteristics (by age 8, most children can classify). SERIATION: knowledge that things can be arranged in logical series. CONSERVATION: now mastered. Logic is applied to concrete (direct, real) experiences but NOT yet to abstract ideas. Culture affects content and method of learning (Vygotsky's contrast). Children are 'apprentices in learning.'

The practice of using a barrier contraceptive device (condom) during sexual activity to reduce the risk of pregnancy and sexually transmitted infections. Adults over the age of 40 have been identified as having the lowest rates of condom use among sexually active populations, increasing their STI risk.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the Big Five personality traits characterized by organization, dependability, self-discipline, and goal-directed behavior. Generative adults of both sexes were found to score high on conscientiousness in research studies linking personality to generativity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The principle that the amount of a substance remains the same (is conserved) when its appearance changes. Preoperational children fail conservation tasks because they center on appearance, use static reasoning, and cannot reverse the process mentally. Classic tasks: liquid poured into a taller, narrower glass appears to be more; candy spread farther apart appears to be more; a rolled-out ball of dough appears to have more. Children typically master conservation around ages 6–7 in the concrete operational stage.

The Western ideal of love described by Robert Sternberg that combines all three dimensions: passion, intimacy, and commitment. It is difficult to achieve; passion may fade, intimacy may grow and stabilize, and commitment may deepen over time.

Methods used to prevent pregnancy during sexual activity. Women who have not yet reached full menopause (defined as 12 consecutive months without menstruation) remain at risk for pregnancy and should continue using contraception, even if their cycles have become irregular.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Higher-order cognitive mechanisms involved in regulating and directing other mental functions, including selective attention, strategic judgment, and appropriate action. In late adulthood, control processes are particularly impaired, especially in retrieval, and older adults increasingly rely on prior knowledge, general principles, and rules of thumb rather than analytical forethought.

The cognitive and behavioral efforts made by an individual to manage the internal and external demands of a stressor that taxes or exceeds their resources. Classified into two main categories: problem-focused coping (addressing the stressor directly) and emotion-focused coping (managing the emotional response to the stressor).

Cognitive and behavioral efforts used to manage internal and external demands perceived as stressful. Carver, Scheier & Weintraub (1989) categorized coping as problem-focused (addressing the stressor directly) or emotion-focused (regulating emotional response). Billings & Moos (1981) also contributed foundational work.

CORE KNOWLEDGE: innate understanding that gravity makes objects fall, moving objects are stopped by solid walls, and adult gaze signals important information. GAZE-FOLLOWING: babies instinctively know that what caregivers look at is important. OWN-RACE EFFECT: infants are much better at recognizing individuals from their own ethnic group than from other groups — related to the fusiform face area; appears by 6 months.

CORPUS CALLOSUM: a long, thick band of nerve fibers that connects the left and right hemispheres of the brain and allows communication between them. As myelination progresses, signals between the hemispheres become quicker and clearer, enabling better thinking and less clumsiness. LATERALIZATION: specialization in certain functions by each side of the brain, with one side dominant for each activity. The left side controls the right side of the body and vice versa. Contemporary research shows the distinction is exaggerated — both sides are involved in almost every skill.

A stress hormone produced by the adrenal glands that, when chronically elevated due to insufficient sleep, keeps the body in a state of alertness and hyperarousal, thereby increasing blood pressure and compounding the negative effects of sleep deprivation.

Surgical procedures performed to alter or improve physical appearance, often used by women experiencing societal pressure to maintain a youthful look. The prevalence of cosmetic surgery is partly driven by media support and anti-aging cultural norms that disproportionately affect women in midlife.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers cited in the context of automatic processing, noting that expertise allows individuals to process information faster and more effectively, making complex responses appear instinctive over time.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The ability to generate novel, useful, or original ideas and solutions. In middle adulthood, creativity is linked to the concept of flow and tends to reflect accumulated experience and crystallized knowledge rather than solely raw processing speed.

The ability to generate novel and valuable ideas, products, or solutions. Csikszentmihalyi (1996) linked creativity to the experience of flow and identified characteristics of highly creative people including curiosity, drive, intellectual values, and the ability to transcend the sense of self. He disputed the notion that creative people are typically unhappy or tortured.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the three components of generativity described by Erikson, referring to the generation of new ideas, inventions, artistic works, or innovations. It contributes to cultural advancement and represents a form of symbolic legacy beyond biological reproduction.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A time when a particular type of developmental growth in body or behavior MUST happen if it is ever going to happen. If the necessary experience or biological event does not occur during this window, the development will not occur at all.

A chronic inflammatory bowel disease that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract. It is a recognized risk factor for gallstone formation, likely due to impaired bile acid absorption in the affected small intestine.

A research method that collects data from different individuals at different ages at a single point in time. Vaillant criticized Levinson's use of this design for adult development research, arguing it can lead to erroneous conclusions such as the inevitability of a midlife crisis because it does not track the same individuals over time.

The accumulated knowledge, skills, and information acquired over a lifetime through experience and education. Crystallized intelligence tends to increase or remain stable throughout middle and older adulthood, in contrast to fluid intelligence.

The accumulated knowledge of the world acquired throughout one's lifetime, including semantic knowledge, vocabulary, language, history, and geography. Crystallized intelligence increases with age and reflects the wisdom and experience gained over time, giving older adults advantages in knowledge-based tasks.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The variation in how menopause is perceived, described, and experienced across different cultures and ethnicities. Cultures that lack specific language for symptoms like hot flashes (e.g., Hmong, Mayan) tend not to report those symptoms. Some cultures, such as Indian and Japanese, view menopause positively as a transition to greater freedom and social status.

The differences across cultures in how aging individuals are perceived, treated, and valued. Research shows that attitudes about aging women vary by race, ethnicity, culture, and sexual orientation—for example, Black and Latina women hold fewer negative stereotypes about aging than white women, and Asian women may gain greater household authority as they age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: CYBERBULLYING: bullying that occurs via Internet insults and rumors, texting, anonymous phone calls, and video embarrassment. Anonymity often brings out the worst in people. Particularly prevalent between ages 11 and 14. Adolescent victims are likely to suffer from depression and may commit suicide. SEXTING: sending sexual photographs; dangers include pictures being forwarded without the subject's knowledge. Adolescent imaginary audience can go viral — thousands can see a private message instantly. Most parents and teachers are unaware of cyberbullying; few laws successfully prevent it.

Minor, everyday stressors such as getting stuck in traffic or arguing with a friend. Research by DeLongis, Folkman, and Lazarus (1988) and Twisk et al. (1999) demonstrated that even small daily hassles can raise blood pressure, alter stress hormones, and suppress immune system function, contributing to illness risk.

The practice of praying every day, used as a measure of individual religious devotion. In the United States, 64% of women report praying daily compared to 47% of men. In Israel's Orthodox Jewish community, a higher percentage of men than women report daily prayer due to religious requirements.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: American psychologist who published 'The Seasons of a Man's Life' in 1978, proposing a stage theory of adult development based on in-depth interviews with 40 men aged 35-45. He argued that a midlife crisis was a normal and expected part of adult development, though his methodology has been widely criticized.

Brown or discolored patches on the skin that develop with aging, primarily as a result of long-term sun exposure. They occur due to uneven distribution and clumping of melanin in the skin and are also referred to as age spots or liver spots. They are a common sign of photodamage accumulated over the lifespan.

Authors of a UK-based study examining the rate of hearing problems across adulthood. Their research found that the rate of hearing problems doubled between ages 40 and 55 and tripled by age 64, providing key epidemiological data on the prevalence of hearing decline in middle adulthood.

Researchers who investigated the relationship between generativity and the Big Five personality traits, finding that generative individuals scored high on conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, and openness to experience, and low on neuroticism.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A stage of slow-wave, non-REM sleep during which growth hormone is released. This growth hormone stimulates protein synthesis, breaks down fat for energy, and promotes cell division. Decreases in deep sleep are associated with reduced growth hormone secretion and physical decline in aging.

Intentional, effortful practice of skills aimed at maintaining or improving performance. As adults age, they must be more deliberate in their practice of skills in order to maintain them, because the passive accumulation of experience is less sufficient with advancing age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A significant change in the composition or distribution of a population over time. In the U.S. labor force context, this refers to the aging of the workforce: by 2022, adults aged 55+ were projected to make up 38% of the population, up from 26% in 1992, while young adult labor force entrants (ages 16–24) were projected to shrink by nearly half a percent.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The formation of new dendritic branches on neurons in adulthood, which is variable in rate and extent. Dendrite growth is considered evidence of lifelong neural plasticity and contributes to cognitive adaptability in older adults.

glossary.term: Depression, Suicide, and Delinquency in Adolescence
glossary.def: DEPRESSION: self-esteem dips at puberty for both sexes; familism is protective. MAJOR DEPRESSIVE DISORDER: deep sadness, hopelessness, lethargy, and worthlessness. LGBT youth have higher risk of depression and anxiety. CLUSTER SUICIDES: several suicides committed by members of a group within a brief period. PARASUICIDE: any potentially lethal action against self that does not result in death — common; completed suicide is not. Girls attempt more; boys complete more. LIFE-COURSE-PERSISTENT OFFENDER: criminal activity begins in early adolescence and continues throughout life. ADOLESCENCE-LIMITED OFFENDER: criminal activity stops by age 21. Delinquency pathway: stubbornness → defiance → shoplifting → more serious crime.

Positive changes, improvements, or growth in abilities, knowledge, or functioning that occur across the lifespan during a given developmental stage. In middle adulthood, developmental gains may include increased wisdom, expertise, emotional regulation, and social competence, even as some physical capacities begin to decline.

Declines or reductions in abilities, physical capacity, or functioning that occur during a given developmental stage. In middle adulthood, developmental losses may include declines in peak physical performance, changes in sensory acuity, and shifts in certain cognitive abilities, though these are often offset by gains in other areas.

DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOPATHOLOGY: links the study of typical development with the study of disorders. General principles: abnormality is normal; disability changes year by year; life may be better or worse in adulthood; diagnosis and treatment reflect social context. MULTIFINALITY: one cause can have many (multiple) final manifestations. EQUIFINALITY: one symptom can have many causes. COMORBIDITY: presence of two or more unrelated disease conditions at the same time in the same person. These principles should lead to caution in diagnosis and treatment.

A group of ideas, assumptions, and generalizations that interpret and illuminate the thousands of observations made about human growth. Provides a framework for understanding how and why people change as they grow older. Theories produce hypotheses, generate discoveries, and offer practical guidance.

A metabolic disease characterized by high blood sugar levels, either due to insufficient insulin production (Type 1) or insulin resistance (Type 2). Diabetes is a recognized risk factor for gallstone development, possibly related to altered bile composition and gallbladder motility.

A metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and elevated blood glucose levels. The American Diabetes Association (2016) standards of medical care emphasize lifestyle modification and pharmacological management. CDC (2014d) reported national statistics on diabetes prevalence.

A chronic disease in which the body does not properly regulate the amount of glucose in the blood, either due to insufficient insulin production or improper use of insulin by the body's cells. In adults, 90–95% of diagnosed cases are Type 2. As of 2012, 29 million Americans (over 9% of the population) had diabetes. Diabetes is associated with serious complications including cardiovascular disease, diabetic retinopathy, kidney failure, neuropathy, limb amputations, and hearing loss.

Nerve damage caused by long-term high blood glucose levels in people with diabetes. It can cause pain, tingling, numbness, and loss of sensation, particularly in the extremities. Neuropathy is a contributing factor in the approximately 60% of non-traumatic limb amputations that occur in people with diabetes.

Damage to the small blood vessels in the retina caused by diabetes, which may lead to vision loss or blindness. Among diabetics aged 40 or older, 28.5% showed signs of diabetic retinopathy, and more than 4% had the advanced form. It is one of the leading causes of blindness in adults.

The primary muscle responsible for breathing, located beneath the lungs. With aging, the diaphragm weakens as part of broader age-related muscle changes, which reduces lung capacity and can contribute to shortness of breath and fatigue.

The second (bottom) number in a blood pressure reading, representing the pressure in the blood vessels when the heart is at rest between beats. Normal diastolic pressure is under 80 mmHg; 80–89 indicates prehypertension; 90 or higher indicates hypertension.

Federal nutrition recommendations jointly published by the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (USHHS) and U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA), most recently cited in this content as the 2015-2020 edition. They provide science-based advice on sodium, fat, added sugars, caloric intake, and physical activity for Americans aged 2 and older.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The mistaken belief that a deviation from some norm is necessarily inferior to the behavior or characteristics that meet the standard. A common error in cross-cultural comparisons that fails to recognize cultural variation as equally valid.

Sensitivity to any particular experience differs from one person to another because of the particular genes each person has inherited, or what happened to them in the past. Some people are more vulnerable than others to certain experiences, usually due to genetic differences.

Researchers who studied sexuality in older adults and highlighted the importance of sexual activity and intimacy throughout the lifespan, emphasizing that many older adults remain highly interested in and engaged in sexual activity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The phenomenon, noted by Charness and Krampe (2006), in which increasing practice does not yield the same advances in older adults as similar efforts produce in younger individuals. The rate of return for effort diminishes as we age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Livingston (2014) found that 27% of adults aged 45–54 were divorced, with 57% of divorced adults being women, reflecting men's higher likelihood of remarriage. Brown and Lin (2013) noted that while the overall U.S. divorce rate has declined since the 1990s, the rate among those 50 and older has doubled.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A societal phenomenon in which aging men are viewed as 'distinguished' while aging women are viewed as 'old.' Men are traditionally valued for achievements, competence, and power, so they are not considered old until physically unable to work, whereas women have been valued for reproductive capabilities and are considered old once postmenopausal. First described by Teuscher & Teuscher (2006) in the context of Western society.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Unauthorized immigrants to the United States who meet five specific criteria: under age 31, entered the U.S. before age 16, lived continuously in the U.S. for at least five years, never convicted of a felony or serious misdemeanor, and graduated from a U.S. high school or served in the armed forces. They cannot become citizens and differ from peers in their original nations.

Drug use becomes widespread from ages 10 to 25, then decreases. Drug use before age 15 is the best predictor of later drug use. ALCOHOL: most frequently abused drug among North American teenagers; heavy drinking may permanently impair memory and self-control by damaging the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex. MARIJUANA: adolescents who regularly smoke are more likely to drop out, become teenage parents, and be unemployed. TOBACCO: slows growth, reduces appetite, causes protein/vitamin deficiencies, damages developing hearts and lungs. GENERATIONAL FORGETTING: each new generation forgets what the previous generation learned about the harm drugs do. Drug use excites the limbic system and interferes with the prefrontal cortex — users are more emotional and less reflective.

A condition that occurs when the eyes do not produce sufficient tears, or when tears evaporate too quickly due to incorrect consistency, leading to dryness, irritation, and potential surface damage to the eye. Although it can affect people at any age, nearly 5 million Americans over age 50 experience it. It affects women more than men, particularly after menopause, and women who experienced early menopause are at heightened risk.

glossary.term: Dual Processing: Intuitive vs. Analytic Thought
glossary.def: INTUITIVE THOUGHT: thought arising from emotion or hunch, beyond rational explanation; influenced by past experiences and cultural assumptions. ANALYTIC THOUGHT: thought resulting from analysis — systematic ranking of pros and cons, risks and consequences, possibilities and facts; depends on logic and rationality. Adolescents rely MORE on intuitive thinking than adults. With maturity, adolescents gradually balance formal analytic thinking and emotional, experiential thinking. Brain basis: limbic system activated by puberty; prefrontal cortex matures more gradually — producing the dual processing gap.

The simultaneous loss of both vision and hearing, which increases significantly in midlife and older adulthood. Dawes et al. (2014) documented vision impairment and dual sensory problems in middle age.

A Vygotsky-derived assessment approach that measures learning potential rather than current performance. Unlike static IQ tests, the assessor actively intervenes during testing (providing hints, coaching, feedback) to reveal a student's ZPD and capacity to learn from instruction. DA is both diagnostic and developmental — it promotes development while assessing it.

Persistent or recurrent pain during vaginal intercourse, often experienced by women during and after menopause due to reduced estrogen levels causing vaginal atrophy and decreased lubrication. It can significantly affect sexual functioning and quality of life.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A developmental orientation identified by Levinson in which younger adults are primarily motivated by aspirations and plans for the future. This future orientation gives way in midlife to a greater emphasis on the present and on current quality of life, contributing to the psychological shift characteristic of the midlife transition.

glossary.term: Early Childhood Education: Programs and Approaches
glossary.def: CHILD-CENTERED programs (Piaget/Vygotsky influence): stress children's natural inclination to learn through play; self-paced exploration; includes Montessori (individual pride, literacy tasks), Reggio Emilia (creativity in carefully designed setting, originated in Reggio Emilia, Italy), and Waldorf (creativity, social understanding, emotional growth). TEACHER-DIRECTED programs: stress academic subjects taught by a teacher; clear work/play distinction; higher child-adult ratio; less expensive. HEAD START: most widespread U.S. early childhood program, begun in 1965; strongest benefits for children in poverty or with disabilities; vocabulary gains persisted longest. Long-term intensive programs (Perry/High Scope, Abecedarian) show lasting gains.

A behavioral risk factor for heartburn and GERD. Lying down shortly after eating reduces the effect of gravity in keeping stomach acid from entering the esophagus, increasing the likelihood of acid reflux.

glossary.term: Eating Disorders: Anorexia, Bulimia, Binge Eating
glossary.def: ANOREXIA NERVOSA: characterized by self-starvation; individual voluntarily undereats and often overexercises. DSM-5 diagnosis requires: significantly low body weight (BMI 17 or lower), intense fear of weight gain, disturbed body perception. Can be fatal. BULIMIA NERVOSA (binge-purge syndrome): bingeing and subsequent purging through induced vomiting and/or laxatives. DSM-5 requires: bingeing and purging at least once a week for three months, uncontrollable urges to overeat, sense of self tied to body shape. BINGE EATING DISORDER: introduced in DSM-5, recognizes bingeing sometimes associated with anorexia. Origins: cultural image, stress, puberty, hormones, childhood and family patterns.

Approach taken by most developmentalists, in which aspects of each of the various theories of development are selectively applied rather than adhering exclusively to one theory. Helps guard against bias and facilitates open-mindedness. Recognizes that no single theory explains everything.

Each person is affected by many social contexts arranged in nested levels: MICROSYSTEM (immediate, direct: family, school, peers, neighborhood), MESOSYSTEM (interactions between microsystems), EXOSYSTEM (external networks: community structures, media, medical institutions), MACROSYSTEM (cultural patterns, political philosophies, economic policies), CHRONOSYSTEM (dimension of time). Each level interacts with all others.

The degree to which cognitive tests and research measures reflect real-world functioning and the abilities needed in everyday life. Ecological validity is particularly important when assessing cognition in the elderly, as standard tests may not accurately capture practical cognitive abilities.

A period of significant decline in economic activity, typically involving rising unemployment, reduced consumer spending, and financial instability. The U.S. experienced notable recessions starting in 2001 and again in 2008. During the most recent recession, 55% of adults reported workplace problems such as fewer hours, pay cuts, or switching to part-time work.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Weeks 3 through 8. Primitive streak becomes the neural tube, forming the brain and spine (CNS). Head takes shape; eyes, ears, nose, and mouth form; heart begins to pulsate; extremities develop. KEY FACT: by week 8, ALL body organs and systems have formed in rudimentary form. After this point development focuses on maturation and growth of each system.

A coping strategy that regulates the emotional response to a stressor rather than addressing the stressor directly. For example, watching a funny movie to reduce exam anxiety. More effective in the short term or when stressors are uncontrollable (e.g., death of a loved one). Long-term reliance on emotion-focused coping is less effective for mental wellness than problem-focused coping.

6 weeks: social smile evoked by human faces. 3-4 months: laughter, often associated with curiosity. 6 months: anger as a healthy response to frustration; sadness appears with cortisol increase. 9 months: fear emerges; stranger wariness (infant cries at unfamiliar faces); separation anxiety (distress when caregiver leaves — if strong after age 3, may be an emotional disorder). Toddlerhood: new emotions including pride, shame, embarrassment, disgust, and guilt, which require self-awareness.

The ability to manage and modulate one's emotional responses to stimuli. Middle-aged adults demonstrate improved emotional regulation, including greater calmness, reduced neuroticism, and better ability to navigate social situations.

The ability to control when and how emotions are expressed. Possibly due to connections between the limbic system and prefrontal cortex. The MOST IMPORTANT psychosocial accomplishment between ages 2 and 6. EFFORTFUL CONTROL: the ability to regulate one's emotions and actions through effort, not simply through natural inclination. Is a function of executive function. Maturation, learning, and culture all contribute.

Also called the post-parental period, this refers to the stage of life when children have grown up and left the family home, typically occurring during midlife. It is considered a normative developmental event because parents generally anticipate that their children will eventually leave. The empty nest creates both positive and negative emotional responses depending on the individual and cultural context.

A concept referring to the great emotional distress, loneliness, and sense of loss experienced by some parents, typically mothers, after their children have left home. It is linked to the absence of alternative roles through which the parent could establish their identity. Research by Bouchard (2013) found that relatively few parents actually report significant loneliness or loss after children leave, questioning the prevalence of this syndrome.

A psychological condition experienced by parents, particularly mothers, when their children leave home. Characterized by feelings of loss, sadness, and purposelessness. Research shows many parents adapt positively, and some experience improved marital satisfaction.

The physiological balance between energy intake and energy expenditure. Disrupted circadian timing due to irregular sleep schedules can impair energy homeostasis, contributing to metabolic dysfunction and weight gain.

A category of divorced individuals identified by Hetherington and Kelly (2002) who used the divorce experience as an opportunity for self-improvement and to seek more productive intimate relationships. The overwhelming majority of enhancers were women.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Entity vs. Incremental Approach to Intelligence
glossary.def: ENTITY APPROACH: sees ability as innate, a fixed quantity present at birth; rejects the idea that effort enhances achievement. Students with this view give up when challenged. INCREMENTAL APPROACH: holds that intelligence can be directly increased by effort; suggests children can master whatever they seek to learn if they pay attention, participate, study, and complete homework. School transitions (middle school, high school) often increase bullying, decrease achievement, depression, and eating disorders — particularly stressful when combined with puberty. HIGH-STAKES TESTS: evaluations critical in determining whether a student will graduate or be promoted.

A dimension of psychological well-being referring to an individual's ability to manage and adapt to their surrounding environment effectively. It is one of the outcomes positively associated with higher levels of spirituality in the National Survey of Midlife data.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Referring to the effects of environmental forces on the expression of an individual's or species' genetic inheritance. All important human characteristics are epigenetic — every trait, psychological as well as physical, is influenced by the interaction of genes and environment.

The inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for sexual intercourse, either consistently or intermittently. It becomes increasingly common with age: approximately 4% of men in their 40s, 17% in their 60s, and 47% of men over 75 experience chronic ED. About 50% of men aged 40–70 experience intermittent ED. Medical conditions account for 70% of chronic cases and psychological factors for 10–20%.

A medical condition in men characterized by the persistent inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for satisfactory sexual intercourse.
