glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The process by which cancer cells break away from the primary tumor site and travel through the lymph system or bloodstream to form new (metastatic) tumors in other parts of the body. The metastatic tumor is the same type of cancer as the original primary tumor. Metastasis is a key feature that distinguishes malignant from benign tumors.

The developmental period of the lifespan between early adulthood and late adulthood, most commonly defined as ages 40-45 to 60-65. Also referred to as midlife, it is characterized by both developmental gains and losses, considerable individual differences, and is one of the least studied periods of the lifespan. Cultural factors influence the specific age boundaries and developmental tasks associated with this period.

A stage of the human lifespan typically spanning from approximately age 40 to 65. It is characterized by a range of physical changes including the onset of graying and thinning hair, increased skin wrinkling, the beginning of sarcopenia, and changes in lung capacity, many of which are influenced by both genetics and lifestyle factors.

The developmental life stage roughly spanning the ages of 40–65, during which sleep quality and duration often decline. This stage is associated with increased risks of metabolic disorders, sleep disturbances related to menopause, and reduced deep sleep.

The developmental period generally spanning ages 40 to 65, characterized by continued brain plasticity, improved emotional regulation, peak financial decision-making ability, and diverse cognitive trajectories including both gains and losses.

A stage of the lifespan typically associated with middle age, during which individuals often possess a substantial store of knowledge and experience that allows them to recognize familiar problems, ignore irrelevant information, and focus on important aspects of issues. Many people reach the peak of their careers during this period.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The developmental stage generally spanning ages 40 to 65, during which individuals experience peak career involvement, potential job satisfaction, physiological changes, and significant life transitions including preparation for retirement. This stage is characterized by both peak career performance and vulnerability to workplace challenges such as burnout and discrimination.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A life stage occurring approximately between ages 45-50 according to Levinson, during which individuals commit to new choices made during the midlife transition and direct their energies into these revised commitments. More broadly used to refer to the period between roughly 40-65 years of age.

The developmental period roughly spanning ages 40–65, during which Erikson's seventh psychosocial stage (Generativity vs. Stagnation) takes place. Individuals during this time are primarily concerned with leaving a positive legacy, raising families, advancing careers, and contributing to their communities.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The life stage roughly spanning ages 40–65 in which many individuals take on the role of grandparent in addition to maintaining relationships with their own children and aging parents. Becoming a grandparent is identified as a significant new relational role during this developmental period.

Period between early childhood and early adolescence, approximately ages 6 to 11. The HEALTHIEST years of the life span — fatal diseases or accidents are rare. Good childhood habits protect later adult health. Permanent teeth come in. Muscles become stronger. Children run faster and exercise longer. Children can master any motor skill that does not require an adult-sized body. ORAL HEALTH is important — gum disease can lead to cardiovascular and other systemic diseases. EMBODIED COGNITION: movement improves intellectual functioning through better cerebral blood flow and increased neurotransmitters.

The common accumulation of body fat in the abdominal region experienced by many adults during midlife, often described as a thickening around the midsection. Men tend to gain fat on the upper abdomen and back, while women tend to gain fat on the waist and upper arms. It is largely driven by a slowing metabolism and age-related muscle loss.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An alternative term for middle adulthood, referring to the approximate middle portion of the human lifespan, typically spanning ages 40-45 to 60-65. It is a period marked by ongoing physical, cognitive, and psychosocial development, with both gains and losses occurring simultaneously.

The period of adult development roughly spanning the ages of 40 to 65, characterized by significant biological, psychological, and social changes. For women, midlife often includes menopause and increased concern about aging due to societal pressures related to youthful beauty and identity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The period of adult development generally spanning ages 40–65, characterized by transitions in career, family roles, physical health, and psychological identity, including phenomena such as the midlife crisis, generativity, and the empty nest.

A controversial concept suggesting that middle-aged adults experience a period of significant psychological turmoil, questioning, and identity re-evaluation triggered by awareness of aging and mortality. Its universality and severity are debated in developmental psychology.

A period of psychological distress and reevaluation that some adults experience during midlife, typically between the ages of 40-60. Characterized by questioning life choices, sense of urgency, and sometimes dramatic behavioral changes. Research suggests it is not universal or inevitable, occurring in only about 23% of midlife adults, and is more common among highly educated individuals triggered by major life events rather than fear of aging.

A cognitive reorientation that occurs during middle adulthood in which individuals shift from thinking about how long they have lived to thinking about how many years they have left to live. Identified by Neugarten (1968), this shift reflects a growing awareness of mortality and influences priorities and values.

A developmental stage identified by Levinson occurring approximately between ages 40-45, characterized by reevaluating previous commitments, making dramatic changes if necessary, expressing previously ignored talents or aspirations, and feeling increased urgency about life and its meaning.

A psychologist who developed the theory of flow (1990) and researched creativity in relation to optimal experience. He identified characteristics of creative people including curiosity, drive, value for intellectual endeavors, and ability to lose one's sense of self. He also argued that the 'tortured creative person' is a myth and that creative people tend to be very happy with their lives.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A quorum of at least 10 Jewish men required for communal worship services in Orthodox Judaism. The requirement ensures that men have high rates of synagogue attendance in Orthodox communities, which is why Jewish men in Orthodox communities attend synagogue more often than Jewish women.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Physical activity that raises the heart rate and causes increased breathing but still allows conversation. Examples include brisk walking. Guidelines recommend at least 150 minutes per week of moderate intensity aerobic activity for substantial health benefits, with sessions lasting at least 10 minutes, preferably spread throughout the week.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Exercise performed at a moderate intensity level, such as brisk walking or cycling. When incorporated into leisure time, moderate physical activity has been shown to provide additional physical health benefits beyond rest alone (Lee et al., 2015).

A lifestyle that includes physical activity equivalent to walking more than 1.5 to 3 miles per day at 3 to 4 miles per hour, in addition to the activities of independent living. Caloric needs are higher than for sedentary individuals; for example, a moderately active 36-40 year old male requires approximately 2,600 calories per day.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A peer-reviewed journal published by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics that covers topics including employment, wages, productivity, and occupational safety. It is cited in this content as the source for workforce participation projections by age, gender, and race through 2022.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Any person or resource that possesses greater knowledge or skill than the learner on a specific task. The MKO is not necessarily a teacher or adult — it can be a peer, sibling, or technology tool (educational software, AI). The role is fluid: learners can become MKOs for others as their own competence grows. MKO guides learners through the ZPD via scaffolding.

GROSS MOTOR SKILLS: physical abilities involving large body movements such as walking and jumping. Develop in cephalocaudal direction; normal walking range is roughly 9-15+ months. FINE MOTOR SKILLS: physical abilities involving small body movements, especially of the hands and fingers. The PINCER GRASP (controlling thumb and forefinger) is a key milestone enabling purposeful grasping and self-feeding. First movements are reflexes that become skills through practice and encouragement.

A household in which multiple generations of a family live together, such as middle-aged parents living with both their own aging parents and their adult children. Research suggests that living in a multigenerational household may serve as an economic safety net for young adults, as those living with parents are less likely to be living in poverty (10%) compared to those living independently (17%).

A living arrangement in which three or more generations of a family reside together under one roof. This dynamic is more common in Latin America, Asia, and Africa, but has been increasing in the United States. Grandparents in these households often play a greater role in the day-to-day activities of grandchildren.

Exercises of moderate or high intensity that work all major muscle groups, such as weight lifting and resistance training. Guidelines recommend performing these activities on two or more days per week. These exercises help maintain or increase muscle mass, which supports a higher metabolic rate and reduces age-related fat accumulation.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The proportion of muscle tissue to fat tissue in the body. During middle adulthood, this ratio changes for both men and women, with fat accumulating particularly in the stomach (abdominal) area while muscle mass declines, contributing to changes in body composition and metabolic health.

The process by which nerve fibers are coated with a fatty white substance (myelin) that speeds neural transmission. In late adulthood, myelination thins as part of senescence, contributing to slower processing speed and cognitive decline.

The process by which axons become coated with myelin, a fatty substance that speeds the transmission of nerve impulses from neuron to neuron. The primary reason for faster thinking in early childhood. A gradual increase in myelination makes 5-year-olds much quicker than 3-year-olds, who are quicker than toddlers. The brain reaches 90% of adult weight by age 6.

A refractive error of the eye, commonly known as nearsightedness, in which distant objects appear blurry because light focuses in front of the retina rather than directly on it. Highly myopic individuals are at greater risk for floaters and retinal complications.

Researchers who described flow as the height of enjoyment, finding that individuals who experience flow more frequently judge their lives as more gratifying. Their work extended Csikszentmihalyi's theory of flow to examine its relationship with life satisfaction and well-being.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A U.S. federal agency within the National Institutes of Health (NIH) that conducts and supports research on eye diseases and visual disorders. It is cited as a source for clinical information on presbyopia, floaters, and dry eye syndrome in the context of age-related sensory changes.

A nonprofit organization dedicated to improving sleep health. The National Sleep Foundation's Sleep in America polls have documented declining sleep duration among Americans and have provided data on sleep disorders, sleep debt, and the impact of lifestyle factors on sleep.

glossary.term: National Survey of Midlife in the United States (MIDUS)
glossary.def: A large-scale survey conducted in the United States used to study health and well-being across adulthood. The 2005 data from this survey were analyzed by Greenfield et al. (2009) to examine relationships between religiosity, spirituality, and psychological well-being.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: National Survey of Sexual Health and Behavior (NSSHB)
glossary.def: A large-scale nationally representative survey conducted by the Center for Sexual Health Promotion (2010) that collected data on sexual behaviors, attitudes, and health across the lifespan, including middle and older adulthood. It provided data showing that a significant majority of adults aged 40–59 remained sexually active.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Nature = the genetic influence on a person. Nurture = all non-genetic influences, from the mother's nutrition during pregnancy to national culture, family, school, community, and society. Nature always affects nurture, and nurture affects nature. The two are inseparable and interact continuously.

The experience of negative emotional states such as sadness, anxiety, and hostility. Higher levels of spirituality have been associated with lower levels of negative affect in research by Greenfield et al. (2009).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Bernice Neugarten, a developmental psychologist who noted that in midlife, adults no longer conceptualize life in terms of years lived since birth. Instead, they begin to think in terms of time remaining, reflecting a fundamental shift in temporal perspective that influences their goals and identity.

Early researchers in the field of grandparent roles who identified specific role functions including family historian and family advisor. Their work noted gender differences in role adoption, with grandfathers more likely to see themselves as family historians and advisors compared to grandmothers.

Synaptic links between neurons that form the structural basis of brain function, memory, and learning. White matter facilitates these connections, and their continued development into the 50s supports sustained cognitive functioning in middle adulthood.

A DSM-5 classification encompassing conditions characterized by significant decline in one or more cognitive domains. NCDs are classified as either major (previously called dementia) or mild (previously called mild cognitive impairment), depending on the severity of symptoms and impact on daily functioning.

The formation of new neurons in the adult brain, occurring particularly in the hippocampus and olfactory areas. This process provides cognitive adaptability to succeed in challenging and changing environments, though growth is slow, limited, and insufficient to restore the brain to its younger state.

glossary.term: Neuron Structure: Axon, Dendrite, Synapse, Neurotransmitter
glossary.def: AXON: fiber extending FROM a neuron that TRANSMITS electrochemical impulses to dendrites of other neurons. DENDRITE: fiber extending from a neuron that RECEIVES impulses transmitted from other neurons via their axons. SYNAPSE: intersection between the axon of one neuron and the dendrites of another — the gap where signals cross. NEUROTRANSMITTER: brain chemical that carries information across the synapse. MYELINIZATION: coating of axons with insulating substance that increases speed and efficiency of nerve transmission.

A personality trait characterized by emotional instability, anxiety, moodiness, and negative emotional reactivity. The middle-aged brain is described as less neurotic than younger brains, indicating improved emotional regulation with age.

One of the Big Five personality traits characterized by emotional instability, anxiety, moodiness, and negative affect. Generative adults were found to score low on neuroticism, suggesting that emotional stability is associated with successful generative functioning.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Episodes of excessive sweating during sleep, often associated with hot flashes during perimenopause and menopause. African American women were found in the SWAN study to experience higher rates of night sweats compared to other ethnic groups, though this finding varied across research sites.

An age-related deterioration in the ability to see adequately in low-light or nighttime conditions. It occurs because the pupil loses some of its ability to dilate fully in response to darkness, reducing the amount of light entering the eye. This increases sensitivity to glare from headlights and streetlights and reduces awareness of objects and movement in peripheral vision.

Cumulative hearing damage resulting from prolonged or repeated exposure to loud sounds. It is a major contributor to hearing loss in middle and older adulthood. Men are disproportionately affected due to higher rates of employment in loud occupational environments. Most such hearing loss is preventable by limiting exposure to intense noise levels.

Researcher cited in the context of strategic thinking among medical experts, noting that skilled clinicians generate more accurate diagnostic hypotheses than novices by discounting misleading symptoms and focusing on the most probable problem.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A developmental occurrence that is expected, anticipated, and commonly experienced at a particular life stage. The empty nest is considered a normative event because most parents expect their children to eventually become adults and leave home. Normative events tend to be less destabilizing than non-normative events because individuals can psychologically prepare for them.

An example of selective expertise in academic settings, where experienced students develop more efficient and strategic note-taking skills over time, such as writing only central ideas and developing personal shorthand, rather than transcribing everything verbatim as a novice might.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An individual who is new to a particular domain or skill set and relies heavily on explicit rules and conscious processing. Novices lack the pattern recognition and intuitive decision-making that characterize experts.

An individual who has limited experience with a particular task or domain. Novices typically follow step-by-step rules and procedures when problem solving, and their actions are more formulaic and less efficient compared to experts.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The ability to perform arithmetic and mathematical calculations. The Seattle Longitudinal Study found that numerical computation declines in middle and late adulthood, alongside perceptual speed, contrasting with skills like vocabulary that improve with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Dietary habits and nutritional intake during the middle adult years, recognized as a key factor in promoting optimal health and preventing or mitigating secondary aging. Poor diet is a controllable factor that can accelerate age-related physical decline, while a balanced, nutritious diet supports physical health, cognitive functioning, and overall well-being during this developmental stage.

A medical condition characterized by excess body fat, typically defined by a body mass index (BMI) of 30 or higher. Obesity increases cholesterol levels in the bile, which raises the risk of gallstone development. It is one of the major modifiable risk factors for gallstones.

A health condition characterized by excessive body fat that significantly increases the risk of diseases including diabetes, high blood pressure, and heart disease. In midlife, obesity has also been linked to earlier onset of Alzheimer's disease. Obesity rates in the United States have increased by 25% over the 15-year period studied in the United Health Foundation's report.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The realization that objects or people continue to exist when they are no longer in sight. Piaget's findings: infants younger than 8 months do not search for a hidden object. At about 8 months, infants search immediately after hiding but not if they must wait. By 2 years, children fully understand object permanence.

The use of internet-based platforms to seek romantic or companionate relationships. Montenegro (2003) surveyed over 3,000 singles aged 40–69, finding that almost half cited having someone to talk to or do things with as their most important reason for dating. Alterovitz and Mendelsohn (2013) found that romantic and sexual interests were mentioned at similar rates among middle-aged and young-old online daters, but less so among old-old individuals.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the Big Five personality traits characterized by intellectual curiosity, creativity, and receptivity to new ideas. Highly generative individuals scored high on this trait, reflecting the creative and idea-generating dimensions of generativity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Reinforcement or punishment may be used to either increase or decrease the probability that a behavior will occur again. Positive consequences make behavior more likely to repeat (reinforcement); negative consequences make it less likely. Also called instrumental conditioning. What counts as punishment or reward is individualized.

A form of sexual expression involving stimulation of the genitals with the mouth. It is one of several alternatives to vaginal intercourse that older couples may incorporate more frequently as age-related physical changes affect traditional sexual functioning.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD)
glossary.def: An intergovernmental economic organisation with 38 member countries, founded to stimulate economic progress and world trade. In the context of work at midlife, OECD data is cited to compare annual work hours across nations, showing that American workers work more hours per year than most European workers but fewer hours than Mexican workers.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A major branch of Judaism characterized by strict adherence to traditional Jewish law and practice. In Orthodox Judaism, a minyan of 10 men is required for communal prayer, which influences gender differences in worship attendance. Roughly 22% of Jewish adults in Israel self-identify as Orthodox.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A condition characterized by decreased bone mass and density, leading to increased bone fragility and fracture risk. During menopause, the decline in estrogen—which plays a key role in bone formation—accelerates bone loss and increases a woman's risk of developing osteoporosis.

U.S. federal and state regulations requiring employers to pay additional compensation (typically 1.5 times the regular rate) to employees who work beyond a standard number of hours. Not all employees are covered under these laws, which contributes to longer work hours without additional compensation for many American workers.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A key biological mechanism of primary aging involving harm to cells and tissues caused by free radicals — unstable molecules that react with and damage DNA, proteins, and lipids. Oxidative damage accumulates over time and contributes to cellular aging, organ dysfunction, and increased susceptibility to age-related diseases.

Employer-provided compensated time away from work. In the United States, unlike many other developed nations, there is no federal law mandating paid vacation time for workers in many job sectors. Higher-paying jobs and unionized positions are more likely to include paid vacation and holidays.

Specialized cells in the pancreas responsible for producing and secreting insulin. In Type 2 diabetes, as insulin resistance increases, beta cells are forced to produce more insulin to compensate, and over time may gradually lose this capacity. In some Type 2 diabetics, beta cells cease functioning entirely, making insulin injections necessary.

The duct through which pancreatic enzymes flow from the pancreas to the small intestine. If a gallstone becomes lodged at the junction with the bile duct, it can obstruct pancreatic secretion, leading to serious complications such as pancreatitis.

Researchers who conducted a comprehensive Pew Research survey on the sandwich generation, finding that 47% of middle-aged adults are part of this group, 15% provide financial support to an older parent while also supporting children, 48% have supported adult children in the past year, and 27% are the primary source of support for their grown children.

Developmental psychologist (with Staudinger and Lindenberger, 1999) who researched wisdom and lifespan development. Along with Scheibe and Kunzmann, contributed to understanding how crystallized intelligence and life experience contribute to wisdom in older adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Peer Relationships: Selection, Facilitation, Deviancy Training
glossary.def: PEER PRESSURE: encouragement to conform to friends in behavior, dress, and attitude. SELECTION: teenagers select friends whose values and interests they share, abandoning friends who follow other paths. FACILITATION: peers facilitate both destructive AND constructive behaviors — it is easier to do both the wrong thing and the right thing. DEVIANCY TRAINING: destructive peer support in which one person shows another how to rebel against authority or social norms. ROMANCE SEQUENCE: same-sex groups → loose mixed crowd → small mixed-sex groups → couples with private intimacies. Sexual orientation is fluid during the teen years.

The ability to quickly and accurately perceive and compare visual information. According to the Seattle Longitudinal Study, perceptual speed declines in middle and late adulthood, representing one area where younger adults outperform older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The transitional phase before menopause during which a woman's ovaries gradually stop releasing eggs and the production of estrogen and progesterone decreases. This period can begin 2 to 8 years before menopause and is characterized by irregular periods and menopausal symptoms such as hot flashes and sleep disturbances.

The transitional phase leading up to menopause during which hormonal fluctuations cause irregular menstrual cycles and various symptoms such as hot flashes, mood changes, and vaginal dryness. It can last several years before menopause is officially reached.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Hands-on physical assistance provided to aging parents, such as helping with bathing or getting dressed. In the United States, approximately 1 in 5 women report having provided personal care to aging parents in the past year, compared to 8% of men. In Germany, the figures are similar, but in Italy, men (25%) and women (26%) are equally likely to have provided personal care, reflecting cultural differences in caregiving norms.

A dimension of psychological well-being referring to an individual's sense of continued development, openness to new experiences, and realization of personal potential. Both spirituality and formal religious participation (among older adults) were associated with higher personal growth.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who studied the relationship between generativity and positive outcomes across adulthood. Their work demonstrated that generative adults possess good cultural knowledge and healthy adaptation to the world, and that women scoring high in generativity at age 52 were rated higher in positive personality characteristics, marital satisfaction, and successful aging at age 62.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An American nonpartisan think tank that informs the public about issues, attitudes, and trends shaping the world. In the midlife work context, Pew Research data from 2010 is cited to show that 55% of adults experienced workplace difficulties during the recent recession and that middle-aged adults (especially ages 50–64) reported being hit hardest by the economic downturn.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A nonpartisan American fact tank that conducts public opinion polling, demographic research, and surveys on a wide range of topics including religion. Their 2016 report analyzed religious beliefs and practices of men and women in 84 countries between 2008 and 2015.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A research organization whose findings documented the increasing prevalence of multigenerational households in the United States, a trend more historically associated with Latin American, Asian, and African family structures. This increase reflects broader demographic and economic shifts influencing American family dynamics.

A controllable lifestyle factor and contributor to secondary aging characterized by insufficient levels of physical exercise and movement. Physical inactivity in middle adulthood is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, obesity, diabetes, and accelerated physical decline. Getting out of shape during middle adulthood is largely attributed to reduced physical activity rather than inevitable aging.

A buildup of fat, cholesterol, calcium, and other blood substances on arterial walls that hardens over time and restricts blood flow. Relevant to erectile dysfunction as plaque restricts oxygenated blood flow to the penis. It is the pathological basis of atherosclerosis.

Two complementary aspects: (1) human traits can be molded — people can and do change; (2) people maintain a certain durability of identity. Dynamic systems approach: human development is an ongoing, ever-changing interaction between the physical and emotional being and every aspect of the environment.

Five types of social play in developmental sequence: SOLITARY play (child plays alone, unaware of others). ONLOOKER play (child watches other children play). PARALLEL play (children play with similar toys in similar ways but not together). ASSOCIATIVE play (children interact, share materials, observe each other, but play is not yet mutual and reciprocal). COOPERATIVE play (children play together, creating and elaborating a joint activity or taking turns). Also: ROUGH-AND-TUMBLE play — mimics aggression with no intent to harm; common among young males; may positively affect prefrontal cortex development. SOCIODRAMATIC play — enables children to explore social rules, practice emotional regulation, develop self-concept.

The experience of positive emotional states such as joy, enthusiasm, and contentment. Higher levels of spirituality have been associated with higher levels of positive affect according to the 2005 National Survey of Midlife in the United States.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The tendency of older adults to focus more on positive information and less on negative information compared to younger adults. This includes better memory for positive images and reduced amygdala response to negative stimuli.

A term used interchangeably with 'empty nest' to describe the phase of parenting after children have grown up and left the home. This period is associated with changes in marital satisfaction, personal identity, and emotional well-being, and has been studied primarily in American samples, with different outcomes observed in Chinese populations.

A clinical illness and form of stress response that can result from exposure to extremely traumatic or threatening events. Listed in the source as an example of stress manifesting as clinical illness, distinct from everyday stressors or major life events.

The stage of life following the permanent cessation of menstrual cycles. In Western cultural contexts, postmenopausal women may be perceived as having lost social value tied to reproductive ability, contributing to heightened anxiety and identity concerns associated with aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A condition in which blood glucose levels are higher than normal but not yet high enough to be diagnosed as Type 2 diabetes. Approximately 1 in 3 American adults has prediabetes, and 9 in 10 of them are unaware. Without intervention, 15–30% of people with prediabetes will develop Type 2 diabetes within 5 years.

Between ages 2 and 6, neurological increases are especially notable in areas of the cortex where planning, thinking, social awareness, and language occur. Maturation gradually facilitates: focused attention, reduced impulsiveness, and less perseveration. PERSEVERATION: a child persists in or sticks to one thought or action, unable to quit — an opposite manifestation to impulsiveness, both caused by prefrontal immaturity. Neurological immaturity also underlies why adults must prevent childhood injury.

A condition in which blood pressure is elevated above normal but not yet at hypertensive levels. Defined as systolic pressure of 120–139 mmHg or diastolic pressure of 80–89 mmHg. Individuals with prehypertension are considered at risk for developing hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

Piaget's second stage of cognitive development. Major accomplishment: SYMBOLIC THOUGHT — understanding that words can refer to things not seen and that an item can symbolize something else. Characteristics that obstruct logic: ANIMISM (belief that natural objects are alive), CENTRATION (focusing on one idea, excluding others), EGOCENTRISM (seeing world only from own perspective — not selfishness but inability to take another's viewpoint), FOCUS ON APPEARANCE (ignoring non-apparent attributes), STATIC REASONING (nothing changes), IRREVERSIBILITY (nothing can be undone), and CONSERVATION FAILURES (the amount of a substance remains the same when its appearance changes — not yet understood).

Age-related hearing loss that accumulates gradually over time, often resulting from years of exposure to loud noise, biological aging of the auditory system, or both. It commonly includes difficulty hearing high-frequency sounds and understanding speech in noisy environments. It is more common and progresses more noticeably in men than women, partly because men are more likely to work in noisy occupational environments.

An age-related vision condition derived from the Latin for 'old vision,' characterized by a progressive loss of elasticity in the lens of the eye that makes it increasingly difficult to focus on nearby objects. It results from weakening of the tiny ciliary muscles surrounding the lens, which can no longer adequately bend the lens to focus light on the retina. Anyone over the age of 35 is at risk. Symptoms include difficulty reading small print, needing to hold reading material farther away than arm's length, headaches, and eyestrain.

Healthcare practices aimed at preventing disease or detecting it early before symptoms become severe, rather than treating conditions after they develop. Exercise is considered a form of preventative health care. Midlife adults who engage in challenging physical and mental activity, monitor nutrition, get adequate sleep, and use social resources are most likely to maintain good health.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Age-related physical changes that result from biological factors beyond individual control, including molecular and cellular changes and oxidative damage. Coined by Busse (1969), primary aging is universal and inevitable, occurring regardless of lifestyle choices. Examples include cellular senescence, collagen changes in skin, and age-related declines in organ function.

A set of basic cognitive capacities identified in research, including verbal meaning, spatial orientation, inductive reasoning, number ability, and word fluency. Gradual decline in these abilities is normal in late adulthood, typically beginning around age 60 according to the Seattle Longitudinal Study.

Audible speech directed at oneself (common in children ages 3+) that serves an intellectual rather than communicative purpose — guiding thinking and behavior. Vygotsky viewed it as the transition point between social speech and inner speech. It is more similar in function to inner speech than to social speech.

The ability of middle-aged adults to draw on their accumulated store of knowledge and experience to recognize patterns in new problems, allowing them to ignore irrelevant details and focus on the most important aspects of an issue, contributing to career peak performance during this stage.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A coping strategy that involves actively addressing the stressor itself in an effort to solve the problem at hand. For example, studying extra hours before an important exam. Research suggests problem-focused coping has the greatest impact on long-term mental wellness, particularly when the stressor is controllable.

The rate at which the brain can perceive and respond to information. Processing speed declines with age, as evidenced by the Seattle Longitudinal Study and research on aging pilots, though older individuals may compensate with greater experience and knowledge.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the three components of Erikson's concept of generativity. It refers to the biological and social act of having and raising children, which Erikson identified as the primary generative type. Parenthood represents the most direct expression of procreativity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the three components of generativity identified by Erikson, referring to contributions made through work, career, and professional achievements. It involves creating goods, services, or accomplishments that benefit others and leave a positive legacy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The efficiency and output level of an employee in the workplace. Studies show that taking leisure time away from work can enhance productivity when the employee returns, as it allows for psychological restoration (Kühnel & Sonnentag, 2011).

A female sex hormone produced by the ovaries that works alongside estrogen in regulating the menstrual cycle and reproductive system. Declining progesterone levels during perimenopause and menopause contribute to hormonal symptoms and menstrual irregularity.

A normal biological process by which the body eliminates unneeded or damaged cells in a controlled manner. Cancer cells are able to ignore the signals that initiate programmed cell death, allowing them to survive and multiply indefinitely, contributing to tumor growth and progression.

An organization that tracks American workers' vacation habits. According to their 2016 report, 55% of U.S. workers in 2015 did not use all of their paid vacation and holiday leave, resulting in a total loss of 658 million vacation days, or an average of 2 vacation days per worker.

The ability to remember to perform a planned action or task at some point in the future, as well as the ability to quickly shift mentally among tasks. Prospective memory fades notably with age but may be aided through establishing routines.

A condition common in middle-aged and older men in which the prostate gland increases in size, potentially interfering with urination and urinary flow. It is a distinct condition from prostate cancer and becomes increasingly prevalent with age.

PROXIMAL PARENTING: caregiving practices involving physical closeness — frequent holding and touching. Tends to produce toddlers who are less self-aware but more compliant. DISTAL PARENTING: caregiving at a distance — toys, food, and face-to-face communication with minimal holding. Tends to produce children who are more self-aware but less obedient. Notable cultural differences exist. No culture encourages indifference to infant emotions.

Proposes that irrational, unconscious drives and motives, often originating in childhood, underlie human behavior. Five psychosexual stages; early conflict resolution determines personality patterns. Personality has three parts: id (instinctual drives), ego (rational mediator), and superego (internalized moral standards).

A multidimensional construct encompassing positive affect, personal growth, purpose in life, positive relationships with others, self-acceptance, environmental mastery, and autonomy. Research shows spirituality is strongly related to psychological well-being irrespective of religious participation (Vaillant, 2008).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The socio-emotional changes and challenges faced during middle age (approximately 40–65 years), including shifts in self-perception, relationships with children and aging parents, marital changes, grandparenting, friendship, spirituality, and Erikson's stage of generativity vs. stagnation.

Physical symptoms that are influenced or caused by mental or emotional factors. In the context of menopause, white women were more likely than women of other ethnic groups in the SWAN study to report psychosomatic symptoms such as depression, irritability, forgetfulness, and headaches.

glossary.term: Puberty: Menarche, Spermarche, and the HPG Axis
glossary.def: PUBERTY: time between the first onrush of hormones and full adult physical development; usually lasting 3–5 years. MENARCHE: a girl's first menstrual period, signaling she has begun ovulation; pregnancy is biologically possible but ovulation is often irregular for years after. SPERMARCHE: a boy's first ejaculation of sperm, signaling sperm production. THE HPG AXIS: hormonal signal chain — HYPOTHALAMUS signals PITUITARY, which signals ADRENAL GLANDS and GONADS (ovaries or testes) to produce more hormones. ESTRADIOL: chief estrogen, produced more by females. TESTOSTERONE: chief androgen, produced far more by males. Both sexes have some of each.

The ability of the pupil to constrict and dilate in response to changes in light levels. With aging, the pupil loses some of its range of motion and speed of response, reducing the amount of light entering the eye in dim conditions and increasing glare sensitivity in bright conditions.

A dimension of psychological well-being referring to an individual's sense that their life has meaning and direction. Both spirituality and formal religious participation were associated with higher levels of purpose in life, although religious participation's association was primarily observed in older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The medical term for heartburn or acid indigestion. It refers to the burning sensation in the chest or throat caused by stomach acid refluxing into the esophagus.

A broad measure of an individual's overall well-being and satisfaction with life, encompassing physical, psychological, and social dimensions. Spirituality has been shown to be positively associated with quality of life at both individual and societal levels (Sawatzky et al., 2005).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Racial and Ethnic Differences in Aging Attitudes
glossary.def: Research finding (Schuler et al., 2008) that compared to white women, Black and Latina women possess fewer negative stereotypes about aging. These differences suggest that cultural background and community values significantly mediate how women experience and perceive the aging process.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who reported that many nations beyond the United States provide additional mandated time off for specific worker groups, including young workers, older workers, and shift workers, highlighting the disparity in leisure protections internationally.

A central feature of Levinson's midlife transition stage (ages 40-45) in which individuals critically examine their previous life choices, relationships, and career decisions, and consider whether dramatic changes are necessary to align their lives with revised values and aspirations.

Carbohydrates that have been processed to remove fiber and nutrients, such as white bread and white rice. Diets high in refined carbohydrates are a risk factor for gallstone formation, likely due to their effects on cholesterol and bile composition.

Stress-reduction interventions including breathing exercises, muscle relaxation, and mental imagery designed to reduce physiological arousal caused by stress. These methods are used as part of stress management programs, particularly for individuals whose stressors (e.g., finances, job) are difficult to change directly.

The role of faith, spirituality, and religious practice in the lives of middle-aged adults. Religion often becomes more salient during midlife as individuals grapple with mortality, meaning, and purpose, and may serve as a source of coping and social support.

Engaging with a formal religious group's doctrines, values, traditions, and co-members. It involves participation in organized religious institutions and adherence to their established beliefs and practices, as defined by Greenfield, Vaillant, and Marks (2009).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A marriage in which one or both partners have been previously married. In 2013, the remarriage rate was approximately 28 per 1,000 adults aged 18 and older, representing a 44% decline since 1990. Men remarry at higher rates than women at every age group starting at 25, though this gender gap has narrowed over time.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The degree to which remarriages are happy and stable. Reviews are mixed (Hetherington & Kelly, 2002); some remarriages are more successful, particularly when divorce motivated self-improvement. However, many divorced adults end up in similar relationship patterns in subsequent marriages. Remarriages face unique stressors including unclear family roles, stepparenting challenges, and a higher divorce rate than first marriages.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of three grandparenting styles identified by Cherlin and Furstenberg (1986). Approximately 30% of grandparents fall into this category. These grandparents rarely see their grandchildren, typically due to geographic distance or an emotionally distant relationship. Contact is usually limited to special occasions such as holidays or birthdays.

The process by which a divorced or widowed individual forms a new romantic partnership, which may or may not lead to remarriage or cohabitation. Factors influencing repartnering include the presence of children, the age and temperament of children, concerns about ex-spouse reactions, and whether the parent is adult-focused or child-focused.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The process of forming new romantic relationships following marital dissolution. Anderson et al. (2004) studied transitions into dating among divorced parents, and Anderson & Greene (2011) examined the challenges of balancing adult and child concerns in repartnering.

Repetition of a study using different participants to verify findings. Built into the scientific method as a critical step before conclusions are widely accepted. Without replication, a single study's results may reflect bias or error.

The traditional societal measure of women's worth based on their capacity to bear children. Because women have historically been valued for reproductive capabilities, postmenopausal women may be considered socially 'old,' contributing to the double standard of aging and increased anxiety in midlife women.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The capacity to adapt well to significant adversity and overcome serious stress. Three important principles: (1) Resilience is DYNAMIC — a person may be resilient at some periods but not others. (2) Resilience is a POSITIVE ADAPTATION to stress — if rejection by a parent leads a child to establish a closer relationship with another adult, that child is resilient. (3) ADVERSITY must be significant — resilient children overcome conditions that overwhelm many peers. CUMULATIVE STRESS: accumulated stresses over time, including minor ones, are MORE devastating than an isolated major stress. COGNITIVE COPING: a child's interpretation of a family situation impacts how it affects them.

The light-sensitive layer of tissue lining the inner surface of the eye. It contains photoreceptor cells (rods and cones) that convert light into neural signals sent to the brain. The retina is central to both scotopic (low-light) and photopic (bright-light) vision, and age-related changes reduce the amount of light reaching it.

A serious eye condition in which the retina pulls away or tears from its underlying supportive tissue. It can be indicated by sudden onset of floaters or flashes of light and requires prompt medical attention to prevent vision loss. Risk factors include age, high myopia, diabetes, and cataract surgery.

The accumulated savings and investments that individuals build throughout their working lives to fund their retirement. Middle-aged adults were particularly vulnerable during economic recessions because their retirement savings were significantly depleted as financial markets declined and housing values dropped simultaneously.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A chronic inflammatory autoimmune disorder primarily affecting the joints, causing pain, swelling, and potential joint damage. Crowson et al. (2011) documented lifetime risk in adults, distinguishing it from osteoarthritis which is degenerative rather than autoimmune.

The bony structure surrounding and protecting the lungs. With age, thinning of the bones can change the shape of the rib cage, reducing its ability to expand fully during breathing and thereby contributing to decreased lung capacity.

A theoretical perspective predicting that when individuals lose an important social role in their lives, they experience a decrease in emotional well-being. When applied to the empty nest, this hypothesis suggests that parents will suffer emotionally as they lose their primary caregiving role. This perspective underpins the concept of empty nest syndrome.

A theoretical perspective suggesting that the empty nest period should lead to more positive changes for parents because the stresses and responsibilities of raising children have been lifted. Supported by multiple studies reviewed by Bouchard (2013), this hypothesis is consistent with findings that marital satisfaction often increases during and after the launching phase of the empty nest.

Thousands of children adopted from severely deprived Romanian orphanages in the early 1990s. Key findings: adopted before 6 months — most developed normally; adopted after age 1 — long-term cognitive and emotional deficits in many; as adults, most had emotional and/or conduct problems. Conclusion: early ENVIRONMENT, not genetics, was the main problem. Confirms sensitive periods for social-emotional development and the experience-expectant nature of synchrony and attachment.

Sexual practices that reduce the risk of transmitting or acquiring sexually transmitted infections, including consistent and correct condom use, limiting sexual partners, and regular STI testing. Safe sex is important at any age but is frequently neglected by middle-aged and older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The number of participants included in a research study. Levinson's study was criticized for its small sample size of only 40 men, which limits the generalizability of findings to broader populations.

Researchers who studied boomerang kids and found that some return home due to emotional distress and mental health issues, in addition to financial reasons. They also found that young adults who return are more likely to have had good prior relationships with their parents, which may reduce interpersonal stress in the household.

Adults who have at least one parent age 65 or older and are either raising their own children or providing financial and emotional support for their grown children. According to a 2013 Pew Research survey, 47% of middle-aged adults fall into this category. These individuals often experience greater time pressure and financial strain than those not in this position, though they report similar levels of happiness.

The progressive loss of muscle mass and strength that occurs with aging, first described by Morley and colleagues (2001). It is considered a significant factor in frailty and functional impairment in older adults. Causes include the decline of growth and anabolic hormones (especially testosterone) and decreased physical activity. It can begin as early as age 40 and is associated with decreased quality of life, increased healthcare costs, and early death.

A type of dietary fat found in animal products and some plant oils that is solid at room temperature. Dietary guidelines recommend adults consume less than 10% of daily calories from saturated fat, while the American Heart Association recommends 5%–6% of calories. In the average American diet, 15% of calories come from saturated fat, well above recommended levels. High intake is linked to heart disease, stroke, and elevated cholesterol.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Dietary fats found primarily in animal products that are solid at room temperature and associated with increased LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular disease risk. The American Heart Association (2016) recommends limiting saturated fat intake.

Researchers who found that spirituality was related to a higher quality of life for both individuals and societies, supporting the broader health benefits of spiritual orientation beyond formal religious participation.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The temporary, calibrated support provided by a more knowledgeable other to help a learner complete tasks within their ZPD. Introduced by Wood, Bruner & Ross (1976) — Vygotsky himself never used the term. Effective scaffolding is responsive and gradually faded as the learner gains competence, ultimately transferring full responsibility to the learner.

Researchers who found that compared to white women, Black and Latina women possess fewer stereotypes about aging, highlighting the role of racial and ethnic background in mediating attitudes toward the aging process for women.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Seeks to understand how and why people of all ages and circumstances change or remain the same over time. The key word is change — development is a dynamic process throughout the entire lifespan, though the rate and nature of change varies.

A way to answer questions using empirical research and data-based conclusions. Five steps: (1) begin with curiosity, (2) develop a hypothesis, (3) test the hypothesis, (4) draw conclusions, (5) report the results. A sixth crucial step — replication — is often required before the scientific community accepts conclusions. Empirical means based on observations, repeated experiences, and verifiable experiments, not theoretical.

The visual system's ability to see in dim or low-light conditions. This sensitivity declines with age; by age 60, the retina receives only one-third as much light as it did at age 20, making tasks in dimmer environments increasingly difficult. This decline is due to changes in the pupil's ability to dilate and reduced retinal sensitivity.

Vision that occurs under low-light or nighttime conditions, primarily mediated by rod photoreceptor cells in the retina. Scotopic sensitivity declines with age, and by age 60 the retina receives only one-third the light it received at age 20, impairing the ability to function effectively in dim environments.

A landmark long-term research project begun by K. Warner Schaie in 1956 that tracks cognitive abilities across the lifespan using a cohort-sequential design. It found that many cognitive abilities peak in middle adulthood and some, especially crystallized abilities, remain stable well into old age.

A landmark longitudinal study of adult cognitive abilities initiated in 1956 by K. Warner Schaie. Approximately 6,000 participants have been included, with evaluations conducted every seven years. Results show that middle-aged adults perform better on four of six cognitive tasks than they did as young adults, including verbal memory, spatial skills, inductive reasoning, and vocabulary, while numerical computation and perceptual speed decline in middle and late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A long-term research project that measured cognitive output across adulthood using standardized tests of five primary mental abilities. The study found measurable decline beginning around age 60, though tests were normed and validated on younger adult output, raising questions about ecological validity.

Age-related physical changes that result from controllable, lifestyle-related factors rather than inevitable biological processes. Coined by Busse (1969), secondary aging includes changes caused by an unhealthy lifestyle such as lack of physical exercise, poor diet, smoking, alcohol use, chronic stress, and chronic diseases like diabetes or arthritis. Unlike primary aging, secondary aging can often be prevented or mitigated through behavioral changes.

Each generation has experienced puberty a few weeks earlier, and has grown a centimeter or so taller, than the preceding one — due to improved nutrition and medical care. The secular trend has stopped in developed nations. STRESS causes earlier puberty in children genetically sensitive to context. EARLY-MATURING GIRLS: tend to have lower self-esteem, more depression, poorer body image, may be attracted to older boyfriends and enter abusive relationships. EARLY-MATURING BOYS: more aggressive, law-breaking, and alcohol-abusing than later-maturing boys. Body fat proportion correlates with earlier puberty onset.

A lifestyle that includes only the physical activity required for independent living, with no additional deliberate exercise. Sedentary individuals require fewer calories and are at greater risk for obesity, cardiovascular disease, and metabolic disorders. Dietary guidelines state that adults should avoid being inactive, noting that any activity will result in some health benefits.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A methodological concern raised by Umberson et al. (2005) suggesting that the apparent increase in marital satisfaction in later marriages may be artificial. The argument is that couples who were unhappy have likely already divorced, leaving only relatively satisfied couples to be studied, making late-life marriages appear more satisfying than they actually are.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The concept that everyone develops some degree of specialized knowledge and skill in areas that are personally meaningful to them, such as baking, quilting, computer programming, or diagnosing illness, regardless of formal training.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A dimension of generativity concerned with the individual's own continued identity development during middle adulthood. It reflects the idea that generativity includes personal growth and further refinement of the self, not only contributions to others.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Knowledge of facts, concepts, and meanings of words stored in long-term memory, independent of personal experience. Older adults demonstrate greater semantic knowledge as a component of crystallized intelligence, which contributes to superior performance on vocabulary and knowledge-based tasks.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The biological process of aging that results in gradual deterioration of bodily functions. In the brain, senescence reduces production of neurotransmitters such as glutamate, acetylcholine, serotonin, and especially dopamine; decreases neural fluid; thins myelination; and slows cerebral blood circulation.

glossary.term: Sensation vs. Perception and Sensory Development
glossary.def: SENSATION: the response of a sensory system when it detects a stimulus. PERCEPTION: mental processing of sensory information when the brain interprets a sensation. Sensory development typically precedes intellectual and motor development. HEARING: most developed at birth; speech perception by 4 months. VISION: least mature at birth; newborns focus 4-30 inches; binocular vision at 3 months. Smell and taste function at birth. Touch is acute in infants.

A psychological feeling identified by Levinson as characteristic of the midlife transition, in which individuals become acutely aware of the passage of time, recognize that life is finite, and feel compelled to act on long-deferred goals and aspirations rather than continue to postpone them.
A time when a certain type of development is most likely to happen or happens most easily, although it may still happen later with more difficulty. Less absolute than a critical period.

Piaget's term for the way infants think — by using their senses and motor skills — during the first period of cognitive development (birth to 2 years). Key principles: infants are active learners; adaptation is the core of intelligence. The sensorimotor period has six sub-stages progressing from reflexes to mental combinations.

Age-related alterations in the functioning of the sensory systems — vision, hearing, taste, smell, and touch — that typically begin to become noticeable during the middle adulthood years. These changes may include the onset of presbyopia (farsightedness), presbycusis (hearing loss), and reduced sensitivity in other sensory modalities.

glossary.term: Sex vs. Gender and Gender Development Theories
glossary.def: SEX DIFFERENCES: biological differences between males and females in organs, hormones, and body type. GENDER DIFFERENCES: differences in roles and behaviors of males and females — culturally prescribed. By age 2, children apply gender labels; by age 4, children believe certain toys are appropriate for one gender. FIVE THEORIES: (1) Psychoanalytic — phallic stage, Oedipus complex, identification; (2) Behaviorism — gender is learned through reinforcement of gender-appropriate behavior; (3) Cognitive — gender schema: child's cognitive concept of sex differences based on observations; (4) Sociocultural — cultural values and customs; children are 'gender detectives' by age 6; (5) Evolutionary — sexual attraction is crucial for reproduction. GENDER SCHEMA: a child's cognitive concept or general belief about sex differences.

The motivational or drive component of sexuality referring to an interest in sexual activity. In women, decreased sexual desire is a commonly reported change associated with menopausal hormonal shifts, including declining estrogen and testosterone levels.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The physiological and psychological capacity to engage in and enjoy sexual activity. Age-related changes such as hormonal decline, reduced lubrication, erectile difficulties, and medical conditions (e.g., diabetes, heart disease) can all affect sexual functioning in middle and older adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Research finding (Slevin, 2010) indicating that lesbian women tend to be more positive about aging and looking older compared to heterosexual women. This may be due to different community norms and values regarding physical appearance and the reduced pressure to conform to mainstream standards of youthful beauty.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The sexual functioning, behavior, attitudes, and intimate relationships of individuals during middle adulthood. While hormonal changes associated with the climacteric may affect sexual function, many middle adults maintain active and satisfying sexual lives. Factors such as relationship quality, health status, and psychological well-being play important roles.

Infections transmitted through sexual contact, including genital herpes, chlamydia, genital warts, HIV, gonorrhea, and syphilis. Older adults remain at risk for STIs despite lower rates of condom use, and STI rates in adults over 50 have been increasing.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A life-threatening injury occurring when an infant is forcefully shaken back and forth, rupturing blood vessels in the brain and breaking neural connections. Infants have an inborn drive to remedy deficits (self-righting), but it is the patterns — not the moments — of neglect or maltreatment that harm the brain.

A relationship status in which an individual is not married or in a formal partnership. At midlife, singlehood can result from never having married, divorce, or widowhood. According to Pew Research (Wang & Parker, 2014), 16 per 1,000 adults aged 45–54 have never married, and 7 per 1,000 adults aged 55 and older have never married in the U.S.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The state of being unmarried during middle adulthood. This can result from never marrying, divorce, or widowhood. The experience of singlehood at midlife is shaped by social support networks, financial independence, and personal identity.

Stage 1 (0-1 mo): reflexes only. Stage 2 (1-4 mo): first habits — primary circular reactions using infant's own body; behavior becomes purposeful. Stage 3 (4-8 mo): secondary circular reactions involving objects and people; makes interesting things last. Stage 4 (6-12 mo): means-to-an-end and anticipation; begins searching for hidden objects. Stage 5 (12-18 mo): tertiary circular reactions; active exploration — the little scientist. Stage 6 (18-24 mo): mental combinations; can think through consequences before acting.

An age-related process in which the skin becomes progressively thinner due to loss of the underlying fat layer and reduced collagen production. Thinner skin allows blood vessels to become more visible and makes the face appear less smooth and more prone to wrinkling and drooping.

Research by Dew et al. (2003) and Ferrie et al. (2007) demonstrated that sleep quality and duration in older adults predict all-cause mortality, with both short and long sleep durations associated with increased risk.

A sleep disorder characterized by repeated interruptions in breathing during sleep. It is the most commonly diagnosed sleep disorder among Americans, accounting for 71% of those diagnosed with a sleep disorder according to the National Sleep Foundation (2015).

The cumulative deficit of sleep an individual accumulates when they consistently sleep less than the recommended amount. For example, those with chronic pain experience an average of 42 minutes of sleep debt per night, while those with acute pain experience 14 minutes.

A condition resulting from insufficient quantity or quality of sleep, which has significant consequences for physical health, cognitive functioning, emotional regulation, and immune function. In middle adulthood, sleep patterns often change and sleep deprivation becomes a notable health concern with implications for overall well-being and longevity.

A condition in which an individual consistently receives less sleep than needed for optimal health and functioning. Chronic sleep deprivation (less than 7 hours per night for adults) is associated with suppressed immune function, obesity, memory impairment, hypertension, increased cancer risk, heart disease, and type 2 diabetes.

The amount of time it takes an individual to fall asleep after going to bed. Those in midlife with lower life satisfaction experience greater delays in sleep onset, which may be driven by worry and anxiety.

A researcher who documented that aging in Western society is more stressful for women than men due to cultural emphasis on youthful beauty and attractiveness. Slevin also found that lesbian women are more positive about aging and looking older than heterosexual women, suggesting sexual orientation moderates the experience of aging-related stress.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The use of tobacco products, recognized as a risk factor for heartburn and GERD. Smoking can weaken the lower esophageal sphincter and reduce saliva production, which normally helps neutralize acid in the esophagus.
A pejorative term for parents who try to remove any impediments in the way of their children, implying that they do not allow children to learn how to overcome obstacles on their own.

An idea based on shared perceptions, not on objective reality. Many age-related terms such as childhood, adolescence, yuppie, and senior citizen are social constructions that vary across cultures and historical periods.

A concept describing the number and variety of social roles a person occupies (e.g., daughter, team member, volunteer, coworker, student). Greater social integration is associated with better health outcomes, as multiple social roles provide encouragement to maintain healthy lifestyles and access to social support during stress.

The state of lacking social relationships or integration. Research has shown that the impact of social isolation on disease risk and mortality is similar in magnitude to the risk associated with regular smoking, underscoring the critical importance of social relationships to physical health.

Extension of behaviorism emphasizing the influence other people have over behavior. Learning occurs through observational learning or modeling — even without specific reinforcement, individuals learn by observing and imitating others.

Seeking emotional responses or information from other people. Infants observe someone else's expressions and reactions and use that person as a social reference to guide their own behavior. Mothers do more caregiving and comforting. Fathers elicit more smiles and laughter, engage in more intensive play, and teach appropriate emotional expressions.

The relative rank, prestige, or standing of an individual within a social hierarchy. Attitudes about aging and social status for women vary significantly by culture; for example, aging Asian women may attain greater respect and authority within the household, in contrast to Western norms that may diminish older women's perceived value.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Assistance provided by members of one's social network, particularly during times of stress. Can take three primary forms: emotional support (e.g., a hug), tangible support (e.g., lending money), and informational support (e.g., advice). Social support helps improve health behaviors, reduces stress, and can help people with serious illnesses survive longer.

The perception or experience of being cared for, valued, and part of a social network. Cohen & Wills (1985) proposed the buffering hypothesis, suggesting that social support protects against the negative effects of stress on health and wellbeing.

The community, social support, and interpersonal connections provided through religious participation. Research suggests women may benefit more from these aspects because social relationships more strongly influence women's mental health, contributing to higher rates of female religious participation.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Vygotsky's theory asserting that cognitive development is fundamentally shaped by social interactions and cultural context. Learning is a shared activity that occurs first between people (interpsychologically) before becoming internalized as individual thought (intrapsychologically). Higher mental functions are not innate but develop through participation in culturally meaningful activities.

Human development results from the dynamic interaction between developing persons and their surrounding society. Culture is integral to development every day. Key concepts: apprenticeship in thinking, guided participation, and zone of proximal development (ZPD). Learning opportunities can be provided by many different mentors, not just trained educators.
A person's position in society as determined by income, wealth, occupation, education, and place of residence. A pervasive context that affects development across all domains.

A dietary mineral found abundantly in processed foods. Dietary guidelines recommend adults consume less than 2,300 mg (approximately 1 teaspoon) per day. The American Heart Association reports the average American intake is 3,440 mg per day. High sodium intake is correlated with increased blood pressure, and reduction in sodium leads to corresponding drops in blood pressure. Adults with high blood pressure are encouraged to limit intake to 1,500 mg per day.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A grandparent who serves as the primary or exclusive caregiver for one or more grandchildren, often stepping in during family crises such as parental illness, substance abuse, incarceration, or death. This arrangement represents a growing trend and has led many grandparents to redefine their family roles with fewer adopting a formal, traditional grandparenting stance.

Researchers who studied grandfathers' relationships with their grandchildren, finding that: (1) grandfathers used technology such as texting, emailing, and Skype to maintain contact with distant grandchildren; (2) early grandparent research had routinely overlooked grandfathers; and (3) many grandfathers found the grandfather role easier, more enjoyable, and more emotionally expressive than their earlier experience of fatherhood.

A memory impairment in which a person cannot remember where or when they originally learned a piece of information. In older adults, source amnesia may contribute to less critical analysis of information because they cannot recall the origin or context of a fact.

Cognitive abilities related to perceiving, manipulating, and reasoning about visual and spatial information. The Seattle Longitudinal Study found that spatial skills increase with age until one's 70s, making it one of the cognitive domains that improves through middle adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: LEAST RESTRICTIVE ENVIRONMENT (LRE): legal requirement that children with special needs be assigned to the most general educational context in which they can be expected to learn. RESPONSE TO INTERVENTION (RTI): educational strategy using early intervention to help children demonstrating below-average achievement; only children not helped are designated for more intensive measures. INDIVIDUAL EDUCATION PLAN (IEP): a document that specifies educational goals and plans for a child with special needs.
