flashcards.back: Procreativity (having and raising children), Productivity (work and career contributions), and Creativity (generating new ideas and products). Erikson also described a fourth dimension: self-generation, concerned with further identity development.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Which stage in Erikson's theory is the longest, and why?
flashcards.back: Erikson believed Stage 7, Generativity vs. Stagnation, was the longest stage because it encompasses the entire period of middle adulthood, during which individuals establish and maintain a family, build a career, and contribute to the community.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What virtue is associated with the successful resolution of Erikson's Stage 7?
flashcards.back: The virtue of Care. Erikson defined it as the obligation to 'take care of the persons, the products, and the ideas one has learned to care for' (Erikson, 1982, p. 67).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: According to Erikson, what two earlier stages must be resolved before generativity can be fully achieved?
flashcards.back: Identity (Stage 5: Identity vs. Role Confusion) and Intimacy (Stage 6: Intimacy vs. Isolation) must be successfully resolved before an individual can fully achieve generativity, according to Peterson & Duncan (2007) citing Erikson.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What personality profile do highly generative individuals show on the Big Five?
flashcards.back: Highly generative adults score HIGH on conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, and openness to experience, and LOW on neuroticism (de St. Aubin & McAdams, 1995; Peterson, Smirles & Wentworth, 1997).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What long-term cognitive and emotional outcomes were associated with high generativity in men at midlife?
flashcards.back: Men rated higher in generativity at midlife showed stronger global cognitive functioning (memory, attention, calculation), stronger executive functioning (response inhibition, abstract thinking, cognitive flexibility), and lower levels of depression in late adulthood (Malone et al., 2016).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What outcomes were associated with high generativity in women at age 52, as measured at age 62?
flashcards.back: Women scoring high in generativity at age 52 were rated high in positive personality characteristics, satisfaction with marriage and motherhood, and successful aging at age 62 (Peterson & Duncan, 2007).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the primary generative type according to Erikson (1950)?
flashcards.back: Parenthood is identified by Erikson as the primary generative type, as it directly involves bringing new beings into the world and nurturing the next generation.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How can stagnation serve a positive developmental function?
flashcards.back: Although stagnation represents a failure to engage in generative activities, Erikson noted that the sense of stagnation can motivate a person to redirect their energies into more meaningful and generative activities, serving as a catalyst for positive change.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is self-generation in the context of Erikson's generativity stage?
flashcards.back: Self-generation refers to the continued development of one's own identity during middle adulthood. It is a dimension of generativity that focuses on personal growth rather than contributions to others, emphasizing that generativity includes the self, not just others.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Erikson believe about the relationship between work and family obligations during middle adulthood?
flashcards.back: Erikson understood that work and family relationships may be in conflict due to their competing obligations and responsibilities. However, he believed that overall, middle adulthood was a positive developmental time despite these tensions.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are examples of executive functioning abilities, and how are they connected to generativity research?
flashcards.back: Executive functioning includes response inhibition, abstract thinking, and cognitive flexibility. Malone et al. (2016) found that men with higher midlife generativity demonstrated stronger executive functioning in late adulthood, suggesting that generative engagement may protect or promote higher-order cognitive processes.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Erikson warn about fully withdrawing from generative tasks in late adulthood?
flashcards.back: Erikson warned that while it is natural to withdraw somewhat from generative responsibilities as one ages, fully withdrawing from all generative tasks could result in stagnation and a feeling of not being needed or challenged, which is detrimental to well-being.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does Erikson's concept of generativity relate to the community beyond family and work?
flashcards.back: In addition to family (parenthood) and career (productivity), Erikson described community involvement as a third arena of generativity during middle adulthood. Engaging with the broader community represents an extended expression of care and contribution to future generations.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why are the strengths from the six earlier Eriksonian stages considered essential for generativity?
flashcards.back: Erikson believed that the psychological strengths (virtues) accumulated from successfully resolving the first six stages provide the foundational capacities needed to take on the generational task of cultivating strength in the next generation during Stage 7.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What does the research by Peterson & Duncan (2007) reveal about generative adults in general?
flashcards.back: Peterson & Duncan (2007) demonstrated that generative adults possess many positive characteristics, including good cultural knowledge and healthy adaptation to the world, suggesting that generativity is broadly associated with psychological and social well-being.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Compare generativity and stagnation as outcomes of Erikson's Stage 7.
flashcards.back: Generativity is achieved when an individual actively contributes to the next generation through family, work, and community, resulting in the virtue of Care. Stagnation occurs when one is not active in generative matters, leading to self-absorption and a sense of personal impoverishment, though it can motivate redirection toward meaningful activities.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What percentage of middle-aged adults are part of the sandwich generation according to the 2013 Pew Research survey?
flashcards.back: 47% of middle-aged adults are part of the sandwich generation. Additionally, 15% are providing financial support to an older parent while also raising or supporting their own children, and 48% have supported their adult children in the past year.

flashcards.front: What is a kinkeeper and what gender is most likely to fill this role?
flashcards.back: A kinkeeper is the family member who maintains family connections, organizes gatherings, preserves family history, provides support, and promotes solidarity. 86% of kinkeepers are women (Leach & Braithwaite, 1996). Mothers, maternal grandmothers, and paternal grandmothers are more likely to be kinkeepers than fathers, young adult children, and grandfathers combined.

flashcards.front: How many hours per month do kinkeepers spend on kinkeeping activities, and what are the mental health consequences?
flashcards.back: Kinkeepers spend approximately 34 hours per month (nearly a full work week) on kinkeeping activities. The more activities taken on and the more kin helped, the greater the stress and higher the levels of depression experienced. This is especially true when caregiving tasks (transportation, meals, repairs) are included.

flashcards.front: Compare and contrast the role loss hypothesis and the role strain relief hypothesis regarding the empty nest.
flashcards.back: Role loss hypothesis: losing the parenting role leads to decreased emotional well-being; underlies empty nest syndrome. Role strain relief hypothesis: the empty nest leads to positive changes because the stress of raising children is lifted. Research by Bouchard (2013) found stronger support for the role strain relief hypothesis, with few parents reporting significant loneliness after children leave.

flashcards.front: What does research consistently show about marital satisfaction during the empty nest period?
flashcards.back: Research consistently shows that raising children negatively impacts marital relationship quality. Marital satisfaction often increases during the launching phase of the empty nest and this increased satisfaction endures long after the last child has left home (Gorchoff, John, & Helson, 2008), supporting the role strain relief hypothesis.

flashcards.front: How do Chinese empty-nesters differ from American empty-nesters in their emotional response, and why?
flashcards.back: Chinese empty-nesters, especially in rural areas, report greater loneliness and depression than those with children still at home, unlike American samples where most parents adapt well. This is attributed to cultural traditions of family support for the elderly, loss of income when children move to cities, and practical hardships. Urban Chinese empty-nesters did not report the same level of distress.

flashcards.front: What are boomerang kids, and what are the primary reasons they return home?
flashcards.back: Boomerang kids are young adults who return to live with their parents after having previously lived independently. Primary reasons include financial hardship (the most common reason) and emotional distress or mental health issues. Those who return are more likely to have had strong prior relationships with their parents.

flashcards.front: What financial differences exist between young adults living with parents versus those living independently?
flashcards.back: Young adults living with parents are less likely to be living in poverty (10%) compared to those living independently (17%), suggesting that multigenerational households function as an economic safety net. Adult children aged 25 and older are more likely to contribute financially or complete chores when living at home.

flashcards.front: What is the concept of linked lives and how does it apply to middle-aged parents with adult children?
flashcards.back: Linked lives is a life course concept proposing that individuals in important relationships mutually influence each other's developmental pathways. For middle-aged parents, this means that problems faced by adult children (alcoholism, mental health, legal issues, etc.) negatively affect parents' emotional health, causing more negative affect, lower self-acceptance, poorer parent-child interactions, and more family stress, even when the children don't live at home. Single parents fare the worst.

flashcards.front: What demographic factors influence likelihood of being in the sandwich generation?
flashcards.back: Age: 71% of sandwich generation adults are 40-59 years old. Ethnicity: Hispanics are most likely (31% have a parent 65+ and a dependent child), compared to 24% of whites and 21% of blacks. Gender: Women are more likely to provide personal care for aging parents in the U.S. and Germany, though in Italy men and women are equally likely.

flashcards.front: What is empty nest syndrome and how well is it supported by research?
flashcards.back: Empty nest syndrome is the concept of great emotional distress experienced by parents, typically mothers, after children leave home. It is linked to the absence of alternative roles for establishing identity. However, Bouchard's (2013) review found that few parents actually report significant loneliness or loss, suggesting the syndrome is less common than the concept implies.

flashcards.front: How have parental attitudes toward boomerang kids changed over time?
flashcards.back: Early research (1980s-1990s, Aquilino, 1991) found that parents reported more negative perceptions and greater financial hardship from boomerang kids. More recent research shows today's parents are more tolerant, possibly because it has become more normative. Parents of adult children who returned for economic reasons report similar life satisfaction to those with independent children.

flashcards.front: What gender differences exist in providing personal care to aging parents across different countries?
flashcards.back: In the U.S.: ~20% of women vs. 8% of men have helped with personal care of aging parents. In Germany: similar pattern with women more likely to provide care than men (4% of men). In Italy: men (25%) and women (26%) are equally likely to provide personal care, reflecting different cultural norms around caregiving.

flashcards.front: What is the financial impact of being in the sandwich generation?
flashcards.back: Only 28% of sandwich generation adults reported living comfortably, compared to 41% of those not supporting parents. About one-third were just making ends meet, compared to 17% of those without the additional financial burden. Despite this, sandwich generation adults reported similar levels of happiness, though they were more likely to feel rushed (1 in 3 vs. 23% of other adults).

flashcards.front: According to Greenfield & Marks (2006), what specific outcomes are worse for parents whose adult children have personal problems?
flashcards.back: Parents whose adult children face personal problems (alcoholism, chronic health issues, mental health concerns, legal trouble, poor social relationships, academic or job problems) report: more negative affect, lower self-acceptance, poorer parent-child interactions, and more family relationship stress. The more problems the adult children face, the worse the parents' emotional health. Single parents experience the worst outcomes.

flashcards.front: What roles does a kinkeeper typically perform in a family?
flashcards.back: Kinkeepers organize family gatherings, preserve and share family history, provide advice and emotional support, work to strengthen bonds between family members, maintain communication and connection among relatives, and often take on caregiving tasks such as transportation, making repairs, and providing meals. These activities total approximately 34 hours per month on average.

flashcards.front: Why is the empty nest considered a normative event rather than a crisis?
flashcards.back: The empty nest is considered normative because parents are generally aware that their children will become adults and eventually leave home, making it an anticipated and expected life transition. Normative events are typically less psychologically destabilizing than unexpected events because individuals can prepare emotionally and practically for them in advance.

flashcards.front: What did Bella DePaulo find regarding the emotional and physical health of always-single versus married individuals?
flashcards.back: DePaulo (2014) found little meaningful difference in emotional well-being or health between always-single individuals and married individuals, especially when comparing those who have been married four or more years. She argued that most studies are biased because they compare 'married' to 'not married' without distinguishing always-single from divorced or widowed individuals.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the three gatekeeping approaches post-divorce parents use when introducing a new partner to their children?
flashcards.back: (1) Gradual introduction — 47% of parents slowly introduce the new partner to give both children and partner time to adjust; (2) Open and transparent from the outset — 40% disclose the relationship openly from the beginning; (3) Concealment until cohabitation or remarriage is likely — 13% do not reveal the relationship until it is serious.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How do early adulthood divorces differ from midlife divorces according to Gottman and Levenson (2000)?
flashcards.back: Early adulthood divorces tend to be more angry and conflictual, with each partner blaming the other. Midlife divorces tend to be characterized by emotional distance, having grown apart, or a cooling off of the relationship rather than overt conflict.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What gender differences exist in the financial and emotional consequences of midlife divorce?
flashcards.back: In the AARP (2009) survey, 44% of middle-aged women reported financial problems after divorce compared to only 11% of men. However, many women reported feeling a great release from day-to-day unhappiness, and the majority of post-divorce 'enhancers' and 'competent loners' were women.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the reasons men versus women give for seeking divorce at midlife?
flashcards.back: Women cited verbal/physical abuse (23%), drug/alcohol abuse (18%), and infidelity (17%). Men cited falling out of love (17%), no longer sharing interests or values (14%), and infidelity (14%). Both genders felt the marriage had been over long before the formal decision to divorce.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the 'graying of divorce' and what factors explain it?
flashcards.back: The 'graying of divorce' (Brown & Lin, 2013) refers to the doubling of the divorce rate among adults 50 and older since the 1990s, even as the overall U.S. divorce rate declined. Reasons include: reduced stigma around divorce, older women being financially self-sufficient, and increased longevity making an incompatible marriage harder to endure.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the unique challenges that remarriages face compared to first marriages?
flashcards.back: Remarriages face lack of clarity in family roles and expectations, ambiguity in kin terminology and roles, navigating stepparenting, potential resentment among family members, lower tolerance for unhappy situations, and a higher divorce rate than first marriages.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Montenegro (2003) find about the dating motivations of singles aged 40–69?
flashcards.back: In a survey of over 3,000 singles aged 40–69, almost half reported their most important reason for dating was to have someone to talk to or do things with. Sexual fulfillment was also identified as an important goal for many participants.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does having children affect a divorced parent's likelihood of remarriage?
flashcards.back: Goldscheider and Sassler (2006) found that children living with their mothers reduces the mothers' likelihood of marrying a man without children. However, having children in the home increases single men's likelihood of marrying a woman with children. Adults who grew up in stepfamilies may also feel more prepared for stepfamily living.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a child-focused and adult-focused post-divorce parent?
flashcards.back: A child-focused parent allows the child's views, reactions, and needs to influence repartnering decisions. An adult-focused parent expects the child to adapt and accommodate parental wishes. Adult-focused mothers were found to be older, more educated, employed, less close to their children, and more responsive to their new partner's concerns over the child's.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What were the remarriage rate statistics in 2013 and how had they changed from 1990?
flashcards.back: In 2013, the remarriage rate was approximately 28 per 1,000 adults aged 18 and older, representing a 44% decline from 1990 and a 16% decline from 2008 (Payne, 2015). However, among adults 55 and older, remarriage rates were actually higher in 2013 than in 1960.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What gender and racial differences exist in remarriage rates?
flashcards.back: Men have higher remarriage rates than women at every age group starting at 25; in 2013, 64% of divorced/widowed men vs. 52% of women had remarried. Among racial groups, Whites were more likely to remarry than other groups, with the rate increasing for White women in particular. The gender gap persists most strongly among those 65 and older.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What percentage of adults age 45–54 are married, and how is that divided between first marriages and remarriages?
flashcards.back: 48% of adults aged 45–54 are married: 22% are in their first marriage and 26% have remarried, making marriage the most common relationship status for middle-aged adults in the U.S.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Hetherington and Kelly (2002) find about the outcomes for divorced women who used the experience for personal growth?
flashcards.back: Hetherington and Kelly (2002) identified two positive post-divorce groups: 'enhancers' who used divorce to improve themselves and seek better relationships, and 'competent loners' who grew emotionally but chose to remain single. The overwhelming majority of both groups were women, indicating divorce can be a growth experience particularly for women.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How quickly do most divorced adults begin dating and form new relationships?
flashcards.back: Anderson et al. (2004) found that most divorced adults had dated by one year after filing for divorce. One in four recent filers reported being in or having been in a serious relationship, and over half were in a serious relationship by one year after filing. Younger adults were more likely to be dating than middle-aged or older adults.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the Talmudic scholar quote about remarriage and what concept does it illustrate?
flashcards.back: The quote is: 'When a divorced man marries a divorced woman, four go to bed.' It illustrates that past relationship histories, memories, anxieties, and emotional baggage from previous marriages complicate new romantic relationships after divorce.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Alterovitz and Mendelsohn (2013) find about romantic and sexual interests in online dating ads across age groups?
flashcards.back: Reviewing online personal ads for those over 40, they found that romantic activities and sexual interests were mentioned at similar rates among middle-aged and young-old adults, but were mentioned less frequently among the old-old age group.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the three grandparenting styles described by Cherlin and Furstenberg (1986) and their approximate prevalence?
flashcards.back: 1. Remote (30%): Rarely sees grandchildren, usually due to distance or emotional distance; contact on special occasions only. 2. Companionate (55%): Spends time with grandchildren, acts more like a friend, little authority over them. 3. Involved (15%): Very active role, frequent contact, may exercise authority or co-reside; most common among grandmothers.

flashcards.front: Which grandparenting style is most common, and what are its key characteristics?
flashcards.back: Companionate grandparenting is the most common style (55%). These grandparents enjoy spending time with grandchildren and doing activities together, but deliberately avoid interfering with parenting decisions and exercise little authority. They tend to act more like friends than authority figures.

flashcards.front: How did Bengtson (2001) expand on Cherlin and Furstenberg's grandparenting styles?
flashcards.back: Bengtson (2001) noted that grandparents do not maintain a single fixed style. They may adopt different styles with different grandchildren and may change styles over time as family circumstances evolve, such as when stepping in during a family crisis or becoming a sole care provider.

flashcards.front: What gender differences in grandparenting roles were identified by Neugarten and Weinstein (1964)?
flashcards.back: Grandmothers are more likely to adopt the 'involved' grandparenting role with frequent contact and authority. In contrast, more grandfathers than grandmothers saw their role as family historian and family advisor.

flashcards.front: What did Sorenson and Cooper (2010) find about grandfathers and technology?
flashcards.back: Many grandfathers used technology — including texting, emailing, and Skype — to stay in touch with geographically distant grandchildren. This finding highlighted how digital communication tools help maintain grandparent-grandchild bonds across distances.

flashcards.front: Why have grandfathers historically been underrepresented in grandparenting research?
flashcards.back: According to Sorenson and Cooper (2010), early grandparent research routinely focused on grandmothers, making grandfathers 'invisible members of the family.' Despite this research gap, studies such as Waldrop et al. (1999) confirmed that grandfathers value their grandchild relationships just as strongly as grandmothers.

flashcards.front: How does the grandfather role differ emotionally from the father role for many men?
flashcards.back: Many grandfathers report being more openly affectionate, light-hearted, and flexible with grandchildren than they were with their own children. Traditional occupational demands and generational norms around masculinity and fatherhood often prevented men from expressing such affection with their own children. The grandfather role provides a second opportunity for nurturing emotional expression.

flashcards.front: What factors influence the degree of grandparent involvement with grandchildren?
flashcards.back: Key factors include: (1) geographic proximity — grandparents living near grandchildren tend to be more involved; (2) cultural background — multigenerational households are more common in Latin America, Asia, and Africa; (3) family structure and crises, which may require grandparents to become sole care providers; and (4) personal preference and the grandparent's own health and circumstances.

flashcards.front: What trend regarding multigenerational households was identified by the Pew Research Center (2010)?
flashcards.back: Multigenerational households — more historically common in Latin America, Asia, and Africa — have been on the increase in the United States. In these households, grandparents tend to play a greater role in the day-to-day activities of grandchildren.

flashcards.front: What does it mean for a grandparent to be a 'sole care provider,' and what has this trend led to?
flashcards.back: A grandparent who is a sole care provider is the primary or exclusive caregiver for grandchildren, often stepping in during family crises (e.g., parental illness, substance abuse, incarceration). This growing trend has led grandparents to redefine their family roles, with fewer adopting the traditional formal grandparent role (Hayslip, Henderson & Shore, 2003).

flashcards.front: Compare the involved grandparent style to the remote grandparent style on three dimensions.
flashcards.back: Contact frequency: Involved = frequent; Remote = rare. Authority: Involved = exercises authority over grandchild; Remote = little to no authority. Residence: Involved = grandchild may live with grandparent; Remote = typically lives far away. Gender: Involved = more commonly grandmothers; Remote = no specific gender pattern cited.

flashcards.front: What did Waldrop et al. (1999) find about grandfathers' emotional investment in grandchildren?
flashcards.back: Waldrop et al. (1999) found that grandfathers stress the importance of their relationships with their grandchildren as strongly as grandmothers do, contradicting the assumption that grandmothers are inherently more emotionally invested in grandparenting.

flashcards.front: Why might developed nations see more remote grandparent relationships compared to developing nations?
flashcards.back: In developed nations, greater societal mobility means family members — including grandparents — may relocate for employment or other reasons, resulting in grandparents living long distances from grandchildren. This physical separation naturally reduces the frequency and intensity of in-person contact, often leading to remote grandparenting styles.

flashcards.front: What roles did Neugarten and Weinstein (1964) specifically associate more with grandfathers than grandmothers?
flashcards.back: Neugarten and Weinstein (1964) found that grandfathers more commonly identified with the roles of family historian (keeper and transmitter of family stories and heritage) and family advisor (provider of guidance and wisdom), compared to grandmothers who were more likely to take on the involved caregiving role.

flashcards.front: How has technology specifically changed grandparenting practices according to research?
flashcards.back: Technology has enabled geographically distant grandparents to maintain meaningful relationships with grandchildren through digital communication. Sorenson and Cooper (2010) found grandfathers using texting, email, and Skype for this purpose. These tools help bridge the gap created by geographic distance, particularly in highly mobile developed societies.

flashcards.front: What is the developmental context in which most people first become grandparents?
flashcards.back: Most people first become grandparents during middle adulthood (roughly ages 40–65). At this life stage, individuals are simultaneously maintaining relationships with their own children and with aging parents, and the grandparent role adds yet another significant relational dimension to their lives.

flashcards.front: How did Sorenson and Cooper (2010) characterize grandfathers' subjective experience of the grandparent role compared to the father role?
flashcards.back: Many grandfathers in Sorenson and Cooper's (2010) study felt that being a grandfather was easier and considerably more enjoyable than being a father. Even those who took on a more involved grandparenting role reported a greater sense of being light-hearted and flexible in their interactions with grandchildren, and many were more openly affectionate than they had been with their own children.

flashcards.front: What is the 'double standard of aging' and who described it?
flashcards.back: The double standard of aging refers to the phenomenon in which aging men are viewed as 'distinguished' while aging women are viewed as 'old.' It was described by Teuscher & Teuscher (2006) and reflects the different criteria society uses to judge aging in men versus women.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why are women traditionally considered 'old' at menopause while men are not considered old until they cannot work?
flashcards.back: Because women have historically been valued primarily for their reproductive capabilities, they are considered old once postmenopausal. Men, in contrast, have been valued for their achievements, competence, and power, so they are only considered old when physically unable to work. (Carroll, 2016)
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does being a lesbian affect a woman's attitude toward aging compared to heterosexual women?
flashcards.back: Lesbian women tend to be more positive about aging and looking older than heterosexual women (Slevin, 2010), likely due to different community norms and reduced pressure to conform to mainstream standards of youthful beauty.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What racial and ethnic differences exist in women's attitudes toward aging?
flashcards.back: Compared to white women, Black and Latina women possess fewer negative stereotypes about aging (Schuler et al., 2008), indicating that racial and ethnic background moderates the psychological impact of aging.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does aging affect the social status of Asian women according to research?
flashcards.back: According to Fung (2013), as Asian women age, they attain greater respect and have greater authority in the household—a contrast to Western norms where aging is more likely to diminish a woman's perceived social value.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What psychological effects does the double standard of aging have on women in midlife?
flashcards.back: Women experience more fear, anxiety, and concern about their identity as they age. They may also feel pressure to prove themselves as productive and valuable members of society (Bromberger, Kravitz, & Chang, 2013).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What role does the media play in women's experience of aging?
flashcards.back: The media influences how women view aging by selling anti-aging products and supporting cosmetic surgeries aimed at helping women look younger (Gilleard & Higgs, 2000). This reinforces societal emphasis on youthful beauty and can intensify negative perceptions of aging.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: In Western society, aging is described as more stressful for women than men — why?
flashcards.back: Western society places a strong emphasis on youthful beauty and attractiveness for women (Slevin, 2010). Women's social value has traditionally been tied to reproductive capacity, making postmenopause a marker of 'old age,' while men are valued for achievements and power regardless of reproductive status.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Compare how Western culture values men vs. women as they age.
flashcards.back: Men are valued for achievements, competence, and power and are not considered old until physically unable to work. Women are valued for reproductive capabilities and are considered old once postmenopausal, creating a significant double standard (Carroll, 2016; Teuscher & Teuscher, 2006).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Which groups of women are described as having more positive attitudes toward aging?
flashcards.back: Lesbian women (compared to heterosexual women), Black and Latina women (compared to white women), and Asian women who gain cultural status with age all demonstrate more positive attitudes toward aging. This reflects significant variation by sexual orientation, race, ethnicity, and culture.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why might cosmetic surgery rates be higher among midlife women than men?
flashcards.back: Due to the double standard of aging, women face greater societal pressure to maintain youthful appearances. Media promotion of anti-aging products and cosmetic procedures, combined with women's traditional valuation based on physical attractiveness and reproductive capacity, drives higher cosmetic surgery utilization among women.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What does Slevin (2010) specifically contribute to our understanding of women and aging?
flashcards.back: Slevin (2010) documented two key findings: (1) aging in Western society is more stressful for women than men due to the emphasis on youthful beauty and attractiveness, and (2) lesbian women are more positive about aging and looking older than heterosexual women.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How do cultural attitudes about aging differ from Western norms?
flashcards.back: In some cultures, aging women gain greater social status — for example, Asian women gain more respect and authority in the household as they age (Fung, 2013). Black and Latina women also hold fewer stereotypes about aging compared to white women (Schuler et al., 2008), contrasting with typical Western norms that devalue older women.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between reproductive capability and ageism toward women?
flashcards.back: Because women have traditionally been valued for their reproductive capabilities, the end of reproductive capacity (menopause) is treated as a social marker of old age. This ties ageism toward women directly to reproductive status, a phenomenon not paralleled in how men are socially evaluated as they age.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why might women in midlife feel pressure to be 'productive and valuable' members of society?
flashcards.back: Because the double standard of aging labels postmenopausal women as 'old' and less socially valued, women may feel compelled to counter this narrative by demonstrating productivity and value beyond reproductive roles (Bromberger, Kravitz, & Chang, 2013).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What factors moderate how women experience and perceive aging?
flashcards.back: Key moderating factors include race/ethnicity (Black and Latina women hold fewer stereotypes), culture (Asian women may gain status with age), sexual orientation (lesbians more positive about aging), and media exposure (which can increase pressure to look young). These factors all intersect with the broader societal double standard of aging.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between religiosity and spirituality according to Greenfield, Vaillant, and Marks (2009)?
flashcards.back: Religiosity refers to engaging with a formal religious group's doctrines, values, traditions, and co-members. Spirituality refers to an individual's intrapsychic sense of connection with something transcendent and the feelings of awe, gratitude, compassion, and forgiveness that follow. Spirituality can exist independently of formal religious participation.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What psychological well-being outcomes are associated with higher spirituality according to the 2005 MIDUS data?
flashcards.back: Higher spirituality was associated with lower negative affect and higher levels of: positive affect, personal growth, purpose in life, positive relationships with others, self-acceptance, environmental mastery, and autonomy.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What psychological well-being outcomes are associated with formal religious participation according to Greenfield et al. (2009)?
flashcards.back: Formal religious participation was associated with higher levels of purpose in life and personal growth among older adults only, and with lower levels of autonomy. It was NOT associated with the broad range of well-being outcomes linked to spirituality.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What three factors explain why older individuals tend to be more religious and spiritual than younger individuals?
flashcards.back: 1) Religion and spirituality help older individuals cope with age-related losses. 2) They provide opportunities for socialization and social support in later life. 3) A cohort effect — older generations were socialized more strongly to be religious and spiritual than younger cohorts.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: According to the Pew Research Center (2016), what are the key gender differences in religious participation in the United States?
flashcards.back: Women are more likely than men to say religion is very important (60% vs. 47%), pray daily (64% vs. 47%), and attend religious services at least weekly (40% vs. 32%).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why might women be more religious and spiritual than men according to the theoretical explanations provided?
flashcards.back: Two main theories: 1) Women may benefit more from the social-relational aspects of religion/spirituality because social relationships more strongly influence women's mental health. 2) Women have been socialized to internalize behaviors linked with religious values such as cooperation and nurturance more than men.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is a minyan and how does it affect gender differences in Jewish worship attendance?
flashcards.back: A minyan is a quorum of at least 10 Jewish men required for communal worship services in Orthodox Judaism. This religious norm ensures high rates of synagogue attendance among men. In Israel, where ~22% of Jewish adults are Orthodox, a higher percentage of men than women report engaging in daily prayer — a rare exception to the global pattern of women being more religious.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How did Muslim gender differences in religiosity differ from Christian gender differences in the Pew Research Center 2016 survey?
flashcards.back: Among Christians, women reported higher rates of weekly church attendance and daily prayer than men. Among Muslims, men and women showed similar levels of religiousness overall, except that Muslim men attended mosque more frequently than Muslim women due to religious norms, not a difference in general religiosity.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Sawatzky, Ratner, and Chiu (2005) find about spirituality and quality of life?
flashcards.back: Spirituality was related to a higher quality of life for both individuals and societies, suggesting that the benefits of spiritual orientation extend beyond the individual level to broader societal well-being.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: According to Grzywacz and Keyes (2004), how does religious involvement relate to health?
flashcards.back: Involvement in religious-related activities is positively related to optimal health, similar to personal health behaviors like regular exercise, healthy weight, and not smoking. Both personal and social behaviors (including religious involvement) contribute to optimal health outcomes.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Is spirituality's relationship with psychological well-being dependent on formal religious participation?
flashcards.back: No. Research by Vaillant (2008) demonstrated a strong relationship between spirituality and psychological well-being irrespective of an individual's religious participation. One does not need to be formally religious to benefit psychologically from spirituality.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: In the Pew Research Center 2016 global survey, what was the overall gender gap in religious identification worldwide?
flashcards.back: Approximately 83% of women worldwide identified with a religion compared to 80% of men, equaling approximately 97 million more women than men identifying with a religion. There were no countries in which men were more religious than women by 2 percentage points or more.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does formal religious participation relate to autonomy compared to spirituality?
flashcards.back: Formal religious participation is associated with LOWER levels of autonomy (possibly due to adherence to group doctrines and norms), whereas spirituality — as an intrapsychic, individually-driven experience — is associated with HIGHER levels of autonomy.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What age-related factors explain increased religiosity and spirituality in older adulthood?
flashcards.back: Older adults face more age-related losses (health, loved ones, roles), making religion and spirituality important coping mechanisms. They also benefit from the social support and socialization opportunities religious communities provide. Additionally, a cohort effect means older generations were raised to be more religious.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the significance of the finding that spirituality benefits both religious and non-religious individuals?
flashcards.back: It means that the psychological benefits of spirituality — including lower negative affect, higher positive affect, personal growth, purpose, and well-being — are accessible to anyone who cultivates a personal sense of connection to something transcendent, regardless of institutional religious affiliation. This has implications for health promotion across diverse populations.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Compare the scope of psychological well-being benefits from spirituality vs. formal religious participation.
flashcards.back: Spirituality is broadly associated with 8 dimensions of well-being: lower negative affect, higher positive affect, personal growth, purpose in life, positive relationships, self-acceptance, environmental mastery, and autonomy. Formal religious participation has a much narrower association — only purpose in life and personal growth (and only among older adults), plus lower autonomy.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the 'gray divorce revolution' and who documented it?
flashcards.back: The gray divorce revolution refers to the rising divorce rate among adults aged 50 and older in the United States between 1990 and 2010. It was documented by Brown and Lin (2013) through the National Center for Family & Marriage Research at Bowling Green State University.

flashcards.front: What did Chang et al. (2016) find about midlife adiposity and Alzheimer's disease?
flashcards.back: Chang et al. (2016) found that midlife adiposity (obesity/overweight) predicts earlier onset of Alzheimer's dementia, neuropathology, and presymptomatic cerebral amyloid accumulation, suggesting that weight management in midlife may be a modifiable risk factor for Alzheimer's.

flashcards.front: Describe Erik Erikson's stage of generativity versus stagnation and when it occurs.
flashcards.back: Generativity vs. stagnation is Erikson's seventh psychosocial stage, occurring in middle adulthood (approximately ages 40–65). Generativity involves contributing to society through raising children, mentoring, and creative productivity. Failure results in stagnation — a sense of self-absorption and lack of meaningful contribution.

flashcards.front: What is the buffering hypothesis proposed by Cohen and Wills (1985)?
flashcards.back: The buffering hypothesis states that social support protects (buffers) individuals from the harmful psychological and physical effects of stress. Social support is especially protective during periods of high stress, acting as a moderator between stressors and negative health outcomes.

flashcards.front: How does Csikszentmihalyi define 'flow' and what are its key characteristics?
flashcards.back: Csikszentmihalyi (1990) defines flow as a state of optimal experience in which a person is completely absorbed in a challenging, intrinsically rewarding activity. Key characteristics include full concentration, loss of self-consciousness, distorted time perception, and deep enjoyment. It is associated with creativity and peak performance.

flashcards.front: What did Dew et al. (2003) and Ferrie et al. (2007) find about sleep and mortality in adults?
flashcards.back: Dew et al. (2003) found that healthy older adults' sleep quality predicted all-cause mortality at 4 to 19 years of follow-up. Ferrie et al. (2007) found that changes in sleep duration were associated with mortality in the Whitehall II cohort, with both short and long sleep durations linked to increased risk.

flashcards.front: What is the 'glass ceiling' versus the 'glass partition' in workplace research?
flashcards.back: The glass ceiling (Barreto, Ryan & Schmitt, 2009) refers to invisible barriers preventing women and minorities from reaching top organizational positions. The glass partition (Elsesser & Peplau, 2006) refers specifically to obstacles preventing cross-sex friendships in the workplace, such as concerns about sexual misinterpretation.

flashcards.front: What is kinkeeping and which demographic typically performs this role?
flashcards.back: Kinkeeping is the role of maintaining family connections, organizing family gatherings, facilitating communication among family members, and preserving intergenerational bonds. This role is most often assumed by women in midlife. It was studied by Bengtson (2001) and Brown & DeRycke (2010).

flashcards.front: What did Bromberger et al. (2013) find about anxiety and the menopausal transition?
flashcards.back: Bromberger et al. (2013), using data from the Study of Women's Health Across the Nation (SWAN), found evidence suggesting that risk for anxiety increases during the menopausal transition, indicating that hormonal changes during perimenopause may contribute to psychological vulnerability.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between problem-focused and emotion-focused coping?
flashcards.back: Problem-focused coping involves taking direct action to address or eliminate the source of stress (e.g., making a plan, seeking information). Emotion-focused coping involves managing the emotional distress caused by the stressor (e.g., seeking social support, reframing). Carver, Scheier & Weintraub (1989) developed a widely used measure of these coping strategies.

flashcards.front: What did Ahlborg, Misvaer & Möller (2009) find about marital quality and parenthood?
flashcards.back: Ahlborg, Misvaer & Möller (2009) conducted a follow-up study of parents when their firstborn was 4 years old and examined perceptions of marital quality. The study highlighted that having small children can strain marital satisfaction, with couples reporting declines in relationship quality during early parenthood.

flashcards.front: What does research say about the experience of parents when children leave home (empty nest)?
flashcards.back: Research presents mixed findings. Borland (1982) found empty nest syndrome varies by ethnicity. Bouchard (2013) found most parents adapt well. Dennerstein, Dudley & Guthrie (2002) found a 'revolving door' phenomenon where children leave and re-enter. Many parents, especially women, report improved wellbeing and marital satisfaction after children leave.

flashcards.front: What did Baltes, Staudinger & Lindenberger (1999) argue about lifespan psychology and intellectual functioning?
flashcards.back: Baltes, Staudinger & Lindenberger (1999) argued that intellectual functioning across the lifespan is characterized by multidirectionality (some abilities decline while others improve), plasticity (capacity for change), and embeddedness in historical and cultural context. They emphasized that development involves both gains and losses throughout life.

flashcards.front: What are the health risks associated with caregiver roles in midlife and beyond?
flashcards.back: Beach et al. (2000) found both negative and positive health effects of caregiving. Negative effects include increased stress, depression, and physical health decline. Positive effects can include sense of purpose and relationship closeness. Arai et al. (2000) found that in rural Japan, concern for others' opinions deterred caregivers from using public services, compounding burden.

flashcards.front: What did Carmichael, Reis & Duberstein (2015) find about social activity across adulthood?
flashcards.back: Carmichael, Reis & Duberstein (2015) found that in one's 20s, the quantity of social activity is more prognostically valuable, while in one's 30s, the quality of social relationships becomes more important for wellbeing outcomes over a 30-year follow-up period.

flashcards.front: What did the CDC (2014e) report about HIV among adults aged 50 and older?
flashcards.back: The CDC (2014e) reported that HIV diagnoses among adults aged 50 and older represent a significant and growing concern in the United States. Older adults are often underrepresented in prevention messaging and may be diagnosed later due to assumptions that they are not at risk, leading to worse health outcomes.

flashcards.front: What relationship did Avis, Stellato & Crawford (2001) examine regarding menopause?
flashcards.back: Avis, Stellato & Crawford (2001) examined whether a universal menopausal syndrome exists across racial and ethnic groups. They found that menopausal symptoms and their severity vary significantly by racial and ethnic background, suggesting that the menopausal experience is culturally and biologically diverse rather than uniform.

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the 'difference-equals-deficit error'?
options: Assuming a developmental difference from the norm is inferior or abnormal
Failing to replicate a study before publishing results
Confusing cohort effects with individual developmental differences
Measuring development using only one discipline
questions.answer: Assuming a developmental difference from the norm is inferior or abnormal

questions.text: In Bronfenbrenner's ecological model, which system includes cultural patterns, political philosophies, and economic policies?
A critical period differs from a sensitive period in that a critical period is:
options: A time when development MUST happen or it will NOT happen at all
A time when development is most likely but can still occur later with more difficulty
A developmental window that occurs only during childhood
Exclusively determined by genetic factors with no environmental influence
questions.answer: A time when development MUST happen or it will NOT happen at all

questions.text: Piaget's concept of 'accommodation' refers to:
options: Restructuring old ideas to include new experiences that do not fit existing schemas
Interpreting new experiences to fit into existing mental frameworks
The state of mental balance with no cognitive confusion
The stage when infants use senses and motor skills to understand the world
questions.answer: Restructuring old ideas to include new experiences that do not fit existing schemas

questions.text: Which theory proposes that human development results from dynamic interaction between developing persons and their surrounding society, with culture integral to daily development?

options: Focuses on voluntary behaviors and their consequences such as reinforcement and punishment
Pairs neutral stimuli with meaningful stimuli to produce automatic responses
Explains learning through observation and imitation of others
Emphasizes unconscious drives as the basis for all behavior
questions.answer: Focuses on voluntary behaviors and their consequences such as reinforcement and punishment

questions.text: By the end of the embryonic period at week 8, which of the following is TRUE?

questions.text: Which part of the brain is the LAST to mature, specializing in anticipation, planning, and impulse control?

questions.text: In Piaget's Stage 4 of sensorimotor development (6-12 months), the key new ability is:

questions.text: The naming explosion typically begins when an infant's spoken vocabulary reaches approximately:

questions.text: Proximal parenting involving frequent holding and physical closeness tends to produce toddlers who are:

Research on Romanian orphans confirmed that the cognitive and emotional deficits were caused primarily by genetics rather than their deprived environment.
Separation anxiety that remains strong after age 3 may be considered an emotional disorder.
The repetition of a study using different participants to verify findings is called __________.
When a child encounters a new experience that cannot fit into existing schemas, the resulting mental imbalance is called cognitive __________, which leads to growth through adaptation.
Only about __________% of all zygotes successfully become newborns.
The biological mechanism that protects the brain when malnutrition disrupts body growth is called __________.
Early rapid dendrite growth in the developing brain is called transient __________. This is followed by __________ of unused connections.
A single word used by an infant to express a complete thought or sentence is called a __________.
The coordinated, rapid, and smooth exchange of responses between a caregiver and an infant is called __________.
Match each level of Bronfenbrenner's ecological model to its correct description.
pairs: Microsystem
Immediate, direct influences — family, school, peers, neighborhood
Exosystem
External networks that affect microsystems — community structures, media, medical institutions
Macrosystem
Cultural patterns, political philosophies, economic policies, social conditions
Chronosystem
Dimension of time — how personal and societal conditions change over the lifespan
questions.answer: Micro=immediate direct, Exo=external networks, Macro=cultural/political, Chrono=time dimension

questions.text: Match each developmental theory to its key theorist(s) and primary focus.
pairs: Psychoanalytic theory
Freud and Erikson — unconscious drives and early childhood crises
Classical conditioning
Pavlov — associating neutral stimuli with meaningful stimuli
Operant conditioning
Skinner — reinforcement and punishment shape voluntary behavior
Sociocultural theory
Vygotsky — culture, ZPD, and social interaction drive development

questions.text: Match each prenatal period to its timeframe and key developmental event.
pairs: Germinal period
Weeks 1-2 — rapid cell division, implantation; about 60% of zygotes do not survive
Embryonic period
Weeks 3-8 — all organ systems form in rudimentary form; about 20% spontaneously abort
Fetal period
Week 9 to birth — growth and maturation; SRY gene triggers sex organs at week 9
questions.answer: Germinal=weeks 1-2 cell division, Embryonic=weeks 3-8 organ formation, Fetal=week 9-birth growth

questions.text: Match each stage of Piaget's sensorimotor period to its age range and key characteristic.
pairs: Stage 1
Birth to 1 month — reflexes only; behavior is purely reactive
Stage 3
4 to 8 months — secondary circular reactions; makes interesting things last
Stage 4
6 to 12 months — means-to-an-end; begins searching for hidden objects
Stage 6
18 to 24 months — mental combinations; can think through consequences before acting
questions.answer: Stage1=reflexes, Stage3=secondary CRs, Stage4=means-to-end, Stage6=mental combinations

questions.text: Match each attachment type to its characteristic behavior in the Strange Situation.
pairs: Secure (Type B)
Explores freely; distressed at departure; joyfully welcomes return
Insecure-Avoidant (Type A)
Indifferent to caregiver's presence, departure, or return — avoids connection
Insecure-Resistant (Type C)
Clingy before separation; very distressed; difficult to comfort on return
Disorganized (Type D)
No consistent strategy; confused or contradictory responses
questions.answer: Secure=explores/welcomes, Avoidant=indifferent, Resistant=clingy/hard to comfort, Disorganized=confused

questions.text: Compare and contrast the grand theories (psychoanalytic, behaviorism, cognitive) with the newer theories (sociocultural, evolutionary). What does each contribute to understanding human development?
questions.answer:
GRAND THEORIES:
Psychoanalytic (Freud and Erikson): made us aware of the impact of early-childhood experiences, remembered or not, on subsequent development. Unconscious processes matter.
Behaviorism (Watson, Pavlov, Skinner): showed the effect of immediate responses, associations, and modeling on learning moment by moment and over time.
Cognitive (Piaget and information-processing): brought understanding of how intellectual processes and our thoughts and beliefs affect every aspect of development.

NEWER THEORIES:
Sociocultural (Vygotsky): reminds us that development is embedded in a rich cultural context visible in every social interaction; learning happens in the ZPD through mentoring.
Evolutionary: suggests human impulses rooted in survival and reproduction must be recognized before they can be guided.

NATURE vs. NURTURE: Grand theories tend to emphasize either nature (psychoanalytic, cognitive) or nurture (behaviorism). Newer theories focus more on their interaction (sociocultural = more nurture; evolutionary = more nature).

ECLECTIC PERSPECTIVE: Most developmentalists selectively apply aspects of each theory because no single theory explains everything. Each illuminates different aspects of the complexity of human development.

questions.text: What do the Romanian orphan studies tell us about the importance of early experience for infant development? What does the research show about timing of adoption and long-term outcomes?
questions.answer:
CONTEXT: In the early 1990s, thousands of children were adopted from Romanian orphanages after years of severe social deprivation — minimal human interaction, stimulation, or emotional connection.

KEY FINDINGS BY TIMING:
Adopted before 6 months: most developed normally, suggesting high brain plasticity if intervention occurs early enough.
Adopted after age 1: initially showed encouraging signs, but long-term cognitive and emotional deficits manifested in many. As adults, most had emotional and/or conduct problems.

CRITICAL CONCLUSION: Early EXPERIENCE, not genetics, was the main problem. The deficit was environmental deprivation, not inherited traits.

IMPLICATIONS FOR DEVELOPMENT:
1. Confirms the existence of sensitive, possibly critical, periods for social-emotional development.
2. Synchrony and attachment are experience-expectant — the brain literally requires these interactions for normal development.
3. The brain has some capacity for self-righting or plasticity if intervention comes early enough.
4. It is the PATTERNS of deprivation that cause lasting harm, not isolated moments.
5. These findings support Bronfenbrenner's model — the microsystem of immediate caregiving profoundly shapes development.

questions.text: The primary reason for faster thinking in early childhood (ages 2–6) is:
options: Myelination — fatty coating on axons that speeds nerve signal transmission
Pruning of excess dendrites in the limbic system
Growth of the corpus callosum connecting the hemispheres
Maturation of the amygdala and stress-response systems

questions.text: A child who keeps repeating the same answer even after being corrected is demonstrating:

pairs: Role confusion (diffusion)

questions.text: What is the neurodiversity perspective on ASD and other developmental disorders? How does this perspective change the approach to diagnosis and treatment?
questions.answer:
The NEURODIVERSITY PERSPECTIVE holds that human brains make people diverse in abilities and needs — just as human bodies vary physically, human brains vary neurologically, and this variation is a natural part of human diversity rather than a defect to be corrected.

Applied to ASD and other disorders, this perspective:
1. Questions the assumption that all brains should conform to one neurotypical standard
2. Leads to criticism of many ASD treatments that aim to make autistic individuals behave more 'normally' rather than supporting them in living according to their own nature
3. Shifts focus from 'fixing' the person to accommodating their needs and building on their strengths
4. Recognizes that behaviors considered problematic in one context may be adaptive in another

This is a logical extension of the criticism of IQ tests — just as IQ tests measure only certain types of intelligence, treatment approaches that target 'normalization' may be too narrow.

However, it is important to note that some ASD symptoms (such as severe self-injurious behavior or complete inability to communicate) do require intervention regardless of perspective. The neurodiversity view is most applied to higher-functioning individuals and in educational rather than clinical settings.

questions.text: Describe the role of peers in adolescent development, including peer pressure, selection, facilitation, and deviancy training. How does this compare to the role of family?
questions.answer:
PEERS IN ADOLESCENCE play a powerful role, but the relationship is more complex than simple 'peer pressure':

PEER PRESSURE: encouragement to conform to friends in behavior, dress, and attitude. Often portrayed as the primary peer influence, but research shows it is more nuanced.

SELECTION: teenagers actively select friends whose values and interests they already share. This means peer influence is bidirectional — teens choose peers who reinforce their existing tendencies, not just the other way around.

FACILITATION: peers make it EASIER to do BOTH the wrong thing AND the right thing. This is crucial — peer influence is not inherently negative; peers can facilitate prosocial behavior, academic motivation, and positive identity formation.

DEVIANCY TRAINING: destructive peer support in which one person shows another how to rebel against authority or social norms — associated with life-course-persistent offending.

COMPARED TO FAMILY: The family remains important for providing closeness (communication, support, connectedness, control) and the concept of FAMILISM (belief that family members should sacrifice personal freedom to care for one another) is protective against depression and delinquency. However, the influence of shared family environment SHRINKS with age while nonshared environments (peers, schools) increase in importance. The ideal is for families to provide a secure base while gradually increasing adolescent autonomy.

questions.text: According to Vygotsky, where does learning occur FIRST before becoming internalized?
options: Within the individual through independent exploration
Through biological maturation of the brain
Via observation of the environment without social input

questions.text: Which of the following best describes the Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD)?

The gap between actual development and potential development with guidance
A fixed stage of cognitive development tied to biological age
The period when a child transitions from private speech to inner speech

questions.text: A teacher provides a student with hints and prompts during a task, then gradually steps back as the student improves. This best illustrates which concept?

Egocentric speech with no cognitive function
questions.answer: Private speech guiding thinking and behavior

questions.text: Which of the following is the key difference between Dynamic Assessment and traditional static IQ testing?

Dynamic Assessment is administered in groups; IQ tests are individual
Dynamic Assessment was developed by Piaget; IQ tests were developed by Vygotsky
questions.answer: Dynamic Assessment measures learning potential with active support; IQ tests measure independent performance only

notes.title: Chapter 24: Late Adulthood – Cognitive Development (Berger, 11th Edition)
notes.body:
CHAPTER 24: LATE ADULTHOOD: COGNITIVE DEVELOPMENT
The Developing Person Through the Life Span | Kathleen Stassen Berger | Eleventh Edition

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE BRAIN DURING ADULTHOOD
- New brain cells: Neurons form and dendrites grow in adulthood, particularly in the hippocampus.
- New neurons provide cognitive adaptability to succeed in the context of challenging and changing environments.
- Growth of the brain is slow, limited, and not sufficient to restore itself to its younger state.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ATROPHY RANKING
NOTE: Not All Average — A team of neuroscientists in Scotland (Farrell et al., 2009) published images of the brains of healthy 65- to 70-year-olds. The images show normal brain loss from the lowest (5th percentile) to the highest (95th percentile).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ONGOING DEVELOPMENT
- New cells: New neurons appear in the adult brain particularly in the hippocampus (memory center) and the olfactory (smell) areas.
- Dendrite growth is also variable and crucial.
- New dendrites are evidence of lifelong plasticity.

NOTE: Senescence reduces the production of neurotransmitters—including glutamate, acetylcholine, serotonin, and especially dopamine—that allow a nerve impulse to jump quickly across the synaptic gap from one neuron to another. Neural fluid decreases, myelination thins, and cerebral blood circulates more slowly. Some experts believe that processing speed is a basic element of the g. Shrinkage is particularly notable in the hippocampus and the areas of the prefrontal cortex that are needed for planning, inhibiting unwanted responses, and coordinating thoughts. In every part of the brain, the volume of gray matter (crucial for processing new experiences) is reduced, in part because the cortex becomes thinner with every decade of adulthood.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SENESCENCE AND THE BRAIN
- Slower thinking is attributed to overall slowdown in reaction time, movement, speech, and thought.
- Neurotransmitter production is reduced.
- Neural fluid decreases, myelination thins, and cerebral blood circulates more slowly.
- Smaller brains:
  • Total volume of the brain becomes smaller.
  • Gray matter volume is reduced in every part of the brain and white-matter lesions form.
  • Transmission of brain impulses is disrupted.

NOTE: Sensation precedes perception, which precedes comprehension. Some information never reaches sensory memory in older people because the senses never detect the stimuli. The brain automatically fills in missed sights and sounds. Older people may believe that they see and hear whatever is important, but vital information may be distorted or lost without the person realizing it. People can compensate for some sensory decline, which would improve input.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 1) — INPUT
- Sensory loss may be undetected as the brain fills in missed sights and sounds.
- Interpretation of facial expressions, especially anger and sadness, may be impaired.
- Understanding of speech may be hampered by reduced hearing and vision acuity.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 2) — MEMORY
- Stereotype threat impedes memorial processes; suspecting memory loss can impact memory.
- Memory loss can be normal and pathological, but generally explicit memory loss is greater than implicit memory loss.
- Source amnesia may contribute to less analysis of information when elders cannot remember the origin of a fact.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 3) — PROSPECTIVE MEMORY
- Includes the ability to quickly shift mentally among tasks.
- Involves remembering to perform a future task.
- Fades notably with age.
- May be aided through routines.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 4) — CONTROL PROCESSES
- Are involved in the underlying impairment of cognition in late adulthood, especially impaired retrieval.
- Include executive function of the brain: Selective attention, strategic judgment, and then appropriate action.
- Shift as analysis and forethought give way to reliance on prior knowledge, general principles, and rules of thumb.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

OUTPUT (PART 1)
- Gradual decline in output of primary mental abilities (e.g., verbal meaning, spatial orientation, inductive reasoning, number ability, word fluency) is normal.
- In daily life, output is usually verbal.
- Two important modifiers are health and training.

NOTE: Ecological validity: The idea that cognition should be measured in settings that are realistic and that the abilities measured should be those needed in real life. This may be key to accurately measuring cognition in the elderly. Fundamental ecological issue: Addresses the question — What should be assessed—pure, abstract thinking or practical, contextual thought, depersonalized abilities, or everyday actions?

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

OUTPUT (PART 2)
- Cognitive tests: Measured output of five primary mental abilities show decline, beginning around age 60 (Seattle Longitudinal Study).
- Tests are normed and validated on younger adult output.
- Awareness of ecological validity may help restructure memory research.

NOTE: When they were together, younger partners were more accurate than older ones at knowing their partner's emotions, but older partners were as good as younger ones when they were apart. This study used smartphone experience sampling, buzzing both partners simultaneously to ask how they and their partner felt. Interestingly, differences were found with age but not length of relationship—5, 10, 20, or 30 years of partnership.

notes.title: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
RELIGION AND SPIRITUALITY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HEALTH AND RELIGION/SPIRITUALITY — OVERVIEW
• Grzywacz and Keyes (2004): In addition to personal health behaviors (regular exercise, healthy weight, not smoking), social behaviors — including involvement in religious-related activities — have been shown to be POSITIVELY related to optimal health.
• It is not only those involved in a specific religion who benefit — those identified as being SPIRITUAL also benefit.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DEFINITIONS (Greenfield, Vaillant, and Marks, 2009)
• RELIGIOSITY: Engaging with a formal religious group's doctrines, values, traditions, and co-members.
• SPIRITUALITY: An individual's intrapsychic sense of connection with something transcendent (that which exists apart from and not limited by the material universe) and the subsequent feelings of:
  - Awe
  - Gratitude
  - Compassion
  - Forgiveness

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SPIRITUALITY AND PSYCHOLOGICAL WELL-BEING
• Vaillant (2008): Research demonstrates a STRONG relationship between spirituality and psychological well-being, IRRESPECTIVE of an individual's religious participation.
• Sawatzky, Ratner, & Chiu (2005): Spirituality was related to a HIGHER QUALITY OF LIFE for both individuals AND societies.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

NATIONAL SURVEY OF MIDLIFE IN THE UNITED STATES (2005) — FINDINGS
• Source: Greenfield et al. (2009), based on reports from the 2005 National Survey of Midlife in the United States.
• Higher levels of SPIRITUALITY were associated with:
  - Lower levels of NEGATIVE AFFECT
  - Higher levels of POSITIVE AFFECT
  - Personal growth
  - Purpose in life
  - Positive relationships with others
  - Self-acceptance
  - Environmental mastery
  - Autonomy
• Higher levels of FORMAL RELIGIOUS PARTICIPATION were associated with:
  - Higher levels of PURPOSE IN LIFE — only among OLDER ADULTS
  - PERSONAL GROWTH — only among OLDER ADULTS
  - LOWER levels of AUTONOMY
• Summary: Formal religious participation and spirituality relate DIFFERENTLY to an individual's overall psychological well-being.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

AGE DIFFERENCES IN RELIGION/SPIRITUALITY
• Beit-Hallahmi & Argyle (1998): OLDER individuals identify religion/spirituality as being MORE important in their lives than YOUNGER individuals.
• Explanations for this age difference (Greenfield et al., 2009):
  1. Religion and spirituality assist older individuals in COPING WITH AGE-RELATED LOSSES.
  2. Provide OPPORTUNITIES FOR SOCIALIZATION and social support in later life.
  3. COHORT EFFECT: Older individuals were socialized MORE to be religious and spiritual than those who are younger.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

GENDER DIFFERENCES IN RELIGION/SPIRITUALITY (UNITED STATES)
• de Vaus & McAllister (1987): In the United States, WOMEN report identifying as being MORE religious and spiritual than MEN.
• Pew Research Center (2016) — U.S. Statistics:
  - Women more likely to say religion is VERY IMPORTANT: 60% (women) vs. 47% (men)
  - Women more likely to say they PRAY DAILY: 64% (women) vs. 47% (men)
