3. SCOTOPIC SENSITIVITY drops in midlife; by age 60 the retina receives only 1/3 the light it did at age 20 (Jackson & Owsley, 2000)
4. Night vision declines as the pupil loses ability to adjust to drastic light changes; increased glare sensitivity
5. DRY EYE SYNDROME affects nearly 5 million Americans over 50; more common in women, especially post-menopausal women; can cause surface damage to the eye
6. HEARING LOSS doubles between ages 40–55 and triples by age 64 (Dawes et al., 2014, UK study)
7. U.S. stats: 5.5% under 40 have hearing problems vs. 19% ages 40–69
8. With age, ability to hear HIGHER FREQUENCIES declines (Humes et al., 2010)
9. Men have MORE hearing loss than women but underestimate it (Uchida et al., 2003)
10. Hearing loss exacerbated by: cigarette smoking, high blood pressure, stroke, noisy occupations
11. Most hearing loss is PREVENTABLE by avoiding extremely noisy environments

notes.title: Physical Changes in Middle Adulthood | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
PHYSICAL CHANGES IN MIDDLE ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HAIR
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- When asked to imagine someone in middle adulthood, we often picture someone with the beginnings of wrinkles and gray or thinning hair.
- Hair color is due to a pigment called MELANIN, which is produced by hair follicles (Martin, 2014).
- With aging, hair follicles produce LESS melanin → hair becomes gray.
- Hair color typically starts turning lighter at the TEMPLES, but eventually ALL hair will become white.
- For many, graying begins in the 30s, but it is LARGELY DETERMINED BY GENES.
- Gray hair occurs EARLIER in white people and LATER in Asians.
- Genes also determine how much hair remains on your head.
- Almost everyone has SOME hair loss with aging; the rate of hair growth SLOWS with aging.
- Many hair follicles stop producing new hairs and hair strands become smaller.
- Men begin showing signs of balding by AGE 30; some are nearly bald by AGE 60.
- MALE-PATTERN BALDNESS:
  • Related to TESTOSTERONE
  • Identified by a RECEDING HAIRLINE followed by hair loss at the TOP OF THE HEAD
  • Example: Tennis champion Andre Agassi's characteristic male-patterned baldness (Figure 8.2)
- FEMALE-PATTERN BALDNESS:
  • Women can also develop female-patterned baldness
  • Hair becomes LESS DENSE and the scalp becomes visible (Martin, 2014)
- NOTE: Sudden hair loss can be a symptom of a health problem.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SKIN
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Skin continues to DRY OUT and is prone to MORE WRINKLING, particularly on the sensitive FACE area.
- WRINKLES (creases in the skin) are a NORMAL part of aging.
- As we get older, skin dries and loses the underlying layer of FAT → face no longer appears smooth.
- Loss of muscle tone and thinning skin can make the face appear FLABBY or DROOPING.
- Although wrinkles are natural and genetics plays a role, FREQUENT SUN EXPOSURE and SMOKING will cause wrinkles to appear SOONER.
- DARK SPOTS and BLOTCHY SKIN also occur with age due to EXPOSURE TO SUNLIGHT (Moskowitz, 2014).
- Blood vessels become more apparent as the skin continues to DRY and get THINNER.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SARCOPENIA
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- DEFINITION: The loss of muscle mass and strength that occurs with aging is referred to as SARCOPENIA (Morley, Baumgartner, Roubenoff, Mayer, & Nair, 2001).
- Sarcopenia is thought to be a SIGNIFICANT FACTOR in the FRAILTY and FUNCTIONAL IMPAIRMENT that occurs when older.
- CAUSES IMPLICATED:
  • Decline of GROWTH and ANABOLIC HORMONES, especially TESTOSTERONE
  • DECREASED PHYSICAL ACTIVITY
  • (Proctor, Balagopal, & Nair, 1998)
- Decline in muscle mass can occur as EARLY AS 40 YEARS OF AGE.
- Consequences of sarcopenia (Karakelides & Nair, 2005):
  • Significantly DECREASES LIFE QUALITY
  • INCREASES HEALTH CARE COSTS
  • Contributes to EARLY DEATH in older adults
- EXERCISE is important to increase:
  • Strength
  • Aerobic capacity
  • Muscle protein synthesis
- NOTE: Exercise does NOT reverse ALL the age-related changes that occur.
- The MUSCLE-TO-FAT RATIO for both men and women CHANGES throughout middle adulthood.
  • Accumulation of fat in the STOMACH AREA is characteristic.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LUNGS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- The lungs serve TWO FUNCTIONS:
  1. SUPPLY OXYGEN
  2. REMOVE CARBON DIOXIDE
- STRUCTURAL CHANGE: Thinning of the bones with age can CHANGE THE SHAPE OF THE RIB CAGE → results in a LOSS OF LUNG EXPANSION.
- MUSCULAR CHANGE: Age-related changes in muscles, such as the WEAKENING OF THE DIAPHRAGM, can also REDUCE LUNG CAPACITY.
- COMBINED EFFECTS of both changes:
  • LOWER oxygen levels in the blood
  • INCREASE levels of carbon dioxide
  • Can result in SHORTNESS OF BREATH and feeling TIRED (NIH, 2014b)
- In middle adulthood, these changes and their effects are often MINIMAL, especially in:
  • NON-SMOKERS
  • Physically ACTIVE people
- Effects can be MORE PRONOUNCED in those with:
  • CHRONIC BRONCHITIS
  • History of frequent PNEUMONIA
  • ASTHMA
  • Other lung-related disorders
  • SMOKERS

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. MELANIN is the pigment responsible for hair color; reduced melanin production → gray hair.
2. Graying typically begins in the 30s; occurs EARLIER in white people and LATER in Asians.
3. Male-pattern baldness is linked to TESTOSTERONE and begins with a RECEDING HAIRLINE.
4. Sudden hair loss = possible health problem symptom.
5. Sun exposure and smoking accelerate WRINKLING and skin aging.
6. Dark spots are caused by SUNLIGHT EXPOSURE (Moskowitz, 2014).
7. SARCOPENIA = loss of muscle mass/strength with aging; defined by Morley et al., 2001.
8. Sarcopenia can begin as early as AGE 40.
9. Sarcopenia is linked to decreased testosterone, anabolic hormones, and physical inactivity.
10. Sarcopenia consequences: reduced life quality, higher healthcare costs, early death (Karakelides & Nair, 2005).
11. Exercise helps but does NOT fully reverse sarcopenia.
12. Middle adulthood = accumulation of fat in the STOMACH AREA.
13. Lungs: thinning bones → rib cage shape change → reduced lung expansion; weakening diaphragm → reduced lung capacity.
14. Lung changes = lower O₂, higher CO₂, shortness of breath, fatigue.
15. Lung changes are MINIMAL in active non-smokers but PRONOUNCED in smokers, asthmatics, and those with chronic bronchitis.

notes.title: Lifespan Development: Introduction to Middle Adulthood & Physical Development
notes.body:
INTRODUCTION TO MIDDLE ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Middle adulthood = also called MIDLIFE
- Refers to the period of the lifespan BETWEEN early adulthood and late adulthood
- Ages are CULTURALLY DEFINED; most common age definition: 40-45 to 60-65
- This is considered the LEAST STUDIED time of the lifespan
- Research on this developmental period is RELATIVELY NEW
- Many aspects of midlife are still being explored
- In the United States, the large BABY BOOM COHORT (those born between 1946 and 1964) are now midlife adults (and some even late adults)
- The Baby Boom cohort has led to INCREASED INTEREST in this developmental stage
- Key known facts about this stage:
  • Reflects both DEVELOPMENTAL GAINS AND LOSSES
  • There are CONSIDERABLE INDIVIDUAL DIFFERENCES
  • There is still much to learn about this age group

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
LEARNING OBJECTIVES: PHYSICAL DEVELOPMENT IN MIDDLE ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Explain the difference between PRIMARY and SECONDARY aging
- Describe SENSORY CHANGES that occur during middle adulthood
- Identify HEALTH CONCERNS in middle adulthood
- Explain what occurs during the CLIMACTERIC for females and males
- Describe SEXUALITY during middle adulthood
- Explain the importance of SLEEP and consequences of SLEEP DEPRIVATION
- Describe the importance of EXERCISE and NUTRITION for optimal health
- Describe BRAIN FUNCTIONING in middle adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PRIMARY VS. SECONDARY AGING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Each person experiences age-related physical changes based on MANY FACTORS

PRIMARY AGING:
- Definition: Aging based on BIOLOGICAL FACTORS
- Includes: molecular and cellular changes, and OXIDATIVE DAMAGE
- These are largely UNAVOIDABLE biological processes
- Source: Busse, 1969

SECONDARY AGING:
- Definition: Aging that occurs due to CONTROLLABLE FACTORS
- Includes: unhealthy lifestyle choices
  • Lack of physical exercise
  • Poor diet
- These factors are within an individual's control
- Source: Busse, 1969

NOTE: Figure 8.1 — "Contributors to Aging" — visually illustrates these factors (biological/primary vs. lifestyle/secondary)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FACTORS THAT REDUCE OVERALL HEALTH IN MIDDLE ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Getting out of shape is NOT an inevitable part of aging
- It is probably due to:
  • Middle adults becoming LESS PHYSICALLY ACTIVE
  • Experiencing GREATER STRESS
- Factors that reduce overall health (secondary aging contributors):
  • Smoking tobacco
  • Drinking alcohol
  • Poor diet
  • Stress
  • Physical inactivity
  • Chronic disease (e.g., diabetes or arthritis)
- IMPORTANT: Many of these changes can be COMBATED by adopting HEALTHIER LIFESTYLES

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. Middle adulthood is most commonly defined as ages 40-45 to 60-65
2. Middle adulthood is the LEAST STUDIED period of the lifespan
3. The Baby Boom cohort = born between 1946 and 1964 — their entry into midlife increased research interest
4. PRIMARY AGING = biological, unavoidable (molecular/cellular changes, oxidative damage) — Busse, 1969
5. SECONDARY AGING = controllable lifestyle factors (poor diet, inactivity, smoking, etc.) — Busse, 1969
6. Getting out of shape in midlife is largely due to DECREASED ACTIVITY and INCREASED STRESS — NOT inevitable aging
7. Chronic diseases such as DIABETES and ARTHRITIS are examples of health-reducing factors in midlife
8. Healthier lifestyles CAN combat many secondary aging changes
9. Middle adulthood reflects BOTH gains AND losses developmentally, with CONSIDERABLE INDIVIDUAL DIFFERENCES
10. Key topics for physical development in middle adulthood: sensory changes, climacteric (male and female), sexuality, sleep, exercise and nutrition, and brain functioning

notes.title: Chapter 19: Emerging Adulthood – Psychosocial Development
notes.body:
CONTINUITY AND CHANGE
- Identity achieved: the search for identity begins at puberty and continues much longer.
- Most adults still seek to determine who they are.
- At each stage, the outcome of earlier crises provides the foundation of each new stage (Erikson).
NOTE: Dreamers are unauthorized immigrants to the United States who meet five criteria: (1) They are under age 31. (2) They entered the United States before age 16. (3) They lived continuously in the United States for at least five years. (4) They have never been convicted of a felony or a serious misdemeanor. (5) They graduated from a U.S. high school or served in the armed forces.

ETHNIC IDENTITY (PART 1)
- Includes Erikson's political and religious identities, which are crucial in the modern, multiethnic world.
- Is especially difficult for two U.S. groups:
  - Dreamers: Unauthorized U.S. immigrants who meet five criteria; cannot be citizens; different from peers in original nations.
  - Adoptees: International adoptees raised in the United States; reconciliation among many identities.
NOTE: Slide 6 reference.

ETHNIC IDENTITY (PART 2)
- Everyone else: Every emerging adult must establish ethnic and political identity.
- Half of emerging adults have non-European ancestors; many have mixed heritage.
- Knowing personal roots facilitates acceptance of roots of others.
NOTE: Emerging adulthood is a critical time for acquiring resources needed for family and career success. For many younger adults, vocational identity is not just about finding a job, or finding a job they like; it is about establishing who they want to be and then becoming that person. No wonder achieving vocational identity is difficult.

OTHER IDENTITY
- Political choices: The year 2008 was the first time in 70 years that millions of young adults did not vote for the same candidate as their grandparents.
- Gender identity: Gender identity options have broadened beyond traditional roles.
NOTE: A graph shows a steady, although small, rise in young adults' sense of well-being from age 18 to age 24, as measured by respondents' ratings of statements such as 'I feel I am a person of worth.' Ratings ranged from 1 (complete disagreement) to 5 (complete agreement). The average rating was quite high at age 18 and increased steadily over the years of emerging adulthood.

HAPPY AT WORK
- John Holland's six-part diagram helps job seekers realize that income and benefits are not the only goals of employment.
- Workers have healthier hearts and minds if their job fits their personal preferences.
NOTE: Each relationship demands some personal sacrifice, including vulnerability that brings deeper self-understanding and shatters the isolation of too much self-protection.

VOCATIONAL IDENTITY
- Establishing a vocational identity is part of growing up.
- Many go to college as a moratorium and to prepare for a job.
- Temporary jobs are part of preparation.
- Young workers tend to take a series of temporary jobs and feel no loyalty to their employer in reaction to the current global economy.
- Development of work ethic continues to evolve throughout early adulthood.
NOTE: More friends are made during emerging adulthood than any later period. Social media is used to extend and deepen friendships. Around age 30, quality of friends becomes more important than quantity. Rate of loneliness may increase during emerging adulthood.

PERSONALITY IN EMERGING ADULTHOOD
- Rising self-esteem: Only a minority experience decline in self-esteem.
- Continuity and improvement in attitudes of young adults occur.
- Ability to set their own goals, make their own friends, and work toward whatever goals they seek strengthens self-esteem.
NOTE: Some sex differences have been found, according to a meta-analysis of 37 studies.

CLOSE FAMILY AND FRIENDS
- Intimacy versus isolation: Erikson's sixth psychosocial stage emphasizes that humans are social creatures.
- Social isolation is harmful at every age and in every culture.
- Intimacy progresses from attraction to close connection to ongoing commitment.
- Marriage and parenthood, as emerging adults are discovering, are only two of several paths to intimacy.
NOTE: The authors of one study were struck by how similar the friendship patterns of sexual minority and majority people were. The one noticeable trend is age, not sexuality. People over 30 reported fewer friends overall, and fewer other-sex friends in particular, from an average of 2.6 to an average of 2.1.

EMERGING ADULTS AND THEIR PARENTS
- Linked lives: The success, health, and well-being of each family member are connected to those of other members.
- Financial support: Parents of all income levels in the United States help their adult children.
- Global perspective: Parental support and linked lives are typical everywhere. In some countries, it is valued more than in others.

TOO LITTLE OR TOO MUCH? (PART 1)
- Sometimes adults provide too much or too little nonfinancial support.
- Foster care support ends at age 18.
- Former foster children need adult emotional support and encouragement, in addition to some financial help.
- Biological children often have this support.
NOTE: In developing nations, a blend of the upper two often occurs; men usually have more say than women.

TOO LITTLE OR TOO MUCH? (PART 2)
- Helicopter parents: Hover over their emerging-adult child, ready to swoop down if any problem arises.
- Differential treatment: May cause sibling rivalry or resentment.
- Snowplow parents: Parents who try to remove any impediments in the way of their children. This term is pejorative; it implies that parents do not allow children to learn how to overcome obstacles on their own.
NOTE: The number of cohabiting male–female households in the United States has increased dramatically over the past decades. These numbers are an underestimate: Couples do not always tell the U.S. Census Bureau that they are living together, nor are cohabitants counted within their parents' households. Same-sex couples (not tallied until 2000) are also not included.

FRIENDSHIP
- Throughout life, friends defend against stress and provide joy.
- New and old friends are particularly crucial during emerging adulthood.
- Most single young adults have larger and more supportive friendship networks than newly married young adults once did.
NOTE: In a random sample of unmarried emerging adults (half men, half from two-parent homes, two-thirds European American, all from Toledo, Ohio) who had had a serious dating or cohabiting relationship in the past two years: 15 percent had broken up and not reunited; 41 percent had been together without breaking up; 44 percent were churners (defined as having broken up and gotten together again with their partner). Young adult relationships are often problematic, but churning correlates with the stormiest relationships, with half of churners fighting both physically and verbally.

GENDER AND FRIENDSHIPS
- Men tend to share activities and interests and talk about external matters.
  - Do not talk of failures or emotional problems.
  - Demand less of their friends, so they have more of them.
- Women tend to share secrets, reveal their weaknesses and problems, and expect sympathy.
  - Are more intimate and emotional.

MALE–FEMALE FRIENDSHIPS
- Gender differences are cultural, not biological.
- Norms are changing; not every male–female relationship is sexual.
- Similar number of friends among people of every sexual orientation.
- Number of friends did not correlate with life satisfaction; friendship satisfaction strongly predicts overall life satisfaction.
NOTE: Data from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention show that when victims are asked when the first incident of sexual violence, physical violence, or stalking occurred, emerging adulthood is the most likely time. For a sizable minority, the first incidence occurred before age 18 (especially for females) or after age 25 (especially for men). Almost never does intimate partner violence begin after age 45. Over their lifetime, about a third of all adults have been victimized in some way (raped, physically abused, threatened, stalked). Rates are similar for both women (36 percent) and men (34 percent), although more women sought medical, legal, or psychological help in recovery (25 percent of all women versus 11 percent of all men).

DIMENSIONS OF LOVE
- Robert Sternberg (1988) described three distinct aspects of love:
  - Passion: An intense physical, cognitive, and emotional onslaught characterized by excitement, ecstasy, and euphoria.
  - Intimacy: Knowing someone well; sharing secrets as well as sex.
  - Commitment: Grows gradually through decisions to be together, mutual caregiving, kept secrets, shared possessions, and forgiveness.

THE IDEAL AND THE REAL
- Western ideal of consummate love includes passion, intimacy, and commitment.
- Ideal is difficult to achieve.
- Passion may fade, intimacy may grow and stabilize, and commitment may deepen.
- 'Friends with benefits' implies an intimate, but not passionate, relationship.
- Hookups and hookup culture:
  - Involves a sexual encounter with neither intimacy nor commitment.
  - More common in younger college students than older; men than women; lonely than socially active.

FINDING EACH OTHER AND LIVING TOGETHER
- Social network use: About 83 percent of U.S. emerging adults use social networks to keep in contact and seek romance.
- Matchmaking Web sites use filtering strategies.
- Choice overload: Involves having so many possibilities that a thoughtful choice becomes difficult; may occur with matchmaking sites.

CHANGING HISTORICAL PATTERNS
- Emerging adult pattern in the United States:
  - Average age of first marriage is later.
  - Sex before marriage; 40 percent of babies born to unmarried women.
  - Good parenting more important than good marriage, but higher expectations for marriage than previous cohort.
  - Marriage equality for gay and lesbian couples may provide 'defense of marriage.'

COHABITATION
- Cohabitation varies from nation to nation.
- Involves living with an unrelated person—typically a romantic partner—to whom one is not married.
- Prevalence and purpose:
  - Most young adults in the United States, England, and northern Europe cohabit rather than marry before age 25.
  - Half of all cohabitating couples in the United States plan on marrying eventually.
  - Community culture and demographic differences matter.
  - Education increases the chance of marriage and marital childbearing.

LOVE AND MARRIAGE RELATIONSHIPS
- Changes in patterns of relationship between love and marriage:
  - Previously, about one-third of the world's families: Love did not lead to marriage because parents arranged marriages that joined two families together.
  - About one-third of families: Adolescents met only a select group; a young man asked a young woman's father for her hand in marriage; parents supervised interactions.
  - Final pattern: Love marriages occur only after young people are financially and emotionally independent.

LOVE YOU, LOVE YOU NOT
- Young-adult relationships are often problematic.
- Churning correlates with the stormiest relationships.
- In the Toledo, Ohio sample: 44 percent were churners; half of churners fought both physically and verbally.

notes.title: Vygotsky's Sociocultural Theory of Cognitive Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: SimplyPsychology.org — Saul McLeod, PhD (Updated Oct 16, 2025)
URL: https://www.simplypsychology.org/vygotsky.html

OVERVIEW
Vygotsky's sociocultural theory argues that cognitive development is fundamentally shaped by social interaction and cultural context. Learning is a shared activity — it happens first between people (interpsychologically) before it becomes internalized as individual thought (intrapsychologically).

CORE PRINCIPLES
1. Social interaction drives development — children learn through guided participation with more knowledgeable others
2. Culture shapes cognition — tools, language, and traditions provide the framework for how and what children learn
3. Language is the primary cognitive tool — not just for communication but for thinking itself
4. The ZPD identifies the optimal zone for instruction — just beyond current ability, reachable with support

MORE KNOWLEDGEABLE OTHER (MKO)
The MKO is anyone — teacher, peer, sibling, or even a technology tool (AI, educational software) — who has greater knowledge of a specific task. The role is fluid: as learners gain competence they can become the MKO for others. Even tools themselves can act as MKOs (e.g., fraction strips embody mathematical rules and guide learners' thinking by design).

ZONE OF PROXIMAL DEVELOPMENT (ZPD)
The ZPD = gap between what a learner can do independently (actual development) and what they can do with guidance (potential development). It is the optimal zone for instruction — challenging enough to promote growth but achievable with support. The ZPD shifts and expands as the learner develops new skills. It is NOT a fixed state.

KEY RESEARCH: Freund (1990) — children who practiced with their mothers (ZPD condition) significantly outperformed those who worked alone (discovery learning), supporting guided learning over independent exploration.

SCAFFOLDING
Scaffolding = specific strategies an MKO uses to support learners within the ZPD. Term introduced by Wood, Bruner & Ross (1976) — Vygotsky himself never used it.
Steps: (1) recruit interest, (2) simplify/break into steps, (3) model and prompt, (4) fade support, (5) transfer responsibility to learner.
EXAMPLE (Shaffer, 1996): Father guides daughter through jigsaw puzzle — points out edge-piece strategy, then progressively steps back as competence grows.
FADING = systematic reduction of support as learner gains competence.
TRANSFER OF RESPONSIBILITY = end goal; learner completes task fully independently.

CULTURE AND TOOLS OF INTELLECTUAL ADAPTATION
Each culture provides children with tools of intellectual adaptation — culturally specific thinking strategies passed down through generations:
- Language and writing systems
- Number and counting systems
- Mnemonic techniques (e.g., knot-tying in pre-literate societies vs. note-taking in Western cultures)
- Art, diagrams, and maps
Because tools are culturally embedded, cognitive functions are socio-culturally determined. Two children from different cultures may develop equally sophisticated thinking through different tools.

LANGUAGE AND COGNITIVE DEVELOPMENT
Vygotsky viewed language as the most powerful cultural tool — a vehicle for thought, not just communication.
THREE STAGES:
1. SOCIAL SPEECH (age 2+): external speech used to communicate with others
2. PRIVATE SPEECH (age 3+): audible self-directed speech; guides thinking and behavior; child says actions aloud
3. INNER SPEECH (age 7+): silent, internalized thought; the basis of reasoning and planning
Thought and language begin as separate systems and merge at ~age 3. After this, thought becomes verbal and speech becomes representational.
Private speech is the transition point — more similar in function to inner speech than to social speech.

ELEMENTARY vs. HIGHER MENTAL FUNCTIONS
ELEMENTARY: innate basic abilities present from birth (attention, sensation, perception, basic memory)
HIGHER: advanced cognitive processes developed through social interaction (reasoning, planning, self-regulation, metacognition, language) — NOT innate; acquired through culturally meaningful activities with others.

DYNAMIC ASSESSMENT (DA)
Traditional IQ tests measure only what a child can do independently (static). DA measures learning potential by including active support during testing — hints, feedback, coaching — to reveal what a child can achieve with guidance. DA is both diagnostic (reveals capabilities) and developmental (promotes learning during the assessment itself).

EDUCATIONAL IMPLICATIONS
- Target instruction within the ZPD — tasks slightly beyond current ability but achievable with support
- Collaborative learning (peer, teacher-guided) is essential, not supplementary
- Scaffold responsively and fade systematically
- Language-rich environments accelerate cognitive development
- Make-believe play is cognitively valuable, not just recreational
- Use dynamic assessment rather than static testing

notes.title: Ch. 16 — Adolescence: Psychosocial Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 16

ERIKSON STAGE 5: IDENTITY vs. ROLE CONFUSION
Central question: Who am I?
IDENTITY: consistent definition of self as unique individual — roles, attitudes, beliefs, aspirations
IDENTITY ACHIEVEMENT: understanding who one is in accord with past experiences and future plans
ROLE CONFUSION: adolescent does not know or care what their identity is
It is now considered UNUSUAL for teenagers to fully resolve this crisis during adolescence

MARCIA'S FOUR IDENTITY STATUSES
1. ROLE CONFUSION (diffusion): no exploration, no commitment
2. FORECLOSURE: commitment WITHOUT exploration — adopts parents'/society's values wholesale
3. MORATORIUM: exploration WITHOUT commitment — socially acceptable postponement (college)
4. IDENTITY ACHIEVEMENT: exploration AND commitment — the goal

ARENAS OF IDENTITY
Religious: most accept broad parental/cultural outlines; specific beliefs may be questioned
Political: more identify as independent; tend to be more liberal than parents
Ethnic: pride correlates with academic achievement; especially difficult for multiracial or immigrant adolescents
Gender: DSM-IV had 'gender identity disorder'; DSM-5 has GENDER DYSPHORIA (distress at biological gender — problem is the distress, not the identity)

FAMILY RELATIONSHIPS
Four dimensions of family closeness: communication, support, connectedness, control
FAMILISM: belief that family members should sacrifice personal freedom to care for one another — PROTECTIVE against depression and delinquency
Crucial to avoid extremes of strictness or leniency; maintain support while increasing autonomy

PEER RELATIONSHIPS
PEER PRESSURE: encouragement to conform
SELECTION: choose friends who share existing values
FACILITATION: peers make it easier to do BOTH right and wrong things
DEVIANCY TRAINING: showing others how to rebel against authority

ROMANCE SEQUENCE
1. Same-sex friend groups
2. Loose mixed crowd (public interactions)
3. Small mixed-sex groups of advanced members
4. Couples with private intimacies
Sexual orientation is FLUID during teen years; first romances rarely last more than a year

DEPRESSION AND SUICIDE
Self-esteem dips at puberty for both sexes
Familism and family support are PROTECTIVE
LGBT youth: higher risk of depression and anxiety
CLUSTER SUICIDES: several suicides in a group in a brief time period
PARASUICIDE: potentially lethal action that does not result in death — COMMON; completed suicide is NOT
Girls attempt more; boys COMPLETE more

DELINQUENCY
Most adolescents self-report breaking the law at least once before age 20
LIFE-COURSE-PERSISTENT OFFENDER: begins in early adolescence, continues throughout life
ADOLESCENCE-LIMITED OFFENDER: criminal activity stops by age 21
Pathway: Stubbornness → Defiance → Shoplifting/arson → Bullying → Assault/rape/murder

DRUG USE
Peaks ages 10–25; decreases after
Drug use before age 15 = best predictor of later use
ALCOHOL: most abused; damages hippocampus and prefrontal cortex (heavy use)
MARIJUANA: correlates with dropout, teen parenthood, unemployment
TOBACCO: slows growth, protein/vitamin deficiencies, damages heart/lungs/brain
GENERATIONAL FORGETTING: each new generation forgets the harm drugs do
Drug use: excites limbic system, interferes with prefrontal cortex → more emotional, less reflective

notes.title: Ch. 14 & 15 — Adolescence: Biosocial and Cognitive Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 14 and Ch. 15

CHAPTER 14: BIOSOCIAL DEVELOPMENT

PUBERTY
PUBERTY: 3–5 years from first hormone surge to full adult physical development
MENARCHE: girl's first menstrual period; pregnancy biologically possible but ovulation often irregular for years
SPERMARCHE: boy's first ejaculation of sperm

HPG AXIS: Hypothalamus → Pituitary → Adrenal glands + Gonads → Estradiol (females) and Testosterone (males)
Both sexes have some of each hormone
Visible signs of puberty appear ~1 year AFTER hormonal surge begins

TIMING
GIRLS develop ahead of boys generally
SECULAR TREND: earlier puberty each generation — stopped in developed nations; due to improved nutrition/medical care
STRESS causes earlier puberty in genetically sensitive children
EARLY-MATURING GIRLS: lower self-esteem, more depression, poorer body image, attracted to older boyfriends
EARLY-MATURING BOYS: more aggressive, law-breaking, alcohol-abusing

GROWTH SPURT SEQUENCE
Weight → Height → Muscles (then a year or two later)
Limbs grow before torso
Lungs triple in weight; heart doubles in size
PRIMARY sex characteristics: directly involved in reproduction (vagina, uterus, ovaries, testicles, penis)
SECONDARY sex characteristics: indicate maturity but not directly reproductive (shape, hair, breasts)

NUTRITION AND EATING DISORDERS
Deficiencies: iron, calcium, zinc — needed for bone and muscle growth
BODY IMAGE: few adolescents satisfied with their bodies
ANOREXIA NERVOSA: self-starvation; BMI 17 or lower; intense fear of weight gain; distorted body perception; can be fatal
BULIMIA NERVOSA: binge-purge at least weekly for 3 months; uncontrollable urges; self-worth tied to body shape
BINGE EATING DISORDER: new in DSM-5; bingeing without regular purging

BRAIN DEVELOPMENT (Lopsided Growth)
LIMBIC SYSTEM matures BEFORE PREFRONTAL CORTEX → emotional impulses precede rational control
NUCLEUS ACCUMBENS: heightened reward-seeking in adolescence
CIRCADIAN SHIFT: push toward eveningness — 3 in 4 high school seniors are sleep deprived
POSITIVE aspects: increased myelination, enhanced dopamine activity, synaptic growth enhances moral development
SEXUAL MATURATION: intercourse before age 14 linked to depression and drug use; teen birth rates declining; STI rates remain high

CHAPTER 15: COGNITIVE DEVELOPMENT

ADOLESCENT EGOCENTRISM (Ages 10–13)
IMAGINARY AUDIENCE: everyone is watching and judging me
PERSONAL FABLE: my thoughts/feelings are uniquely wonderful or awful
INVINSIBILITY FABLE: I cannot be harmed by things that hurt others — leads to risk-taking

FORMAL OPERATIONAL THOUGHT (Piaget Stage 4)
Systematic logic; abstract ideas; HYPOTHETICAL THOUGHT
DEDUCTIVE (top-down): general principle → specific conclusions — more cognitively advanced
INDUCTIVE (bottom-up): specific experiences → general conclusion — more characteristic of childhood
Adolescents rely MORE on intuitive than analytic thinking

DUAL PROCESSING
INTUITIVE THOUGHT: from emotion or hunch; influenced by culture and past experience
ANALYTIC THOUGHT: systematic, logical analysis of pros/cons
Brain basis: limbic matures before prefrontal cortex
With maturity, adolescents gradually balance both modes

TECHNOLOGY AND SCHOOLING
CYBERBULLYING: most prevalent ages 11–14; imaginary audience can go viral; leads to depression and suicide risk
SEXTING: photos forwarded without subject's knowledge
MIDDLE SCHOOL: often impersonal; behavioral problems increase; students dislike it
ENTITY approach: intelligence is fixed and inborn; INCREMENTAL approach: intelligence can grow through effort
School transitions increase stress — bullying, depression, eating disorders often spike
PISA correlates of high achievement: valuing education, individualized learning, high standards, valued teachers

notes.title: Ch. 13 — Middle Childhood: The Social World
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 13

ERIKSON STAGE 4: INDUSTRY vs. INFERIORITY
Tension between productivity and incompetence
Self-pride depends on how peers view accomplishments, not just actual achievements
SOCIAL COMPARISON: comparing attributes to others; helps abandon rosy preschool self-evaluation
Growth mindset and effortful control develop

SELF-CONCEPT AND RESILIENCE
Self-concept becomes more complex, logical, and less optimistic in middle childhood
RESILIENCE: capacity to adapt well to significant adversity
  Dynamic: resilient at some periods but not others
  Positive adaptation: responding to adversity in a growth-promoting way
  Adversity must be SIGNIFICANT
CUMULATIVE STRESS: minor stresses accumulated over time are MORE devastating than a single major stress
COGNITIVE COPING: a child's interpretation of family situation affects its impact (parentification)

FAMILY STRUCTURE AND FUNCTION
Structure: legal/genetic relationships (nuclear, single-parent, extended, stepfamily, polygamous)
Function: how a family meets members' needs — learning, self-respect, friendships, harmony, stability
FAMILY-STRESS MODEL: two main dysfunction factors in ALL structures — LOW INCOME and HIGH CONFLICT
Shared vs. nonshared environment: shared influence shrinks with age; nonshared (peers, schools) increases
Two-parent advantage: better education, social, cognitive, behavioral outcomes on average

PEER CULTURE
Culture of children: particular habits, styles, values distinct from adult society
Friendships: valued more than peer acceptance; provide psychosocial growth; buffer against psychopathology
Gender differences: girls talk and share secrets; boys play more active games
Popular children: kind, trustworthy, cooperative (early); athletic, dominant, sometimes aggressive (around 5th grade)
Unpopular: Neglected (ignored but not shunned), Aggressive-rejected (antagonistic), Withdrawn-rejected (timid, anxious)

BULLYING
Repeated, systematic harm of a weaker person
Bully-victim (provocative victim): both attacks and is attacked
Four types: Physical, Verbal, Relational (destroying friendships), Cyber
Relational bullying often more devastating than physical
Consequences: depression, impaired social understanding, lower achievement
Prevention: WHOLE SCHOOL must be involved; earlier intervention more effective

KOHLBERG'S MORAL REASONING
Preconventional: rewards and punishments (what benefits me)
Conventional: social rules and norms (what others expect)
Postconventional: abstract moral principles (universal justice)
Judged by REASONS, not answers
Criticism: ignores culture, gender, and family; doesn't address differences between child and adult morality
Current research: talking about moral issues advances moral development — not immediately but soon

notes.title: Ch. 11 & 12 — Middle Childhood: Biosocial and Cognitive Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 11 and Ch. 12

CHAPTER 11: BIOSOCIAL DEVELOPMENT (Ages 6–11)

OVERVIEW
Healthiest years of the lifespan — fatal diseases and accidents are rare
Permanent teeth come in; muscles grow stronger; children run faster, exercise longer
Oral health critical — gum disease can lead to cardiovascular disease
Good habits formed now protect adult health
EMBODIED COGNITION: movement improves intellectual functioning (better cerebral blood flow, more neurotransmitters)

OBESITY AND HEALTH
BMI: ratio of weight to height
Overweight: above 85th percentile; Obese: above 95th percentile
18% of 6-to-11-year-olds in US obese (2016)
Obesity now causes more deaths worldwide than malnutrition
Correlates with asthma, high blood pressure, elevated cholesterol, Type II diabetes

ASTHMA and HYGIENE HYPOTHESIS
Asthma: chronic inflammation narrowing airways; wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness
Hygiene hypothesis: early exposure to microbes builds natural resistance; first-born children and urban children have higher rates

BRAIN AND INTELLIGENCE
Selective attention and reaction time improve with age
IQ TEST: measures intellectual aptitude; ACHIEVEMENT TEST: measures mastery of subjects
FLYNN EFFECT: IQ scores have risen over decades — better prenatal care and nutrition are factors
STERNBERG: 3 intelligences — academic, creative, practical
GARDNER: 9 intelligences — linguistic, logical-mathematical, musical, spatial, bodily-kinesthetic, interpersonal, intrapersonal, naturalistic, spiritual/existential

DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOPATHOLOGY
Multifinality: one cause → many outcomes; Equifinality: one symptom → many causes; Comorbidity: two+ conditions simultaneously
ADHD: inattentive, impulsive, overactive; concern about misdiagnosis and overmedication in US
DYSLEXIA: reading difficulty — processing problem, not intelligence; DYSCALCULIA: math difficulty
ASD: social interaction ALWAYS impaired; neurodiversity perspective values variation
SPECIAL EDUCATION: LRE (least restrictive environment), RTI (response to intervention), IEP (individual education plan)

CHAPTER 12: COGNITIVE DEVELOPMENT

PIAGET: CONCRETE OPERATIONAL (Ages 6–11)
Logic applied to DIRECT, CONCRETE experiences — not yet abstract
New abilities: CLASSIFICATION (groups by shared characteristics), SERIATION (logical series), CONSERVATION (now mastered)
Vygotsky: children are apprentices in learning; language mediates; culture affects content and method

INFORMATION PROCESSING
Knowledge base: extensive prior knowledge makes new learning easier
Control processes: SELECTIVE ATTENTION, METACOGNITION (thinking about thinking), METAMEMORY (knowing how memory works)
AUTOMATIZATION: well-practiced processes require no conscious thought
Hubs develop where axons meet — link reading and social skills

LANGUAGE IN MIDDLE CHILDHOOD
METAPHORS and PUNS: new cognitive flexibility makes them funny
PRAGMATICS: use words appropriately in different contexts
CODE-SWITCHING: switching between formal code (academic), informal code (friends), and texting code
Bilingualism: correlates with school achievement; code-switching is a strength
ELL (English Language Learners): English proficiency below cutoff score

INTERNATIONAL TESTING
TIMSS: math and science, 4th and 8th graders
PIRLS: reading, 4th graders; girls ahead of boys in verbal skills in every nation
PISA: problem-solving and daily life cognition, 15-year-olds; US scores worse here than on PIRLS or TIMSS
Hidden curriculum: unofficial patterns in schools that influence what children learn
SES strongly correlates with academic achievement; brain scans confirm hippocampus development affected by SES

notes.title: Ch. 10 — Early Childhood: Psychosocial Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 10

EMOTIONAL REGULATION
Most important psychosocial accomplishment ages 2–6
Due to limbic system and prefrontal cortex connections and brain maturation
EFFORTFUL CONTROL: regulating emotions through effort, not natural inclination
Maturation + learning + culture all contribute

ERIKSON STAGE 3: INITIATIVE vs. GUILT
Children undertake new skills; feel guilty when they fail
GUILT: self-blame for doing something wrong
SHAME: feeling others disapprove
SELF-CONCEPT: understanding of who one is (self-esteem, appearance, personality, traits)
PROTECTIVE OPTIMISM: children believe they are strong, smart, able — this confidence helps them persist

MOTIVATION
INTRINSIC: from inside — need to feel smart or competent; imaginary friends are an example
EXTRINSIC: from outside — rewards, material possessions, others' esteem
Intrinsic motivation more important for continued development

PLAY (Parten 1932)
1. Solitary — alone
2. Onlooker — watches
3. Parallel — similar toys, not together
4. Associative — interact/share but not mutual
5. Cooperative — together, joint activity or taking turns
ROUGH-AND-TUMBLE: mimics aggression, no intent to harm; common in young males; may help prefrontal cortex
SOCIODRAMATIC: explores/rehearses social rules; emotional regulation practice; self-concept in safe context

BAUMRIND'S PARENTING STYLES
Authoritarian: strict, little communication → obedient but unhappy, may rebel
Permissive: high nurturance, little discipline → unhappy, immature, dependent
Authoritative: limits + flexible + listens → successful, happy, well-liked
Neglectful: indifferent, unaware → worst outcomes

DISCIPLINE
Physical punishment: increases obedience temporarily; also increases later bullying, delinquency, aggression
PSYCHOLOGICAL CONTROL: threatening to withdraw love; linked to lower math scores and depression
TIME-OUT: separation from others; effectiveness varies by culture and child
INDUCTION: talking extensively; helping children understand why behavior was wrong; takes time but builds internalization

SEX AND GENDER
Sex differences = biological; Gender differences = culturally prescribed
By age 2: apply gender labels; age 4: toys are gender-appropriate
Five theories: Psychoanalytic (phallic stage, identification), Behaviorism (reinforcement), Cognitive (gender schema), Sociocultural (cultural customs; gender detectives by age 6), Evolutionary (reproductive fitness)

AGGRESSION (four types)
1. Instrumental: get an object or territory
2. Reactive: angry retaliation to frustration or harm
3. Relational: damage peer relationships through exclusion, rumors
4. Bullying: repeated, systematic harm of weaker person
Prosocial and antisocial behavior are both innate; antisocial diminishes over preschool years

notes.title: Ch. 9 — Early Childhood: Cognitive Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 9

EXECUTIVE FUNCTION
Cognitive ability to organize and prioritize thoughts; allows anticipating, strategizing, planning
3 essential abilities: MEMORY (short-term/working), INHIBITION (control responses), FLEXIBILITY/SHIFTING (take perspectives, share, take turns)
Begins by age 2; develops throughout lifespan
NOT inborn — developed through family interactions and experience
Foundational for school readiness

PIAGET: PREOPERATIONAL THOUGHT (Ages 2–6)
Major accomplishment: SYMBOLIC THOUGHT — words can refer to things not seen
6 obstacles to logic:
  1. ANIMISM: natural objects are alive
  2. CENTRATION: focus on one idea (includes egocentrism)
  3. EGOCENTRISM: see world only from own perspective (not selfishness!)
  4. FOCUS ON APPEARANCE: ignore non-apparent attributes
  5. STATIC REASONING: nothing changes
  6. IRREVERSIBILITY: nothing can be undone
CONSERVATION FAILURES: all 6 obstacles combine — tall/narrow glass seems like more liquid
Limitation: Piaget's tasks required words; modification of tasks showed BETTER performance → he underestimated preschoolers

VYGOTSKY (review from Ch. 2)
Scaffolding, ZPD, guided participation, apprentice in thinking
OVERIMITATION: copying actions not relevant to the behavior being learned — universal in ages 2–6
PRIVATE SPEECH: words mediate between brain potential and comprehension; language advances thinking
STEM education begins in early childhood

THEORY-THEORY and THEORY OF MIND
Theory-theory: children explain everything they see and hear; develop theories before imitating
Theory of mind: understanding what others might be thinking; begins ~age 4; linked to lying to escape punishment
Correlates with prefrontal cortex maturity and executive processing

LANGUAGE (~500 words at age 2 → 10,000+ at age 6)
Vocabulary explosion; comprehension always greater than production
FAST-MAPPING: speedy but imprecise word learning; tentative mental categories
LOGICAL EXTENSION: use words to describe other objects in same category
OVERREGULARIZATION: applying grammar rules to exceptions (foots, tooths) — sign of INCREASING knowledge
Bilingualism: beneficial; both languages in same brain areas for young children

EARLY CHILDHOOD EDUCATION
Child-centered: Montessori (individual pride, literacy tasks), Reggio Emilia (creativity/Italy), Waldorf (creativity, emotional growth)
Teacher-directed: academic subjects, structured, clearer work/play distinction, higher child-adult ratio, less expensive
Head Start: 1965, federal, benefits strongest for children in poverty — vocabulary gains persisted
Long-term intensive programs (Perry/High Scope, Abecedarian) show lasting gains

notes.title: Ch. 8 — Early Childhood: Biosocial Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 8

BODY CHANGES (Ages 2–6)
Children become slimmer as lower body lengthens
Add ~3 inches in height and ~4.5 lbs per year
BMI lowest at ages 5 and 6 — normal for appetite to decrease
Nutrition deficiencies: most children in developed nations get enough calories but lack iron, zinc, and calcium
Sugar consumption in US = 3 times the recommended amount
3–8% of children allergic to a specific food

BRAIN DEVELOPMENT
Brain reaches 90% of adult weight by age 6
MYELINATION: fatty coating on axons that speeds nerve signal transmission — primary reason for faster thinking
CORPUS CALLOSUM: connects left and right hemispheres; as myelination improves, signals between hemispheres become quicker and clearer
LATERALIZATION: each side of brain dominant for certain functions — but distinction is exaggerated; both sides involved in almost every skill
PREFRONTAL CORTEX (ages 2–6): facilitates focused attention, reduces impulsiveness and perseveration
PERSEVERATION: sticking to one thought or action — opposite of impulsiveness; both caused by prefrontal immaturity
IMPULSIVENESS: jumping from task to task without staying quiet
Both too much AND too little stress impair learning

MOTOR SKILLS
GROSS motor: brain maturation, motivation, and guided practice make skills possible; culture influences development
FINE motor: more difficult; mature ~6 months earlier in females; influenced by practice and maturation
Artistic expression: blossoms during early childhood; correlated with later creative drawing

INJURY CONTROL
In every nation, more young children die from accidents than any other cause
Three levels of prevention:
  Primary = change background conditions (safety surfaces, car seats, helmets, child-proof containers)
  Secondary = avert harm in high-risk situation (traffic lights, stopping before impact)
  Tertiary = reduce harm after event (golden hour medical treatment)

CHILD MALTREATMENT
Abuse: deliberate harmful action to physical, emotional, or sexual well-being
Neglect: failure to meet basic physical, educational, or emotional needs
Reported vs. Substantiated (verified): ~6:1 ratio of reports to substantiated cases
Signs: injuries inconsistent with story, repeated broken bones, violence/sex themes in play, slow growth, no friendships, hypervigilance
PTSD: anxiety disorder as delayed reaction to shocking/frightening event
Consequences: long-lasting; children regard others as hostile and exploitative
Three prevention levels: Primary (macrosystem), Secondary (identify insecure attachment), Tertiary (limit harm after)
Permanency planning: foster care, kinship care (grandparent), adoption

notes.title: Ch. 7 — First Two Years: Psychosocial Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 7

EMOTIONAL DEVELOPMENT TIMELINE
6 WEEKS: Social smile (evoked by human faces)
3-4 MONTHS: Laughter (often associated with curiosity)
6 MONTHS: Anger (healthy response to frustration); sadness appears with cortisol increase
9 MONTHS: STRANGER WARINESS (cries or frightened at unfamiliar faces)
           SEPARATION ANXIETY (distress when caregiver leaves; if strong after age 3, may be disorder)
           Fear emerges in response to people, things, situations
TODDLERHOOD: new emotions — pride, shame, embarrassment, disgust, guilt (require self-awareness)
SELF-AWARENESS: emerges 15-24 months (sense of me)
All emotional responses begin in the BRAIN — limbic system crucial

TEMPERAMENT
Inborn differences in emotions, activity, self-regulation
EPIGENETIC: genes and child-rearing practices
4 CATEGORIES: Easy (40%), Difficult (10%), Slow to warm up (15%), Hard to classify (35%)
3 DIMENSIONS: effortful control, negative mood, exuberant

SYNCHRONY AND ATTACHMENT
SYNCHRONY: coordinated, rapid, smooth caregiver-infant exchange
Begins with parents IMITATING infants' tone, rhythm, facial expressions
Helps infants learn to read emotions and develop social skills
STILL-FACE TECHNIQUE: adult keeps face unmoving → infant shows significant stress
Synchrony is experience-expectant; lack of it is a troubling sign

ATTACHMENT: lasting emotional bond forming in early infancy
Proximity-seeking and contact-maintaining
Stages: Preattachment (0-6 wk) → In the making (6 wk-8 mo) → Classic secure (8 mo-2 yr) → beyond

4 ATTACHMENT TYPES (Strange Situation):
Type B SECURE: explores freely; distressed when gone; joyful at return — most healthy
Type A INSECURE-AVOIDANT: indifferent to presence, departure, or return
Type C INSECURE-RESISTANT: clingy; very distressed; hard to comfort
Type D DISORGANIZED: confused, contradictory responses
Poverty and harsh contexts reduce likelihood of secure attachment
Insecure attachment CORRELATES with later problems — correlation is NOT causation

ROMANIAN ORPHAN STUDIES:
Adopted before 6 months: most developed normally
Adopted after 1 year: long-term cognitive and emotional deficits
Main problem = early ENVIRONMENT, not genetics

SOCIAL REFERENCING
Using others' emotional responses to guide own behavior
Fathers: more intensive play; elicit more smiles and laughter; teach emotional expressions
Mothers: more caregiving and comforting

THEORIES OF INFANT PSYCHOSOCIAL DEVELOPMENT
Freud: Oral stage (year 1) → Anal stage (year 2)
Erikson:
TRUST vs. MISTRUST (year 1): basic trust if needs met; failure → suspicious adult
AUTONOMY vs. SHAME AND DOUBT (year 2): self-rule over actions and body; failure → easily shamed adult
Behaviorism (Watson): emotions and personality molded by parental reinforcement or punishment
Proximal parenting (frequent holding): less self-aware but more compliant toddlers
Distal parenting (distance and face-to-face): more self-aware but less obedient children
High-quality day care requires: adequate attention, language and sensorimotor encouragement, safety, warm caregivers

notes.title: Ch. 6 — First Two Years: Cognitive Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 6

INFANT COGNITION
Infant brains are PRESET to understand the world using all steps of scientific method
Curiosity → hypothesizing → observing → experimenting → analyzing → concluding
GAZE-FOLLOWING: babies follow caregivers' attention as learning strategy
CORE KNOWLEDGE: innate understanding of gravity, solid walls, and significance of adult gaze
OWN-RACE EFFECT: infants recognize faces from own ethnic group better (fusiform face area)
By 1 year: ability to distinguish sounds in never-heard languages deteriorates

PIAGET'S SENSORIMOTOR PERIOD (6 stages)
Stage 1 (0-1 mo): REFLEXES only
Stage 2 (1-4 mo): First HABITS — PRIMARY circular reactions (own body); behavior becomes purposeful
Stage 3 (4-8 mo): Make things LAST — SECONDARY circular reactions (objects and people); mirror neurons
Stage 4 (6-12 mo): MEANS-TO-AN-END — anticipation; begins searching for hidden objects
Stage 5 (12-18 mo): Active EXPLORATION — TERTIARY circular reactions; little scientist
Stage 6 (18-24 mo): MENTAL COMBINATIONS — imagination; thinks before acting

Key concepts:
Assimilation: new fits old ideas
Accommodation: old ideas restructured for new
OBJECT PERMANENCE:
Below 8 months: does not search
Around 8 months: searches immediately after hiding
2 years: full understanding

MEMORY
INFANTILE AMNESIA: memory is fragile but CAN be activated
Conditions for infant memory: real-life conditions + high motivation + retrieval cues
Repeated reminders more powerful than single reminders
Context crucial for infants below 9 months
Rovee-Collier mobile experiment: 3-month-olds remembered after 2 weeks with reminder session

LANGUAGE DEVELOPMENT
Before birth: language learning via brain organization
Newborn: prefers speech sounds and mother's language
About 6 months: BABBLING begins (ba-ba-ba); experience-expectant
About 10 months: POINTING emerges
About 12 months: HOLOPHRASES; understand 10 times more than produce; babbling sounds like native language
About 18 months: 50-word threshold → NAMING EXPLOSION (50-100 words per month)
21 months: 2 times as many words as at 18 months
Grammar: apparent in holophrases 18-24 months; MLU measures sentence complexity

THEORIES OF LANGUAGE:
Skinner: taught through reinforcement
Social: evolved social beings; emotional content primary
Chomsky: LAD innate — language acquisition device
HYBRID: most likely — different theories explain different aspects at different ages

notes.title: Ch. 5 — First Two Years: Biosocial Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 5

BODY CHANGES
Birthweight doubles by month 4; triples by year 1
Height increases about 10 inches in first year
At age 2: about half adult height; about 3/4 adult brain size

BRAIN DEVELOPMENT
HEAD-SPARING: brain is LAST body part damaged by malnutrition

NEURON STRUCTURE:
Cell body + AXON (sends) + DENDRITES (receive) + SYNAPSE (gap) + NEUROTRANSMITTER (chemical carrier)
MYELINIZATION: axon coating acts as insulation → faster, more efficient nerve transmission

BRAIN REGIONS (maturation order):
1. BRAIN STEM: automatic responses — present at birth
2. MIDBRAIN: emotions and memory
3. CORTEX: thinking, feeling, sensing — develops through adolescence
4. PREFRONTAL CORTEX: anticipation, planning, impulse control — LAST to mature
Limbic system: emotions — amygdala, hypothalamus, hippocampus

TRANSIENT EXUBERANCE AND PRUNING:
Rapid early dendrite growth (exuberant and transient)
Unused dendrites pruned → more synapses → more complex thinking
Use it or lose it principle

EXPERIENCE TYPES:
Experience-EXPECTANT: must occur for normal brain development
Experience-DEPENDENT: individual; enriches development; not required
Romanian orphan research: lack of experience-expectant interactions → long-term deficits

HARMFUL TO BRAIN:
Overabundance of cortisol (stress hormones)
SHAKEN BABY SYNDROME: ruptures blood vessels, breaks neural connections
Self-righting: inborn drive to fix developmental deficits

SENSES
Sensation → Perception (brain interprets sensation)
HEARING: most developed at birth; speech perception by 4 months
VISION: least developed; newborns focus 4-30 inches; binocular vision at 3 months
Smell and Taste: function at birth; culturally influenced
Touch: acute in infants
REM SLEEP: newborns mostly REM; shifts to slow-wave by 3-4 months → sleep through night

MOTOR SKILLS
Cephalocaudal and proximodistal development direction
GROSS MOTOR: large movements (walking about 9-15+ months); influenced by brain maturation and culture
FINE MOTOR: small movements; PINCER GRASP = key milestone (thumb and forefinger)
First movements = reflexes → skills through practice and encouragement
Cultural variations in timing are NORMAL

notes.title: Ch. 4 — Prenatal Development and Birth
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 4

3 PERIODS — SURVIVAL STATISTICS

GERMINAL (weeks 1-2):
Zygote duplicates and divides; implantation ~10 days after conception
Cells differentiate into future embryo vs. placenta
About 60% of zygotes do NOT survive

EMBRYONIC (weeks 3-8):
Neural tube forms brain and spine; head forms; heart pulsates; extremities develop
By WEEK 8: ALL organs and systems formed in rudimentary form
About 20% of embryos spontaneously abort, usually chromosomal abnormality

FETAL (week 9 to birth):
Week 9: SRY gene triggers sex organ development
Month 3 average: 3 ounces, 3 inches
Middle months: digestive and excretory systems; hair, nails, teeth buds
Brain: rapid growth; neurogenesis and synaptogenesis; brain stem → midbrain → cortex
Final months: lung maturation, extensive cortex folding
About 5% of fetuses lost before viability or stillborn
Only about 31% of all zygotes become newborns

GROWTH PATTERNS (continue after birth)
Cephalocaudal: head-to-toe direction
Proximodistal: inner-to-outer direction
Infant can grasp purposefully BEFORE they can walk

TERATOGENS and RISK ANALYSIS
Teratogen = any agent causing birth defects (viruses, drugs, etc.)
Behavioral teratogens = harm prenatal brain, impair future intellectual and emotional functioning
Risk factors: (1) critical timing, (2) dose and frequency — THRESHOLD EFFECT, (3) genetic vulnerability
Male fetuses MORE vulnerable to teratogens than female
FAS (Fetal Alcohol Syndrome): threshold effect example; worse with poor nutrition and smoking

BIRTH AND NEWBORN
APGAR SCALE (Virginia Apgar, 1952):
At 1 min and 5 min after birth
5 categories: heart rate, breathing, muscle tone, color, reflexes
Score 0-2 each; max = 10
7 or above = healthy transition; below 7 = may need assistance

NEWBORN REFLEXES:
Survival: breathing, hiccupping, sneezing (oxygen); crying, shivering (temp); sucking, rooting, swallowing (feeding)
Signaling: Babinski, stepping, palmar grasping, Moro startle

Postpartum depression: 8-15% of new mothers; ranges from baby blues to psychosis
Low birthweight: LBW below 2,500g; VLBW below 1,500g; ELBW below 1,000g
U.S. LBW rate about 8.2% — worse than most developed nations

notes.title: Ch. 2 — Theories of Human Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 2

GRAND THEORIES

1. PSYCHOANALYTIC
FREUD: 5 psychosexual stages; id, ego, superego; unconscious drives; early conflict shapes personality
ERIKSON: 8 psychosocial stages; family and culture focus; 3 adult stages beyond adolescence
Difference: Freud = sexual urges; Erikson = social and cultural crises

2. BEHAVIORISM (observable behavior only)
Classical Conditioning (PAVLOV): neutral stimulus + meaningful stimulus → same response
Operant Conditioning (SKINNER): reinforcement increases behavior; punishment decreases it
Social Learning (BANDURA): observational learning and modeling, even without reinforcement

3. COGNITIVE (PIAGET)
4 stages: Sensorimotor → Preoperational → Concrete Operational → Formal Operational
Assimilation: new experience fits OLD ideas
Accommodation: OLD ideas restructured for new experience
Disequilibrium → Adaptation → New Equilibrium (this is how cognitive growth happens)
Information Processing: mind = computer; how attention and thought affect mental function

NEWER THEORIES

4. SOCIOCULTURAL (VYGOTSKY)
Culture is INTEGRAL to development — not external to it
ZPD: what learner can do WITH help; scaffolding builds on prior knowledge
Apprenticeship in thinking; guided participation
Focus: more NURTURE

5. EVOLUTIONARY
Two biologically-based drives: SURVIVAL and REPRODUCTION
Selective adaptation: beneficial genetic variations become more prevalent
Focus: more NATURE

ECLECTIC PERSPECTIVE
Most developmentalists selectively apply aspects of EACH theory
No single theory explains everything
Nature vs. Nurture emphasis varies: More nature = psychoanalytic, cognitive, evolutionary; More nurture = behaviorism, sociocultural

notes.title: Ch. 1 — The Science of Human Development
notes.body:
SOURCE: Berger, K.S. The Developing Person Through the Life Span, 11th ed. — Ch. 1

SCIENTIFIC METHOD
Empirical = based on observations, repeated experiences, verifiable experiments — NOT theoretical
5 Steps: Curiosity → Hypothesis → Test → Draw Conclusions → Report Results
Star 6th step: REPLICATION — repetition with different participants; required for wide acceptance

HUMAN DEVELOPMENT: seeks to understand how and why people change or remain the same over time
Key word: CHANGE — development is dynamic throughout the entire lifespan

NATURE vs. NURTURE
NATURE = genetic influences
NURTURE = ALL non-genetic influences (mother's nutrition through national culture)
Nature always affects nurture; nurture always affects nature — INSEPARABLE
DIFFERENTIAL SUSCEPTIBILITY: sensitivity to experiences differs person to person based on genes and past history

LIFE-SPAN PERSPECTIVE — 5 Characteristics
1. MULTIDIRECTIONAL: changes in every direction; discontinuous (stages) vs. continuous patterns
CRITICAL PERIOD: must happen now or never
SENSITIVE PERIOD: most likely now; possible later but harder

2. MULTICONTEXTUAL: Bronfenbrenner's 5 systems
Microsystem → Mesosystem → Exosystem → Macrosystem → Chronosystem

3. MULTICULTURAL:
Culture = shared beliefs, norms, behaviors, expectations
Social construction = idea based on shared perceptions, not objective reality
Difference-equals-deficit error = mistaking difference for inferiority
Cohort = people born in same historical period

4. MULTIDISCIPLINARY: all characteristics are EPIGENETIC
Epigenetic = environmental forces on genetic expression
Every trait (psychological and physical) influenced by genes AND environment

5. PLASTIC: traits can be molded; people maintain durability of identity
Dynamic-systems: ongoing, ever-changing interaction between person and environment
SES = income, wealth, occupation, education, place of residence
